findings.title: Exports-map exact-key lookup is a linear scan of Vec<MapEntry>
findings.problem: `Entry::value_for_key` iterates `EntryDataMap.list` (a plain `Vec<MapEntry>`) and compares each key with `strings::eql`. It is called from `resolve_imports_exports` (line 1777) for the exact-subpath lookup and from `resolve_exports` (line 1722) for the "." lookup — i.e. once per `import 'pkg/sub'` that hits a package with an "exports" map. For packages with large exports maps (rxjs ~150 keys, @mui/material ~400, lodash-es-style layouts), this is O(n) string compares per import instead of O(1).
findings.proposed_fix: Store a `StringArrayHashMap<u32>` (key → index into `list`) alongside `list` in `EntryDataMap`, built once in `visit_object` (package_json.rs:1125) where the map is parsed. `value_for_key` then becomes a single hash lookup. `expansion_keys` already gets this parse-time preprocessing; exact keys should too.

findings.title: ESModule::finalize scans path 4x and re-allocates even when no '%' is present
findings.problem: `finalize()` runs on every successful exports/imports-map resolution. It first does four separate `strings::contains` passes over `result.path` (one per `INVALID_PERCENT_CHARS` entry), then unconditionally calls `PercentEncoding::decode_into` (byte-by-byte copy into a PathBuffer), then allocates a fresh `Box::<[u8]>::from(resolved_path)` at line 1697 — dropping the `Box<[u8]>` that `resolve_target` just allocated (e.g. line 2142/2159). For the overwhelmingly common case where the resolved path contains no `%`, all of this is wasted: 4 scans + 1 copy + 1 extra heap allocation per import.
findings.proposed_fix: At the top of `finalize` (after the status check), do a single `strings::index_of_char(&result.path, b'%')`. If `None`, return `result` as-is — no decode, no realloc, and the 4 substring scans become unnecessary (all four patterns start with `%`). Only when a `%` is present fall through to the existing check/decode/realloc path.

findings.title: O(V²) publish-time lookup in npm manifest parser
findings.problem: Inside the per-version loop (`for prop in versions` at line 2342), the code re-fetches `json.get(b"time")` every iteration (linear scan of root props) and then does `time_obj.get().get(version_name)` — a linear scan over the `time` object, which has one entry per version. With V versions that is V linear scans of ~V keys each, i.e. O(V²) byte-slice comparisons. Packages like typescript (~3700 versions) or @types/node (~2000 versions) incur millions of string comparisons per manifest parse, and this runs for every unique package on a cold install.
findings.proposed_fix: Before the versions loop, look up the `time` object once and build a `HashMap<u64, &[u8]>` (string-hash → date str) from its properties in a single pass. In the loop, hash `version_name` and do an O(1) lookup. This reduces the whole thing to O(V).

findings.title: Unused 32KB zeroed heap allocation per HTTP/1.1 request in RequestBodyBuffer
findings.problem: Every call to send_initial_request_payload (once per HTTP/1.1 request, and again on each write-backpressure retry) invokes get_request_body_send_buffer() which in the common (<32KB) path does Box::new([0u8; 32768]) — a 32KB heap allocation plus a 32KB memset — and for the large path takes a 512KB HeapRequestBodyBuffer. The only thing ever done with that buffer is to_array_list(), which reads its .len() to size a fresh Vec::with_capacity(...) and otherwise ignores it. The boxed buffer is never written or read from and is dropped at scope exit. Net effect: an extra 32KB (or 512KB) malloc+memset+free per request on top of the Vec that is actually used.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the RequestBodyBuffer indirection for this caller: compute the target capacity (min(32KB, estimate) or 512KB) and call Vec::with_capacity directly in send_initial_request_payload, mirroring what the ProxyHeaders arm already does at lib.rs:3475. Alternatively make the Stack variant a zero-sized capacity marker instead of a Box<[u8; 32K]>.

findings.title: H3 request URL built with unsized Vec + redundant copy (per-request)
findings.problem: In prepare_js_request_context_for (the H3 path, IS_H3 branch) the request URL is built by `let mut s = Vec::new(); write!(&mut s, "https://{}", fmt); ... s.extend_from_slice(path); request_object.url.set(BunString::clone_utf8(&s));`. The Vec starts with zero capacity, so `write!` triggers several grow-reallocations for the `https://` + host bytes, then `extend_from_slice(path)` can realloc again, and finally `clone_utf8` copies the whole buffer a second time. This runs for every HTTP/3 request.
findings.proposed_fix: Pre-size the buffer: `Vec::with_capacity("https://".len() + host.len() + path.len())` (HostFormatter writes at most host + optional brackets, so an upper bound is cheap). This collapses the Vec growth into a single allocation. Further, `BunString` could be built once from the owned Vec (e.g. a create-from-owned-bytes helper) instead of `clone_utf8(&s)`, avoiding the second copy.

findings.title: TokenList::parse reallocates via drain().collect() for every value with >=2 tokens
findings.problem: After `parse_into` fills `tokens`, the whitespace-trim step unconditionally does `tokens.drain(start..end).collect()` whenever `tokens.len() >= 2`. This always allocates a brand-new Vec and moves every element into it, even in the common case where `start==0 && end==tokens.len()` (no trimming needed). `TokenList::parse` runs for every `--custom: ...;` declaration (CustomProperty::parse) and every known property that contains `var()` (UnparsedProperty::parse), so this is a per-declaration heap allocation + memcpy.
findings.proposed_fix: Skip the rebuild when `start == 0 && end == tokens.len()` and return `TokenList { v: tokens }` directly. When trimming is needed, do it in place: `tokens.truncate(end); if start > 0 { tokens.remove(0); }` (a single O(n) shift, no second allocation).

findings.title: bun_join::<true> does 3 allocator round-trips per joined path (to_vec + push-grow + shrink)
findings.problem: In SENTINEL mode, `s.as_bytes().to_vec()` allocates exactly `len` bytes, then `v.push(0)` must grow (realloc), then `v.into_boxed_slice()` must shrink (realloc) because the growth left cap > len. That is three allocator calls where one suffices. `join()` (which routes here when `absolute: true`) is called per subdirectory pushed onto the work stack and per matched path in `prepare_matched_path`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with `let mut v = Vec::with_capacity(s.as_bytes().len() + 1); v.extend_from_slice(s.as_bytes()); v.push(0); v.into_boxed_slice()` so capacity == len and neither `push` nor `into_boxed_slice` reallocates.

findings.title: parse_version_number re-filters and copies bytes that are already guaranteed all-digit
findings.problem: `parse_version_number` loops over its input with a per-byte match that handles x/*/X, space, dot, and other chars, copying digits into a 20-byte stack buffer before calling `T::parse_ascii` on the copy. But all three call sites (lines 542/547/552) pass `&input[part_start_i..last_char_i]`, a slice the caller already proved is 100% ASCII digits via the `while input[i].is_ascii_digit()` loop at line 533. The match-filter and buffer copy are therefore dead work done 3× per `Version::parse`, which runs for every version key in every npm manifest (npm.rs:2082/2345 — thousands per popular package) and every dependency spec.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the body with a direct `T::parse_ascii(input)` (keeping the debug-build error log on None). The overflow case (>20 digits) is already handled by `parse_unsigned` returning Err. This removes one O(n) scan + one small memcpy per major/minor/patch component.

findings.title: Quadratic per-item insert() loop when splicing instance members into constructor
findings.problem: In `lower_class` (TS legacy-decorator / parameter-property lowering), instance-member initializers are spliced into the constructor body by calling `constructor_stmts.insert(i + off, *m)` once per member. `BumpVec::insert` (BabyVec) memmoves the entire tail (`ptr::copy(p, p.add(1), len - index)`), so with M instance members and N constructor statements after super() this does O(M·(N+M)) work instead of O(N+M). The comment admits it is 'emulating insert_slice', but the emulation is quadratic.
findings.proposed_fix: Build the result in one pass the way `lower_decorators.rs:2331-2337` already does: don't pre-`extend_from_slice(old_stmts)`; instead scan `old_stmts` for super(), then `extend_from_slice(&old_stmts[..i])`, `extend_from_slice(&instance_members)`, `extend_from_slice(&old_stmts[i..])`. This is linear and avoids the repeated memmove.

findings.title: Double hash lookup on import_items_for_namespace per property access during bundling
findings.problem: `maybe_rewrite_property_access` runs for every `a.b` / `a[b]` expression. When `p.options.bundle` is set and the target is an identifier, it calls `import_items_for_namespace.contains_key(&id.ref_)` and, on hit, immediately `import_items_for_namespace.get(&id.ref_).unwrap()` — hashing and probing the same key twice. For files that heavily use `ns.foo` accesses on a namespace import, every access pays 2 map lookups where 1 suffices.
findings.evidence:
if p.import_items_for_namespace.contains_key(&id.ref_) {
    let existing = p
        .import_items_for_namespace
        .get(&id.ref_)
        .unwrap()
        .get(name)
        .copied();
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `contains_key` + `get().unwrap()` with a single `if let Some(items) = p.import_items_for_namespace.get(&id.ref_) { let existing = items.get(name).copied(); ... }`. The later `get_mut` (for the insert path) already re-borrows after `new_symbol`, so this change is purely removing the redundant first probe.

findings.title: ExportRenamer::next_renamed_name is O(n²) on name collisions and arena-allocates every failed attempt
findings.problem: When a cross-chunk export alias collides, the loop always restarts `tries` at 1 instead of reading the value previously stored at `*entry.value_ptr` (line 1086), so the k-th collision on the same name re-walks 1..k — O(n²) total for n collisions. Contrast with `NumberScope::find_unused_name` (line 936) which explicitly resumes from the stored count to avoid exactly this. Additionally, inside the loop `self.arena.alloc_slice_copy(attempt)` is called before checking `found_existing`, so every failed probe leaks an arena chunk — O(n²) wasted arena bytes per same-named export set.
findings.proposed_fix: Initialize `tries` from `*entry.value_ptr` when `found_existing` so each collision resumes where the last one left off (O(n) total). Defer the arena copy: probe `self.used.contains_key(attempt)` first and only `alloc_slice_copy` + insert once an unused name is found.

findings.title: Linear scan over commonjs_named_exports for every ECommonjsExportIdentifier expression
findings.problem: Each `ECommonjsExportIdentifier` (produced for every `exports.foo` / `module.exports.foo` reference in a CJS file) linearly scans all `commonjs_named_exports.values()` comparing `.loc_ref.ref_` to `id.ref_`. For a file with N named exports each referenced once, this is N scans × N entries = O(N²) Ref comparisons. `CommonJSNamedExports` is keyed by the string name, so there is no reverse Ref→index lookup available.
findings.proposed_fix: Build a `HashMap<Ref, u32>` reverse index once per file (either when constructing `Options` or lazily on first `ECommonjsExportIdentifier`) and do an O(1) lookup here. The map is tiny (one entry per named export) and avoids the quadratic scan.

findings.title: SExportClause always heap-allocates a Vec<&ClauseItem> even when no filtering is needed
findings.problem: `let mut array: Vec<&js_ast::ClauseItem> = slice_of(s.items).iter().collect();` allocates a fresh heap Vec for every `export { ... }` statement so that the rare `namespace_alias.was_originally_property_access` items can be swap-removed. In the overwhelmingly common case (no aliased property-access exports) nothing is removed and the Vec is just a throwaway copy of the item pointers. This is a per-export-statement heap allocation on the main print path.
findings.proposed_fix: First do a cheap pass over `slice_of(s.items)` to see if any item has `namespace_alias.was_originally_property_access`; if none, iterate the original slice directly without allocating. Alternatively, hoist a reusable scratch `Vec<&ClauseItem>` onto `Printer` (like `temporary_bindings` / `binary_expression_stack`) and `.clear()` it between uses.

findings.title: match_tsconfig_paths exact-match does O(n) key scan over an ArrayHashMap
findings.problem: `tsconfig.paths` is an `ArrayHashMap<Box<[u8]>, Vec<Box<[u8]>>>` (tsconfig_json.rs:121, populated via `.put()` so keys are unique), but the exact-match pass iterates all `keys()` and compares each with `strings::eql_long` instead of doing a hash lookup. `match_tsconfig_paths` runs for every bare-specifier import when a tsconfig with `paths` is in scope (resolver.rs:2647, 1888, 1045). In monorepos with many path aliases (dozens–hundreds of entries) this is an O(n·keylen) scan per import where O(1) is available.
findings.proposed_fix: Either change `PathsMap` to `StringArrayHashMap<Vec<Box<[u8]>>>` so `tsconfig.paths.get(path)` takes `&[u8]` directly, or add/use a `get_adapted` byte-slice adapter on the existing `ArrayHashMap`. Replace the loop with a single lookup followed by the inner `for original_path in value.iter()` block.

findings.title: match_tsconfig_paths recomputes '*' position for every key on every import
findings.problem: The wildcard pass iterates every `tsconfig.paths` key and calls `strings::index_of_char(key, b'*')` to find the star on each one, on every call (i.e. per bare-specifier import). The tsconfig is parsed once and cached for the process; the star position and the resulting (prefix, suffix) split are invariant and could be stored at parse time. The exports-map code already does exactly this preprocessing (`expansion_keys`, package_json.rs:1166–1191). With many path entries this is repeated O(n·keylen) work per import.
findings.proposed_fix: At tsconfig parse time (tsconfig_json.rs:568–645) partition `paths` into (a) exact keys kept in the hash map and (b) a `Vec<{prefix: Box<[u8]>, suffix: Box<[u8]>, originals: Vec<Box<[u8]>>}>` for wildcard keys. `match_tsconfig_paths` then does one hash lookup for exact and iterates only the precomputed wildcard vector without rescanning for '*'.

findings.title: O(N²·M) reverse-dependency path matching in metafile markdown generator
findings.problem: `JsonObject` (line 470-490) stores entries in a `Vec<(Box<[u8]>, JsonValue)>` so every `.get()`/`.contains()` is an O(entries) linear scan. In `generate_markdown`, the reverse-dependency pass (line 835-938) iterates every input file × every import, and for each import first calls `inputs_obj.contains(target)` (O(N)) and on miss does a second full `inputs_obj.iter()` scan doing `ends_with()`/basename matching. With N input files and M imports per file this is O(N²·M) byte-slice comparisons — a 5k-file project with 10 imports each is ~250M comparisons.
findings.evidence: if inputs_obj.contains(target) { matched_key = Some(target); } else { for (input_key, _) in inputs_obj.iter() { if target.ends_with(input_key) { ... } ... } }   // nested under `for (path, input) in inputs_obj.iter()` → `for imp in imps_arr.iter()`
findings.proposed_fix: Give `JsonObject` a `StringHashMap<usize>` side-index (or replace `entries: Vec<..>` with an order-preserving hash map) so `get`/`contains` are O(1). For the suffix-match fallback, build a one-time `basename -> Vec<&[u8]>` index over `inputs_obj` keys before the loop and probe that instead of rescanning all inputs per import.

findings.title: Fresh Vec<usize> allocated and sorted per source file in Step 4 import matching
findings.problem: `match_imports_with_exports_for_file` is called once per reachable file with named imports (scanImportsAndExports.rs:410-427, sequential Step 4). Each call does `let mut order: Vec<usize> = (0..keys.len()).collect();` then sorts it — a fresh heap allocation per file. For a 10k-file graph this is 10k short-lived Vec allocations on the main linker thread. The adjacent `cycle_detector: Vec<ImportTracker>` is already hoisted to `LinkerContext` as a reused scratch, so the pattern exists but wasn't applied here.
findings.evidence:
let mut order: Vec<usize> = (0..unsafe { (&*keys).len() }).collect();
order.sort_by(|&a, &b| unsafe { (&*keys)[a].inner_index().cmp(&(&*keys)[b].inner_index()) });
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `import_order_scratch: Vec<usize>` (or `Vec<u32>`) field on `LinkerContext`, `.clear()` + `.extend(0..n)` at each call, matching the existing `cycle_detector` reuse pattern. Alternatively sort a stack `SmallVec<[u32; 32]>` since most files have few named imports.

findings.title: Yarn printer builds requested-versions map with O(P×D) nested scan
findings.problem: The outer `while i < package_count` loop iterates every package (P), and for each one re-scans the whole `resolutions_buffer` (length D) from the start via `resolutions.iter().position(|&r| r == i)`. Total work is O(P × D); on a 2k-package / 5k-dependency project that is ~10M iterations where a single O(D) pass would suffice.
findings.proposed_fix: Do one pass over `resolutions_buffer` and bucket dependency indices by `PackageID` into a `Vec<Vec<usize>>` (or record `(start,len)` into `all_requested_versions_buf` after a stable-by-pkg_id sort). Then the per-package loop just reads its bucket — O(D + P log P) overall instead of O(P × D).

findings.title: Sort comparators call is_less_than twice, doubling string-compare work
findings.problem: `builder.sort_buf.sort_unstable_by` derives an `Ordering` by calling `DepSorter::is_less_than(a,b)` and, when that returns false, `is_less_than(b,a)`. Each call slices both dep names from the string buffer and runs `strings::order`, so half of all comparisons do the byte compare twice. The identical pattern appears in `bun.lock.rs` at lines 671-678 and 760-767 with `TreeDepsSortCtx::is_less_than`, and those sorts run once per hoisted package during lockfile save.
findings.proposed_fix: Give `DepSorter` / `TreeDepsSortCtx` a `cmp(&self, a, b) -> Ordering` that returns `l_dep.behavior.cmp(r_dep.behavior).then_with(|| strings::order(l_name, r_name))` and pass it directly to `sort_unstable_by`. One string comparison per comparator call instead of two.

findings.title: Fresh Vec<u8> allocated per dependency when building pkg_map key
findings.problem: Inside the doubly-nested `while let Some(node) = pkgs_iter.next(None) { for &dep_id in node.dependencies { … } }` migration loop, `let mut key: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` is allocated and grown via `write!` for every single hoisted dependency, then immediately passed to `pkg_map.put(&key, ())` which copies the bytes again into an owned map key. That is two heap allocations per dependency where one reusable buffer would do.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a single `Vec<u8>` (or reuse `temp_buf` declared just above) outside the `while` loop, `key.clear()` at the top of each inner iteration, and build the key with `extend_from_slice` on `node.relative_path`, the optional `/`, and the dep name — avoiding both the per-iteration allocation and the `write!` formatter overhead.

findings.title: O(W²) workspace-row matching when parsing bun.lock
findings.problem: When loading the text lockfile, for each workspace path recorded in `lockfile.workspace_paths` the parser linearly scans every row of the `workspaces` JSON object comparing paths with `strings::eql_long`. The same nested `for workspace_path { for row in object_rows(workspaces_obj) { … } }` pattern is repeated at line 3366. Both loops run over the same W entries, giving O(W²) string comparisons; in large monorepos (hundreds of workspaces) this is tens to hundreds of thousands of long path comparisons on every `bun install`.
findings.proposed_fix: Before these loops, build a `StringHashMap<usize>` mapping each `workspaces_obj` row key to its row index (one O(W) pass). Then look up each `workspace_path` in O(1) and index directly into the row slice. Apply the same table at the IS_ROOT block near line 3366.

findings.title: String::has_prefix_comptime scans/transcodes entire string to check a short prefix
findings.problem: The fast path calls `as_utf8()`, which for an 8-bit WTFStringImpl runs `strings::is_all_ascii()` over the ENTIRE payload (mod.rs:742). The fallback calls `to_utf8_without_ref()`, which for UTF-16 allocates and transcodes the ENTIRE string (wtf.rs:83). So checking a 5-byte `b"data:"` prefix on a multi-MB data: URL (fetch.rs:580) does O(n) work where O(prefix.len()) would suffice. All in-tree prefixes are ASCII literals, and ASCII bytes are identical in Latin-1/UTF-8, so only the first `prefix.len()` code units ever need examining.
findings.proposed_fix: Dispatch on encoding without materialising UTF-8: for `is_utf16()` call the existing allocation-free `strings::has_prefix_comptime_utf16(self.utf16(), prefix)` (immutable.rs:1243); for any 8-bit tag compare `self.latin1()[..prefix.len()]` directly against `prefix` (ASCII prefix bytes are encoding-invariant, guarded by a `debug_assert!(prefix.iter().all(|b| *b < 0x80))`). No full-string scan or allocation needed.

findings.title: String::eql_utf8/eql_comptime transcodes the whole string before a length-mismatch can short-circuit
findings.problem: `eql_utf8` (and `eql_comptime`, which forwards to it) calls `to_utf8_without_ref()` first. For UTF-16 WTF-backed strings that allocates and transcodes the full string via `to_utf8_alloc` (wtf.rs:83); for 8-bit WTF it runs `is_all_ascii()` over all bytes (via `to_utf8_from_latin1`, lib.rs:1641). Only AFTER this O(n) work does the slice `==` check lengths. There are 151 call sites, many in per-property/per-element loops (pretty_format.rs, ConsoleObject.rs, parse_args.rs) comparing against short ASCII literals like `b"Object"`/`b"return"` where the receiver can be arbitrarily long.
findings.proposed_fix: Route `String::eql_comptime` through `self.to_zig_string().eql_comptime(other)` — `ZigString::eql_comptime` (mod.rs:1635) already does the allocation-free, encoding-aware compare (length check for 8-bit, `eql_comptime_utf16` for 16-bit). Alternatively, early-return on `self.length() != other.len()` for 8-bit inputs and use `strings::utf16_eql_string` (immutable.rs:2828) for UTF-16.

findings.title: StringJoiner::detach_lifetime re-allocates and copies the entire nodes Vec to change a lifetime
findings.problem: `detach_lifetime` builds a fresh `Vec<Node<'static>>` via `self.nodes.into_iter().map(...).collect()`, allocating a new Vec and moving every node (each ~24 bytes). `Node<'a>` and `Node<'static>` differ only by the erased lifetime on `Borrowed(&[u8])`, so their layout is identical and the copy is pure overhead. The bundler calls this once per chunk (postProcessJSChunk.rs:843, postProcessCSSChunk.rs:149, postProcessHTMLChunk.rs:40, MetafileBuilder.rs:417), and a JS chunk's joiner accumulates one node per compile-result/wrapper piece — easily hundreds of nodes on real bundles.
findings.evidence:
nodes: self
    .nodes
    .into_iter()
    .map(|node| match node {
        Node::Borrowed(slice) => Node::Borrowed(unsafe { &*core::ptr::from_ref::<[u8]>(slice) }),
        Node::Owned(boxed) => Node::Owned(boxed),
    })
    .collect(),
findings.proposed_fix: Since lifetimes are erased at runtime and `Node<'a>` / `Node<'static>` have identical repr, replace the iter+collect with `nodes: unsafe { core::mem::transmute::<Vec<Node<'a>>, Vec<Node<'static>>>(self.nodes) }` (the function is already `unsafe` with the same invariant documented). This removes one Vec allocation + N moves per bundler chunk.

findings.title: MultiArrayList::sort() stable path is O(n²) insertion sort
findings.problem: bun_collections_sort_context (the stable sort used by MultiArrayList::sort/sort_span) is a plain nested-while insertion sort: O(n²) comparisons and O(n²) swap_rows calls, where each swap_rows touches every SoA column. The comment says it ports Zig std.mem.sortContext, but Zig's is a block sort (O(n log n)). The sole non-test caller is src/sourcemap/Mapping.rs:216, which sorts the entire parsed sourcemap mapping list when any segment has a negative generated-column delta — that list can be tens of thousands of entries for real bundles.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with an index-permutation sort: collect (a..b) into a Vec<usize>, sort it with slice::sort_by (driftsort, O(n log n) stable) using the SortContext, then apply the permutation in place via cycle-following swap_rows. ArrayHashMap::sort in array_hash_map.rs:906 already uses exactly this pattern and can be copied.

findings.title: LinearFifo::realign() wrapped branch is O(buf_len × head / 2KB) — effectively quadratic
findings.problem: When the readable region wraps, realign() left-rotates the whole buffer by repeatedly: (a) saving a ≤2KB prefix to a stack scratch, (b) memmoving the entire remaining buf_len-n elements down, (c) copying the scratch to the tail — looping ceil(head / tmp_len) times. Total work is O(buf_len × head / tmp_len) instead of O(buf_len). ensure_total_capacity() calls realign() before every Dynamic grow, and writable_with_size() calls it whenever the first writable segment is too short; callers include the JSC event-loop task queue, N-API threadsafe-function queue, install dependency queues, and the WebSocket send/receive buffers (src/http_jsc/websocket_client.rs:456,1010).
findings.proposed_fix: In ensure_total_capacity(), drop the realign() call and copy the two readable segments (old[head..buf_len] then old[0..wrap_len]) straight into new[0..count] — two memcpys, O(count). For realign() itself (used by shrink/writable_with_size), perform the rotate on the raw MaybeUninit storage with ptr::copy using the triple-reversal or slice::rotate_left algorithm, which is O(buf_len) with no heap allocation.

findings.title: StringHashMap safe insert paths heap-allocate the key Box even when the key already exists
findings.problem: put(), put_assume_capacity(), put_no_clobber(), get_or_put_value(), and get_or_put_context_adapted() all build owned_key::<A>(key) (a fresh Box<[u8]> via Vec::with_capacity_in + extend_from_slice) before probing. hashbrown::HashMap::insert/entry keeps the original key on hit and drops the freshly-allocated Box immediately. So every call that hits an existing key pays one malloc + one free for nothing. The unsafe get_or_put_borrowed fast path avoids this, but the safe entry points used by dotenv, bundler defines, js_parser fold, run_command script maps, etc. do not.
findings.proposed_fix: Probe with the borrowed slice first via self.inner.raw_entry_mut().from_key(key); on RawEntryMut::Occupied overwrite the value, on Vacant call e.insert(owned_key::<A>(key), value). This keeps one hash/one probe and allocates only on miss. put_static_key_hashed (line 1929) already demonstrates the raw_entry_mut pattern in this file.

findings.title: Fresh Vec<[u32;3]> allocated for every HTTP/2 response header block
findings.problem: decode_header_block runs on every END_HEADERS frame (i.e. once per HTTP/2 response, plus any 1xx/trailers). It allocates `let mut bounds: Vec<[u32; 3]> = Vec::new();` and pushes one entry per response header, then iterates it once to build decoded_headers. The Vec is dropped at function exit, so a new allocation + growth sequence happens for every response even though the session already owns reusable scratch (encode_scratch) for the outbound side.
findings.proposed_fix: Store a `decode_bounds_scratch: Vec<[u32;3]>` on ClientSession (next to encode_scratch), mem::take it at the top of decode_header_block, clear+return it at the end. This turns a per-response allocation into a single amortized buffer per connection.

findings.title: O(n_streams) linear scan in h2/h3 *_by_http_id, run per streaming-body chunk
findings.problem: stream_body_by_http_id / resume_receive_by_http_id / drain_response_body_by_http_id each iterate all entries of self.streams (keyed by stream_id) comparing client.async_http_id linearly. drain_queued_writes (HTTPThread.rs:733) calls stream_body_by_http_id once per JS-side body chunk, and drain_queued_receive_resumes (HTTPThread.rs:832-833) calls two of these back-to-back for the same id. With many concurrent h2 streams (the default server limit is 100+), each upload chunk or backpressure resume costs O(n_streams). The h3 variant (ClientContext.rs:216) is worse: it iterates every h3 session and every stream within, and stream_body_by_http_id doesn't even break out of the outer session loop on match.
findings.evidence: for &stream in self.streams.values() { let Some(client) = stream_mut(stream).client_mut() else { continue }; if client.async_http_id != async_http_id { continue; } ... }
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a secondary map async_http_id -> *mut Stream on ClientSession (populated in attach/adopt, removed in drop_stream) so these lookups become O(1). At minimum, merge resume_receive_by_http_id + drain_response_body_by_http_id into one scan, and make h3::ClientContext::stream_body_by_http_id return once the inner session reports a match.

findings.title: h2 write_request lowercases and classifies every request header twice
findings.problem: write_request makes two passes over request.headers. Pass 1 (lines 109-127) lowercases each name into lower_buf and calls classify_request_header just to extract Host/Expect. Pass 2 (lines 144-179) lowercases each name again into the same lower_buf and calls classify_request_header again to decide drop/sensitive/never_index. The classification comment even says 'One classification pass per request header', but the code does two. This is 2x the per-header lowering+match work on every HTTP/2 request.
findings.proposed_fix: Record the classification result from the first pass (e.g. a small [Option<RequestHeader>; N] array or two bitsets keyed by header index) and reuse it in the second pass, or have the first pass only check Host/Expect via a cheaper length-gated compare and let the second pass do the sole full classification.

findings.title: O(n²) static-route dedup via Vec::contains + Vec::remove
findings.problem: normalize_static_routes_list loops backwards over static_routes and, for each entry, calls `static_routes_dedupe_list.contains(&h)` (O(n) linear scan over a Vec<u64>) and `list.remove(index)` (O(n) shift). Together this is O(n²) in the number of static routes. It runs on every `reload_static_routes()` — which is invoked once per completed HTML bundle (HTMLBundle.rs:709) and on every `server.reload()` — so a project with hundreds of bundled assets/HTML routes pays quadratic cost repeatedly during dev-server startup and hot-reload.
findings.proposed_fix: Use a HashSet<u64> (or IntSet) for O(1) membership instead of Vec::contains, and use `list.swap_remove(index)` instead of `remove` since the list is re-sorted immediately afterwards (line 252) so preserving order during removal is unnecessary. This brings dedup to O(n) and the whole pass to O(n log n) dominated by the sort.

findings.title: O(N_static × N_user) nested scan for HEAD route check in set_routes
findings.problem: Inside `for entry in &self.config.static_routes` the code runs `self.user_routes.iter().any(|route| ... route.route.path.as_bytes() == &*entry.path)` to decide `path_has_user_head_route`. This is a linear scan of every user route for every static route — O(N_static × N_user). set_routes runs at listen() and on every reload/reload_static_routes, so large route tables (hundreds of routes) pay a quadratic comparison cost on every hot-reload.
findings.proposed_fix: Before the `for entry in &self.config.static_routes` loop, build a HashSet<&[u8]> (or HashSet<u64> of wyhash(path)) containing the paths of user routes whose method == Specific(HEAD). Inside the loop replace the `.iter().any(...)` with a single O(1) set lookup.

findings.title: Per-request heap allocation of file path in FileRoute::on
findings.problem: `FileRoute::on` runs on every request to a file route and immediately does `let path_buf: Vec<u8> = ... get_path() ... .to_vec()`. The comment says this is only to keep the borrow from spanning the scopeguard creation, not for correctness — the FileRoute's refcount is ≥2 for the entire function (route-table ref + `ref_()` at line 327), so the underlying store/path cannot be freed until after the function returns. Every file-route request pays a heap allocation + copy of the path string.
findings.proposed_fix: Bind the store pointer to a local (`let store = this.blob.store.get().unwrap();`) so the temporary lives long enough, then borrow `let path: &[u8] = store.get_path()...;` directly, or use `bun_ptr::detach_lifetime` as is done elsewhere in RequestContext.rs (e.g. render_bytes line 3838) since the backing allocation is provably alive for the function's duration. Removes one heap alloc per request.

findings.title: add_symbol_for_name boxes the lookup key on every class/id selector
findings.problem: `local_scope.entry(Box::<[u8]>::from(name))` heap-allocates a fresh `Box<[u8]>` on every call, even when the symbol is already in the map (the Occupied arm immediately drops the Box). This runs once per `.class`/`#id` selector parsed in CSS-modules mode (called from `SelectorParser::new_local_identifier`), so a stylesheet with thousands of class references does thousands of throwaway heap allocations purely for the hash lookup.
findings.proposed_fix: Do a borrowed lookup first (`local_scope.get_mut(name)` / raw-entry API over `&[u8]`) and only `Box::from(name)` on the miss/insert path. Alternatively change `LocalScope` to key on `&'static [u8]` (arena-lifetime, same erasure already used for `name_static` two lines above) so no Box is ever needed.

findings.title: Minified [attr=val] serialization allocates two Vecs per selector; Printer.scratchbuf is never used
findings.problem: When minifying, serializing an `AttributeInNoNamespace` component creates `let mut id: Vec<u8> = Vec::new()` and `let mut s: Vec<u8> = Vec::new()` to compare identifier vs quoted-string encodings, then throws both away. This is two heap allocations per `[attr=val]` selector in the output. Meanwhile `Printer` carries a `scratchbuf: BumpVec<'a, u8>` field that is constructed and stored but literally never read anywhere in the codebase — exactly the reusable buffer this code asks for in its own `// PERF: should we put a scratch buffer in the printer` comment.
findings.proposed_fix: Reuse `dest.scratchbuf`: clear it, write the identifier form, record `id_len`, then write the quoted form to the tail and compare lengths, writing the shorter slice to `dest`. Same fix applies to the identical pattern in `src/css/values/url.rs:110` and `src/css/properties/custom.rs:105` for `url()` serialization.

findings.title: Fresh Box<PathBuffer> (4KB Linux / ~98KB Windows) allocated per directory traversed
findings.problem: `transition_to_dir_iter_state` runs once per directory the walker enters and does `Box::new(PathBuffer::uninit())` each time — a fresh 4KB (Linux) / ~98KB (Windows) heap allocation. Only one `IterState::Directory` is ever alive (the old one is dropped in the `GetNext` branch before this is called), so the buffer could be reused across transitions. The repo already has `bun_paths::path_buffer_pool` for exactly this pattern and uses it elsewhere in this file (see `statat_windows`).
findings.evidence: let mut dir_path_buf = Box::new(PathBuffer::uninit());
findings.proposed_fix: Store a `bun_paths::path_buffer_pool::Guard` in `Directory` instead of `Box<PathBuffer>`, obtained via `path_buffer_pool::get()`. The guard returns the buffer to the per-thread pool on drop, turning N per-directory allocations into (amortized) zero.

findings.title: Redundant .to_vec() immediately re-copied by dupe_z in literal-tail stat path
findings.problem: The literal-tail optimization (e.g. `**/package.json`) runs once per directory and does `pattern_slice(...).to_vec()` followed by `dupe_z(&pat_slice)`. `dupe_z` already copies its `&[u8]` argument into a new NUL-terminated box, so the intermediate `Vec` is a pure extra allocation+copy. There is no borrow-checker reason for it — the borrow of `self.walker.pattern` ends as soon as `dupe_z` returns, and only the owned `pathz` is used afterward.
findings.proposed_fix: Call `dupe_z` directly on the slice: `let pathz = dupe_z(self.walker.pattern_components[idx as usize].pattern_slice(&self.walker.pattern));` and delete `pat_slice`.

findings.title: prepare_matched_path: two hash probes + duplicate path allocation per matched result
findings.problem: For every matched path the code hashes/probes twice (`contains_key` then `insert`) and allocates the path twice: once via `self.join()` into `name_matched_path`, and again inside `StringArrayHashMap::insert`, which calls `box_key` to clone the key bytes into its own `Box<[u8]>`. The existing PERF comment notes the double lookup but not the duplicate allocation. Same pattern in `prepare_matched_path_symlink` at line 1966.
findings.evidence:
if self.matched_paths.contains_key(&name_matched_path) { ... return Ok(None); }
self.matched_paths.insert(&name_matched_path, ());
findings.proposed_fix: Use a single `get_or_put`/entry-style API that returns whether the key was newly inserted, and either (a) add a `StringArrayHashMap` insert variant that takes ownership of an existing `Box<[u8]>` key, or (b) build the path into the scratch `path_buf`, probe once, and only allocate the returned `MatchedPath` on miss.

findings.title: Heap dupe_z just to NUL-terminate an entry name for lstatat (FileKind::Unknown path)
findings.problem: When `readdir` reports `FileKind::Unknown` (common on NFS/XFS-without-ftype and a few other filesystems), every entry triggers `dupe_z(entry_name)` — a heap `Box<[u8]>` — solely to get a NUL-terminated view for `A::lstatat`. Directory entry names are bounded (NAME_MAX, typically 255 bytes), so a small stack buffer would avoid the per-entry allocation entirely.
findings.proposed_fix: Copy `entry_name` into a `[u8; 256]` (or reuse `self.walker.path_buf`) stack buffer, write the trailing NUL, and build the `ZStr` from that — zero heap traffic on the per-entry hot path.

findings.title: InternalSourceMap::append_vlq_to does not pre-reserve output capacity
findings.problem: append_vlq_to() re-encodes every mapping as VLQ text into a MutableString with no upfront capacity reservation, even though self.mapping_count() is known. For large files (the module doc cites typescript.js at ~843k mappings ≈ 3–5 MB of VLQ text) this forces ~20 geometric-growth realloc+memcpy cycles on the output Vec. All three callers (Chunk::print_source_map_contents_from_internal, ParsedSourceMap::write_vlqs, and the testing API) pass a freshly-created MutableString::init_empty() with zero capacity.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `out.grow_if_needed(self.mapping_count() * 6).ok();` (or similar heuristic — a 4-field VLQ segment averages ~5 bytes plus the comma/semicolon) at the top of append_vlq_to. mapping_count() is read straight from the blob header so the estimate is free.

findings.title: Tag::parse_with_pre_count does a useless first-pass scan whose early-return can never fire
findings.problem: The first `for &c in input` loop scans the entire tag slice (until the first space) counting `+` and `-`, and the counts are used solely for the `if build_count == 0 && pre_count == 0 { return }` early exit at line 1143. Both call sites make that condition unreachable: `Tag::parse` (line 585) is only entered when `input[i]` is `-`/`+`, so the slice starts with one and the first iteration increments a counter; the other caller (line 627) passes `initial_pre_count = 1`. The loop is a pure extra O(tag_len) pass before the real parse loop at line 1163 re-scans the same bytes.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the counting loop and the early-return block (lines 1127–1148); the second loop already handles all cases and `result.len` defaults to 0. Alternatively guard the early-return with a cheap `input.first()` check if defensive behavior is wanted. Eliminates a redundant scan per prerelease/build-tagged version (packages like typescript/next have thousands).

findings.title: Unnecessary clone of symbol_uses + declared_symbols into a throwaway Part for dead-part cleanup
findings.problem: When `append_part` discovers a part is dead (empty stmts but has usages), it heap-clones both `self.declared_symbols` and `self.symbol_uses` (an ArrayHashMap, so three array allocations) into a temporary `js_ast::Part`, passes it by `&` to `clear_symbol_usages_from_dead_part`, and drops it immediately. The callee only iterates `.keys()/.values()/.refs()` and writes to `self.symbols`, so the clones exist solely to satisfy `&mut self`. This is two full allocations + copies per dead top-level part.
findings.proposed_fix: Change `clear_symbol_usages_from_dead_part` to a free fn (or inline it here) taking `(&SymbolUseMap, &DeclaredSymbolList, &mut [Symbol])` so disjoint fields of `self` can be borrowed without cloning. Alternatively, iterate `self.symbol_uses` / `self.declared_symbols` directly and index `self.symbols` — all three fields are disjoint.

findings.title: hoist_symbols snapshots every scope member into an arena Vec even when none are hoisted
findings.problem: `hoist_symbols` recurses over every scope. For each non-hoisting-boundary scope it bump-allocates a `BumpVec<(StoreStr, Member)>` containing *all* members, then the loop body does `if !self.symbols[..].is_hoisted() { continue; }` for most of them. In modern code dominated by `let`/`const`, block scopes rarely contain any hoisted (`var`) symbols, so the snapshot (and its arena memory) is pure overhead for the majority of scopes. The snapshot exists only for the rare `scope.generated` mut-borrow inside the sloppy-mode block-fn branch.
findings.proposed_fix: Filter while building the snapshot: only push members whose symbol `is_hoisted()` (plus the CatchBinding-parent check), and skip the allocation entirely if the count is 0. This keeps the borrowck-motivated snapshot but shrinks it from O(all block-scope members) to O(var-declared members), typically zero for most block scopes.

findings.title: 4 hash lookups per comparison when sorting JS chunk keys, plus duplicate lookup per key
findings.problem: `ChunkSortContext::less_than` (line 399-419) does two `self.chunks.get(&key)` hash lookups, and the comparator wrapper at line 423-431 invokes it twice (`less_than(a,b)` then `less_than(b,a)`), so every sort comparison hashes+probes the map 4 times. Keys are entry-bit byte slices (size = ceil(entry_points/8)), so hashing is non-trivial with many entry points. The follow-up loop (line 433-444) then repeats the work: `js_chunks.get(&key)` followed by `js_chunks.get_index(&key)` for the same key.
findings.proposed_fix: Collect `(entry_point_id, key, index)` once per chunk into a Vec, sort that Vec by `(id==0, key)` directly (no hash lookups in the comparator), then iterate it using the stored `index` into `js_chunks.values_mut()` instead of re-hashing.

findings.title: emit_vlq pushes line-separator ';' one byte at a time
findings.problem: The loop `while *generated_line < state.generated_line { out.list.push(b';'); *generated_line += 1; }` emits each semicolon with a separate Vec::push (length check + potential grow branch) instead of computing the gap once and doing a single fill. The sibling encoder ParsedSourceMap::write_vlqs (ParsedSourceMap.rs:320) already solves the identical problem with `writer.splat_byte_all(b';', inc)`. For transpiler output most gaps are 0–1 so the cost is small, but large line gaps (minified bundles, generated shims) degrade to one push per blank line.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute `let gap = (state.generated_line - *generated_line) as usize;` and use a batched append (e.g. `out.list.extend(core::iter::repeat_n(b';', gap))` or the MutableString equivalent of splat_byte_all), then set `*generated_line = state.generated_line` and `prev.generated_column = 0` once.

findings.title: List::and_range copies the ~128-byte Range twice into the new node
findings.problem: `tail.range = *range;` is executed immediately after `Box::new(Query { range: *range, next: None })`, re-storing the same `Range` (two `Comparator`s, each holding a 56-byte `Version`). It is a dead store — a leftover second copy of a struct that was just initialized with the identical value. Only triggers when a spec has a 2nd+ AND comparator, so not very hot, but trivially removable.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete line 332 (`tail.range = *range;`). The field is already set by the struct literal on line 329.

findings.title: Linear scan of layer_duplicates for every CSS import-order entry
findings.problem: The @layer-dedup pass iterates every `order` entry and, for each, linearly scans `layer_duplicates: Vec<DuplicateEntry>` comparing `LayerName` slices element-by-element until it finds an equal layer set (or appends a new one). This is O(order.len() × distinct_layer_sets × avg_layer_names) slice-compare work. For CSS graphs that import many files sharing a handful of layer sets it's fine, but with many distinct `@layer` combinations it degrades toward quadratic.
findings.proposed_fix: Hash the `layers_key` slice (e.g. wyhash over each `LayerName`) and keep an `ArrayHashMap<u64, usize>` from layer-set hash to `layer_duplicates` index; probe the map first and only fall back to the element-wise `eql` on hash hit to confirm.

findings.title: FormData file parts are copied twice (read_file buffer cloned into joiner, then freed)
findings.problem: When serializing a `DOMFormData` with a file-backed Blob entry, `node_fs.read_file` produces an owned `StringOrBuffer::Buffer`. The code then does `joiner.push_cloned(result.slice())`, which allocates a second buffer of the same size and memcpys (`StringJoiner::push_cloned` = `Box::from(data)`, /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/StringJoiner.rs:71-76), and immediately afterwards `buf.destroy()` frees the original. For every file entry this doubles peak memory and adds a full-file memcpy; this runs once per File field on every `fetch(body: FormData)` / `new Response(formData)` that contains file paths.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:4085-4093 `Ok(mut result) => { joiner.push_cloned(result.slice()); if let StringOrBuffer::Buffer(buf) = &mut result { buf.destroy(); } }` — the comment explicitly notes Drop is a no-op for Buffer, confirming `result` owns a heap buffer that is discarded after the clone.
findings.proposed_fix: Extract the owned allocation from `StringOrBuffer::Buffer` and hand it to `joiner.push_owned(...)` instead of `push_cloned`. `StringJoiner::push_owned(Box<[u8]>)` already exists and avoids the copy; only the Buffer→Box<[u8]> move helper (or an `into_boxed_slice()` on the NodeFS Buffer type) is needed.

findings.title: Command-substitution stdout buffer is .clone()'d instead of moved
findings.problem: When a `$(...)` command substitution finishes, `child_done` does `buffered_stdout_mut().clone()` to obtain the captured stdout, producing a full heap copy of the buffer. The source buffer lives in the child Script's duped `ShellExecEnv` (created at line 217 with `ShellExecEnvKind::CmdSubst` + `OutKind::Pipe`, so always `Bufio::Owned`) and is unconditionally freed a few lines later by `interp.deinit_node(child)` (line 637). The clone is therefore a pure extra allocation + memcpy of the entire captured output; for `$(cat bigfile)` or any substitution producing megabytes, peak memory and CPU roughly double for that step.
findings.evidence: Expansion.rs:602-609: `let stdout = unsafe { interp.as_script_mut(child).base.shell_mut().buffered_stdout_mut() }.clone();` followed by Expansion.rs:637 `interp.deinit_node(child);`. The buffer is consumed by `post_subshell_expansion(me, stdout)` which takes `mut stdout: Vec<u8>` by value (line 545), or by `extend_from_slice(&stdout[..hi])` in the quoted branch — neither needs the original to survive. interpreter.rs:1937-1940 confirms `CmdSubst` + `OutKind::Pipe` yields `Bufio::Owned`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `.clone()` with `core::mem::take(...)` on the `&mut Vec<u8>`. Because the CmdSubst env is always `Bufio::Owned` for stdout (stdout is forced to `OutKind::Pipe` at Expansion.rs:211), taking leaves an empty Vec in an env that is about to be dropped. Optionally add a `ShellExecEnv::take_buffered_stdout()` helper (or a `debug_assert!(matches!(_buffered_stdout, Bufio::Owned(_)))`) to encode that invariant.

findings.title: MySQL result rows heap-allocate a fresh Box<[SQLDataCell]> per row
findings.problem: Both Row::decode_text (line 197) and Row::decode_binary (line 257) do `vec![SQLDataCell::null(); self.columns.len()].into_boxed_slice()` on every row. The caller JSMySQLConnection::on_result_row (JSMySQLConnection.rs:832) builds a fresh Row with `values: Box::default()` and then decode() allocates this Box, all per-row. For a result set of N rows this is N heap alloc+free pairs plus N*cols zeroing, where the buffer size is fixed by the statement's column count and could be reused across rows.
findings.evidence: ResultSet.rs:197 `let cells = vec![SQLDataCell::null(); self.columns.len()].into_boxed_slice();` and identically at line 257; invoked from the per-row path MySQLConnection.rs:1537 `.on_result_row(request, statement, reader)?` -> JSMySQLConnection.rs:832-841 which constructs a new `ResultSet::Row { values: Box::default(), .. }` and calls `row.decode(reader)`. Contrast with the Postgres DataRow path (PostgresSQLConnection.rs:2430) which uses a 70-slot stack array for the common case.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the Postgres approach: use a fixed stack array (e.g. `[SQLDataCell::default(); 70]`) inside on_result_row for column counts below JSObject::max_inline_capacity(), and only heap-allocate when columns exceed that. Alternatively, keep a reusable `Vec<SQLDataCell>` on the MySQLStatement (sized once at header time) and `clear()`/re-fill it per row instead of allocating a new Box each row.

findings.title: O(events × watchlist) nested path-compare loop on Windows (acknowledged TODO)
findings.problem: For every `FILE_NOTIFY_INFORMATION` event, the code iterates the entire watchlist (`for item_idx in 0..n_items`) and calls `is_parent_or_equal(path, eventpath)` on each entry. This is O(events × watched_items × path_len) per `watch_loop_cycle`. The TODO comment at line 437 explicitly acknowledges this needs a better search structure. For projects with thousands of watched files, a burst of events (common on save-all / git checkout) degrades quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a `HashMap<HashType, WatchItemIndex>` keyed on the path hash (already computed/stored as `WatchItem.hash`) and look up the exact path first; only fall back to a directory-prefix scan when no exact match. Alternatively keep the file_path column sorted and binary-search a prefix range, as the TODO suggests.

findings.title: Per-assertion Vec<u8> heap allocation in Expect::get_value() that is discarded on the happy path
findings.problem: Expect::get_value() is the shared prelude for nearly every matcher (reached via matcher_prelude() from toBe, toEqual, toStrictEqual, toContain, toHaveLength, toMatch, toMatchObject, toThrow, run_string_affix_matcher, contain_matcher, etc.). On every single assertion it unconditionally calls Output::pretty_fmt_rt(matcher_params_fmt, ...), which internally runs pretty_fmt_runtime() and allocates a fresh Vec<u8> (Vec::with_capacity(fmt.len()*4), ~72 bytes for the common "<green>expected<r>" literal). The rendered buffer is passed into process_promise() as `impl fmt::Display`, but process_promise() only touches it inside the `Promise::Resolves | Promise::Rejects` failure branches; for the overwhelmingly common case (no .resolves/.rejects, `_ => Ok(value)`) the PrettyBuf is never read and is simply dropped. This is a heap allocation + byte-scan per passing assertion.
findings.evidence: expect.rs:378: `let matcher_params = Output::pretty_fmt_rt(matcher_params_fmt, Output::enable_ansi_colors_stderr());` feeding into `Self::process_promise(self.custom_label.clone(), self.flags.get(), global_this, value, ..., matcher_params, false)`. process_promise() at expect.rs:402-479 only uses matcher_params inside `throw_pretty_matcher_error` calls in the resolves/rejects mismatch branches; the final `_ => Ok(value)` arm drops it unused. bun_core/output.rs:2069 `pretty_fmt_runtime` starts with `let mut out = Vec::with_capacity(fmt.len() * 4);`. Call sites passing non-empty templates include toBe.rs:15, toEqual.rs:14, toStrictEqual.rs:14, toContain.rs:17, toHaveLength.rs:12, toMatch.rs:12, toMatchObject.rs:11, toThrow.rs:63, expect.rs:2068 (run_string_affix_matcher), expect.rs:2197 (contain_matcher).
findings.proposed_fix: Defer the ANSI rewrite: pass the raw `matcher_params_fmt: &'static str` through to process_promise() and only call Output::pretty_fmt_rt inside the three error branches that actually format it (the throw_pretty_matcher_error calls). Alternatively, memoize like get_signature() does (there are only ~4 distinct literal values), or wrap it in a lazy Display adapter that calls pretty_fmt_rt on first use. Any of these makes the passing-assertion path allocation-free.

findings.title: Pattern.cacheKey() rebuilds suffix string via += loop on every call, never memoized
findings.problem: cacheKey(index) concatenates this.#globStrings[index..] with '/' using `key += ...` in a loop every time it is called. #globStrings is immutable after construction and shared across all Pattern.child() instances, so the same (pattern,index) pair is recomputed many times. It is called from cache.add() (line 289, once per index per path) and cache.seen() (line 294, invoked twice per (child × index) at line 640 and again at lines 697/700/704/708). For a glob over a large tree this is O(entries × indexes × patternSegments) string work with repeated reallocs from `+=`.
findings.evidence: Lines 353-362: `let key = ""; for (let i = index; i < this.#globStrings.length; i++) { key += this.#globStrings[i]; if (i !== this.#globStrings.length - 1) key += "/"; }`. Callers: line 289 `cache.add(pattern.cacheKey(index))` inside a for-of, and line 294 `this.#cache.get(path)?.has(pattern.cacheKey(index))` called from the per-child per-index hot loop at line 640.
findings.proposed_fix: Precompute the suffix keys once: in the Pattern constructor, build `#cacheKeys = new Array(globStrings.length+1)` by walking backward (`#cacheKeys[i] = globStrings[i] + '/' + #cacheKeys[i+1]`) and have child() share the parent's array. cacheKey(index) becomes an O(1) array lookup. Alternatively use `this.#globStrings.slice(index).join('/')` with a small memo map keyed by index.

findings.title: rm -rf allocates a fresh Vec<u8> per directory entry despite stated "O(1) memory"
findings.problem: `zig_delete_tree_min_stack_size_with_kind_hint` comments "Here we must avoid recursion, in order to provide O(1) memory guarantee" (line 10110) but then does `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside the per-entry `'dir_it` loop. The Vec is only ever passed by reference to `dt_open_dir`/`dt_delete_file` and copied into the pre-existing stack buffer `dir_name_buf`; ownership is never required. This is one heap alloc+free per file deleted. The sibling `zig_delete_tree` at line 9930 does the same: for non-directory entries (the majority) the Vec is created, borrowed once by `dt_delete_file`, and dropped.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:10121 `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside `'dir_it: loop { ... }` starting at line 10115. Same pattern at node_fs.rs:9930 inside the per-entry loop starting at 9919, where only the `stack.push` branch (line 9938) actually needs an owned Vec.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a reusable `let mut entry_name_buf: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` above the outer loop and replace `.to_vec()` with `entry_name_buf.clear(); entry_name_buf.extend_from_slice(entry.name.slice());`. In `zig_delete_tree` (line 9930), do the same and only `entry_name_buf.clone()` (or mem::take + re-reserve) in the single branch that pushes onto `stack`. This reduces allocations from O(total_files) to O(directories) for `fs.rm({recursive:true})`.

findings.title: Recursive readdir withFileTypes re-joins+dirnames the parent path for every entry
findings.problem: Inside the per-entry inner loop (line 6915) of `readdir_with_entries_recursive_sync`, when T::IS_DIRENT is true the code runs `join_spill([root_basename, name_to_copy])` then `dirname(joined)` then compares the result against `dirent_path_prev` — for every entry. Within one directory iteration, `dirname(join(root, basename/entry))` is always the same (the current directory's full path), so the O(path_len) join+normalize+dirname work is redundant after the first entry. The BunString allocation is correctly deduped by the comparison, but the join+dirname themselves are repeated N times per directory. Same pattern in the async variant at lines 6798-6807.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:6977-6990: `if T::IS_DIRENT { let joined = paths::resolve_path::join_spill(..., &[root_basename.as_bytes(), name_to_copy]); let path_u8 = paths::resolve_path::dirname(joined); if dirent_path_prev.is_empty() || dirent_path_prev.byte_slice() != path_u8 { ... } }` inside `loop { ... iterator.next() ... }` at 6915. Inputs to dirname depend only on `root_basename` + `basename_bytes`, which are fixed for the whole inner loop.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the dirent parent path once per outer-loop directory (before the `loop` at 6915): `let path_u8 = if is_root { root_basename } else { join_spill(&mut dirent_spill, &[root_basename, basename_bytes]) };` and build `dirent_path_prev` from it there. Drop the per-entry join/dirname/compare. Apply the same hoist in `readdir_with_entries_recursive_async` (lines 6798-6807).

findings.title: BlockList addAddress/addRange/addSubnet use Vec::insert(0) — O(n²) to build N rules
findings.problem: Each add_* host function inserts the new Rule at index 0 of a Vec<Rule> (`r.insert(0, ...)` at lines 156, 195, 243). Vec::insert(0) memmoves every existing element, so building a BlockList with N rules costs O(N²) moves. Node.js uses `std::list::push_front` (O(1)). A user loading a blocklist of a few thousand CIDRs (common use case) pays millions of element moves while holding the instance mutex.
findings.evidence: `da_rules: JsCell<Vec<Rule>>` (line 72) with `this.da_rules.with_mut(|r| r.insert(0, Rule::Addr(address)));` (line 156), same pattern at 195 and 243. `check()` (line 276) iterates front→back and returns on first match — result is order-independent (all matches return TRUE), so only the `rules` getter (line 364) exposes ordering to JS.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `r.insert(0, rule)` with `r.push(rule)` (O(1) amortized). To preserve the user-visible newest-first ordering exposed by the `rules` getter and by `check()`'s short-circuit preference, iterate `this.da_rules.get().iter().rev()` in both `check()` (line 276) and `rules()` (line 364). Alternatively keep a VecDeque and `push_front`.

findings.title: skip_chunked_framing() re-scans all request headers on every streamed chunk
findings.problem: For streaming fetch request bodies (ReadableStream), `write_request_data` is called once per chunk. It calls `self.skip_chunked_framing()` (line 2177-2181), which calls `self.request_headers.get(b"content-length")` and `self.request_headers.get(b"transfer-encoding")`. `Headers::get` is an O(headers) linear scan with a case-insensitive compare per entry (/workspace/bun/src/http_types/ETag.rs:217-227). The result never changes for the lifetime of the request, yet it is recomputed on every chunk (and again in `write_end_request` at line 2265). For uploads with many small chunks this is O(chunks × headers) wasted work inside the mutex-adjacent hot path.
findings.evidence: FetchTasklet.rs:2177-2181 `fn skip_chunked_framing(&self) -> bool { self.upgraded_connection || self.result.is_http2 || (self.request_headers.get(b"content-length").is_some() && self.request_headers.get(b"transfer-encoding").is_none()) }` — called at 2215 `if self.skip_chunked_framing() { ... }` inside `write_request_data`, which is the per-chunk sink callback (ResumableSink.rs:636 forwards every chunk here). Headers::get is a for-loop over all entries (ETag.rs:221).
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the (content-length-set ∧ !transfer-encoding-set) bit once in `FetchTasklet::get` right after `request_headers: fetch_options.headers` (around line 1895) and store it as a bool field (e.g. `has_explicit_content_length_framing`). `skip_chunked_framing` then becomes `self.upgraded_connection || self.result.is_http2 || self.has_explicit_content_length_framing` — zero header scans per chunk.

findings.title: Builtins clone/to_vec their whole output buffer before enqueue() which immediately copies it again
findings.problem: `IOWriter::enqueue(&self, child, bytelist, buf: &[u8])` does `s.buf.extend_from_slice(buf)` (IOWriter.rs:1023) — it copies the bytes into its own internal buffer and never retains the caller's slice. Yet several builtins allocate an extra full copy of their rendered output solely to satisfy borrowck before passing it as `&[u8]`: seq.rs:196-204 stores `out` into `state.buf` then `let buf = state.buf.clone()`; echo.rs:94/100 `output.clone()`; ls.rs:338/345, cp.rs:364/371, mkdir.rs:241/248, touch.rs:222/229 all do `output.slice().to_vec()`. This runs once per output chunk (per directory for `ls -R`, per copied file for `cp -v`, once per `echo`/`seq`). For `seq 1 1000000` the rendered buffer is ~6.9 MB, so this is a 6.9 MB allocation + memcpy that is dropped immediately after `enqueue` returns. The `yes` builtin already avoids this via a split-borrow (yes.rs:144-147), proving the zero-copy path works.
findings.evidence: seq.rs:196-204: `Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).buf = out; ... let buf = Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).buf.clone(); return Builtin::of_mut(interp, cmd).stdout.enqueue(child, &buf, safeguard);` (comment even says "reshaped for borrowck — clone the slice"). IOWriter.rs:1022-1029: `s.buf.extend_from_slice(buf); s.writers.push(Writer{ ... });` — no pointer to `buf` is kept. yes.rs:146-147 shows the split-borrow alternative: `let (stdout, yes) = Self::split_stdout_state(...); stdout.enqueue(child, &yes.buffer[..yes.buffer_used], safeguard)`.
findings.proposed_fix: For seq/echo: don't store the buffer in state at all (nothing in `on_io_writer_chunk` reads it); pass `&out` directly to `enqueue`. For ls/cp/mkdir/touch `write_out`: add a split-borrow helper like `Yes::split_stdout_state` (stdout and the OutputTask's `output` are in disjoint allocations) and call `stdout.enqueue(child, output.slice(), safeguard)` without `.to_vec()`. Alternatively add an `IOWriter::enqueue_owned(&self, child, bytelist, buf: Vec<u8>)` that appends-by-move so callers can hand over ownership instead of cloning.

findings.title: flatten_tokens() is O(n²) and clones each merged SmolStr
findings.problem: When two adjacent Text tokens are merged, the code calls `self.tokens[j].to_text()` (which deep-clones the SmolStr, heap-allocating if >15 bytes) purely to read its bytes, then `self.tokens.remove(j)` which is an O(n) shift. This loop runs over the whole token vector, so a run of k adjacent Text tokens costs O(k·n) element moves plus k needless SmolStr allocations. Adjacent Text runs are produced by `rollback_braces` for every unclosed brace group, so an input like `{a,b,c,...` with many commas degrades quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Do a single in-place two-pointer compaction (write index / read index) instead of `remove`, and read the j-token's bytes via a split borrow (e.g. `let (lo, hi) = self.tokens.split_at_mut(j)` then `lo[i].append_slice(hi[0].as_text_slice())`) so no SmolStr clone is needed. This turns the pass into O(n) with zero extra allocations.

findings.title: Brace expansion snapshots the output prefix into a fresh Vec at every expansion node
findings.problem: `expand_flat` (and `expand_nested` at lines 766/815) does `let prefix: Vec<u8> = out[out_key][..len].to_vec();` before iterating variants. That is one global-allocator Vec allocation + full prefix copy per expansion node visited, recursively. For `prefix{a,b}{c,d}{e,f}...` the prefix grows and is re-copied at every level; with the 256-group / depth-10 cap that is up to thousands of heap allocations and redundant byte copies per brace-expanded word, in the runtime-hot path called from Expansion::do_brace_expand. The comment at line 765 already flags it as a borrowck reshaping, not a design choice.
findings.proposed_fix: The prefix is only used to seed `out[new_key]` for `i > 0`, and `new_key` is always a fresh index distinct from `out_key`. Before the j=0 recursion mutates `out[out_key]`, pre-fill all `out[*out_key_counter .. *out_key_counter + variants.len()-1]` slots directly from `out[out_key][..starting_len]` using `split_at_mut` (or a raw-pointer pair) — no intermediate Vec. Alternatively thread a single reusable `&mut Vec<u8>` scratch buffer through the recursion and `clear()` it instead of allocating each call.

findings.title: HTTP/2 DATA partial-flush copies payload into a fresh Vec per chunk
findings.problem: In Stream::flush_queue (the per-stream HTTP/2 DATA send path, driven in a loop from flush_stream_queue at line 3149), when a queued frame is larger than the current flow-control/frame-size window the code does `let able_to_send = frame.slice()[0..max_size].to_vec();`. This heap-allocates and memcpy's up to ~16 KB every time a frame is split — i.e. once per partial DATA chunk on every large upload/response. The bytes already live in `frame.buffer` and are only read (`.len()`, `.as_ptr()`, `write_all(&..)`), never mutated or escaped. The sibling full-flush branch at line 1930 correctly uses a borrowed `frame.slice()` with no copy, so this is an asymmetry, not a correctness requirement.
findings.evidence: 1868: `let able_to_send = frame.slice()[0..max_size].to_vec();` followed by `frame.offset += ...`. All subsequent uses are read-only: `able_to_send.len()` (== `max_size`), `able_to_send.as_ptr()` at 1912, `writer.write_all(&able_to_send)` at 1922. The only reason a Vec is taken is that `self.get_padding(..)` at 1875 takes `&self`, conflicting with the `&mut self.data_frame_queue` borrow held via `frame`. Compare line 1930 `let frame_slice: &[u8] = frame.slice();` which avoids the copy because the frame was dequeued into a local.
findings.proposed_fix: Reshape the borrows instead of cloning: hoist `let padding = self.get_padding(max_size, max_size - 1);` and `let stream_id = self.id;` above `peek_front()`, then keep `able_to_send` as a borrowed `&frame.buffer[frame.offset as usize..frame.offset as usize + max_size]` and defer `frame.offset += max_size as u32` until after the write. Replace every `able_to_send.len()` with `max_size`. Disjoint field writes (`self.remote_used_window_size += ..`) coexist with the `self.data_frame_queue` borrow under NLL; only the method call needed hoisting.

findings.title: to_w_path_normalized does redundant pooled-buffer get + slash scan/copy; to_w_path already normalizes slashes
findings.problem: On every call, `to_w_path_normalized` (1) pulls a pooled `PathBuffer` via `path_buffer_pool::get()`, (2) runs `normalize_slashes_only` which SIMD-scans for '/' and, if found, copies the whole path + byte-loops to replace '/'→'\\', then (3) calls `to_w_path` → `to_w_path_maybe_dir`, which unconditionally runs `dangerously_convert_path_to_windows_in_place` over the converted u16 output (line 401) — a second full scan-and-replace of the same separators. The u8-space normalization and the pooled buffer are pure overhead: the u16-space normalization in `to_w_path_maybe_dir` already guarantees the output has only '\\'. The trailing-slash trim at line 221 uses `is_sep_any` (handles both '/' and '\\'), so it does not need the input pre-normalized either.
findings.evidence: string_paths.rs:208 `let mut renormalized = crate::path_buffer_pool::get();` ... :218 `let mut path_to_use = normalize_slashes_only(&mut renormalized[..], utf8, b'\\');` ... :225 `to_w_path(wbuf, path_to_use)`. Then in `to_w_path_maybe_dir` at :401: `resolve_path::dangerously_convert_path_to_windows_in_place::<u16>(&mut wbuf[..count]);`. Callers: `to_nt_path` (string_paths.rs:98,104,118,125) which is called from `bun_sys` for every Windows syscall path conversion (src/sys/lib.rs:4018,4037,4038,4059,7476), plus install/resolver paths.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the pooled buffer and `normalize_slashes_only` entirely. Do the length bound check against `MAX_PATH_BYTES` (const) instead of `renormalized.len()`. Compute the trailing-slash-trimmed slice directly from `utf8` using `is_sep_any(utf8[utf8.len()-1])` (already separator-agnostic), then pass that to `to_w_path`. `to_w_path_maybe_dir`'s existing in-place normalization handles '/'→'\\'. Net savings per call: TLS pool get+put, one SIMD scan, and (when input has '/') a full path copy + byte-loop.

findings.title: join_sep_vec starts with Vec::new() and grows via extend_from_slice in loop (no capacity reservation)
findings.problem: `join_sep_vec` builds the joined path by starting at `Vec::new()` (capacity 0) and calling `out.extend_from_slice(this)` once per non-empty part, plus `out.push(SEP)` between parts. For two parts `[dir_path, entry_name]` where `dir_path` is long (common in glob walks over deep trees), this incurs multiple reallocation+copy cycles as the Vec grows 0→8→16→...→N. The total length is trivially precomputable from `parts`.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:238 `let mut out: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` then loop at :240-257 with `out.push(SEP)` / `out.extend_from_slice(this)`. Sole caller: `join_sep_maybe_z` (lib.rs:265), called from `GlobWalker::join` (src/glob/GlobWalker.rs:2009) once per matched glob entry when relative paths are enabled — potentially tens of thousands of calls for `**/*.js`-style patterns.
findings.proposed_fix: Precompute `cap = parts.iter().map(|p| p.len()).sum::<usize>() + parts.len()` and use `Vec::with_capacity(cap)` (or `cap + 1` if SENTINEL). This collapses all reallocations into one allocation of the exact final size.

findings.title: Per-module heap reallocation+copy of the entire symbol table on every print
findings.problem: `print_with_source_map_maybe` (the hot per-import print path used by `bun run` via `RuntimeTranspilerStore.rs:1152`, `jsc_hooks.rs:3031`, `AsyncModule.rs:1305`) converts `ast.symbols: ArenaVec<'a, Symbol>` into a fresh global-heap `Vec<Symbol>` via `.into_iter().collect()`, then boxes that into `vec![list]` inside `symbol::Map::init_with_one_list`. `Symbol` is 48 bytes (`symbol.rs:211` asserts `size_of::<Symbol>() <= 48`), so a file with 1000 symbols pays ~48 KB of heap alloc + elementwise move on every print. The common (non-minify) consumer `NoOpRenamer` only ever calls `get_const()` / `follow()` on the map — purely read-only — so the copy is unnecessary work for the dominant `bun run` case.
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:2348-2352: `let symbols = bun_ast::symbol::Map::init_with_one_list(core::mem::replace(&mut ast.symbols, bun_alloc::ArenaVec::new_in(arena)).into_iter().collect());`. `ast/symbol.rs:620` boxes again: `Self::init_list(vec![list])`. `js_printer/renamer.rs:98-108` (`NoOpRenamer.symbols`) explains the Map is owned solely so it can be dropped, and `js_printer/lib.rs:8013` shows the non-minify path only passes it to `NoOpRenamer::init`.
findings.proposed_fix: Allow `symbol::Map` (or the renamer) to borrow a single `&'a [Symbol]` / `&'a mut [Symbol]` for the one-source case instead of requiring `Vec<Vec<Symbol>>`. For the non-minify path, `NoOpRenamer` can hold `&'a [Symbol]` directly (it never mutates); for the minify path, the few `set_must_not_be_renamed(true)` writes (lib.rs:7961-7968) are in-place and could operate on the original `ast.symbols.as_mut_slice()`. This removes one global-heap alloc + O(symbols) copy per transpiled module.

findings.title: Signature::generate stores an unused owned copy of the full SQL text
findings.problem: `Signature { query: Box::<[u8]>::from(query), .. }` heap-allocates and memcpys the entire SQL string every time a query is run, yet a repo-wide grep shows `signature.query` is never read anywhere (only the `name`, `fields`, and `prepared_statement_name` fields are used). The same dead copy exists in the MySQL variant at src/sql_jsc/mysql/protocol/Signature.rs:83. Additionally `name` (line 41-43) already begins with the full query bytes, so even if the text were needed it is redundantly stored twice. Signature::generate runs on every prepared-query execution (PostgresSQLQuery.rs:584, MySQLQuery.rs:310) including cache hits where the whole Signature is immediately dropped (PostgresSQLQuery.rs:633, MySQLQuery.rs:357).
findings.evidence: postgres/Signature.rs:116 `query: Box::<[u8]>::from(query)` and mysql/protocol/Signature.rs:83 same. `grep -rn 'signature\.query' src/sql_jsc/ src/sql/` returns no hits; the field is write-only.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the `query: Box<[u8]>` field from both Signature structs (or set it to `Box::default()`), eliminating one heap allocation + full-SQL memcpy per query execution. Longer term, restructure so cache lookups compute only the `name` key and build `fields`/`prepared_statement_name` only on a miss.

findings.title: Postgres DataRow re-zeroes cells that were just default-initialized
findings.problem: On every DataRow the handler creates `let mut stack_buf = [SQLDataCell::default(); 70]` (line 2430) or collects a heap Vec of defaults (2435-2437), then immediately runs `for c in cells.iter_mut() { *c = SQLDataCell::default(); }` (2444-2446). Both sources are already freshly default-initialized, so the loop is pure redundant work per row. When the heap path is taken (>= max_inline columns) the 70-slot stack buffer is also still initialized even though it goes unused, and the heap Vec is allocated/freed per row.
findings.evidence: PostgresSQLConnection.rs:2430 `let mut stack_buf = [DataCell::SQLDataCell::default(); 70];` ... 2435-2437 `heap_cells = (0..statement.fields.len()).map(|_| DataCell::SQLDataCell::default()).collect();` ... 2444-2446 `for c in cells.iter_mut() { *c = DataCell::SQLDataCell::default(); }` with the comment 'make sure all cells are reset' — but both buffers are brand-new defaults.
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the redundant reset loop at 2444-2446. For the heap branch, hoist the Vec<SQLDataCell> to the statement (re-use across rows of the same result set, `clear()` + resize once) so wide result sets stop allocating per row; or at minimum build `stack_buf` only in the stack branch so the wide-column path doesn't pay for both.

findings.title: Linear scan over ~138 builtin aliases when a prebuilt HashMap already exists
findings.problem: `bun_aliases_get()` iterates every entry in `BUN_ALIASES` (COMMON_ALIAS_KVS ~122 + BUN_EXTRA_ALIAS_KVS ~16 entries) and does a byte-slice compare for each, on every call. For a non-builtin specifier (the common case in `require.resolve.paths` / `Module._resolveLookupPaths`), this is ~138 comparisons. `bun_resolve_builtins` already lazily builds `BUN_ALIAS_MAP: HashMap<&[u8], Alias>` from the same tables (HardcodedModule.rs:861) and exposes `Alias::get(name, Target::Bun, Cfg)` which uses it (line 884). The linear scan is called per user-level `require.resolve.paths(...)`, `Module._resolveLookupPaths(...)` (NodeModuleModule.cpp:507, JSCommonJSModule.cpp:313) and `process.getBuiltinModule(...)` (BunProcess.cpp:4356).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the nested loop with `bun_resolve_builtins::Alias::get(name, bun_ast::Target::Bun, Default::default())` (which already applies the same `stream_iter_alias_gated` guard and looks up `BUN_ALIAS_MAP`), or make `BUN_ALIAS_MAP` `pub` and call `.get(name).copied()` directly. O(n) → O(1) per call; the comment at line 454 already notes this as a known PERF TODO.

findings.title: tick_kqueue allocates a fresh Vec<KEvent> on every IO-thread wakeup
findings.problem: In the macOS/FreeBSD IO-thread event loop `tick_kqueue`, `let mut events_list: Vec<EventType> = Vec::with_capacity(256);` is declared inside the infinite `loop {}` (line 996). Each iteration corresponds to one kevent() wakeup, so every wakeup heap-allocates (and frees at scope end) at least 256 × sizeof(kevent64_s) ≈ 12 KB, plus an additional `events_list.reserve(pending.batch.count)` on top. The sibling `tick_epoll` (line 920) instead uses a fixed-size stack array `[EventType; 256]` with zero heap traffic. The Vec could be hoisted above the loop and `clear()`ed at the start of each iteration to reuse the allocation.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the `events_list` declaration above `loop {` in `tick_kqueue`, then at the top of each iteration call `events_list.clear();` before `reserve(pending.batch.count)`. The Vec's capacity then grows to the high-water mark once and is reused. (Note `set_len(rc_len)` at line 1087 already writes over prior contents, so no re-zeroing is needed; `clear()` just resets len so `add_one`'s `push` starts from 0.)

findings.title: O(events × watchlist) linear wd→index scan per inotify event
findings.problem: Inside the per-event loop (`while events_processed < events.len()` at line 418, up to 128 iterations per batch), each event resolves its watchlist index via `eventlist_index.iter().position(|&x| x == event.watch_descriptor)` — a full linear scan of the watchlist-length snapshot. With N watched files and E events per cycle this is O(E×N). The snapshot Vec is already materialized once at line 403, so building a `HashMap<c_int, WatchItemIndex>` (or sorting + binary search) once per cycle would make each lookup O(1)/O(log N).
findings.proposed_fix: After taking the locked snapshot at line 403, also populate a reusable `HashMap<EventListIndex, WatchItemIndex>` (stored on `INotifyWatcher` and `.clear()`ed each cycle to avoid per-cycle allocation). Replace the `.iter().position()` with a single `map.get(&event.watch_descriptor)` lookup.

findings.title: index_of / parent lookup are O(n) → O(n²) total when registering N files
findings.problem: `index_of` linearly scans `watchlist.items_hash()`. It is called from `add_file` (line 876), `add_directory` (line 779), `remove` (line 911) and `add_file_by_path_slow` (lines 808, 842). Additionally `append_file_maybe_lock` does two more linear scans at lines 670 (`fds.iter().position(..)`) and 677 (`hashes.iter().position(..)`) to find the parent dir. Every module added to the watcher therefore costs up to 3×O(n) scans, so registering N modules at startup/rebuild is O(N²) hash-compares. No hash→index map is maintained despite hashes already being stored.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `HashMap<HashType, WatchItemIndex>` (and optionally `HashMap<Fd, WatchItemIndex>` for the dir_fd lookup) to `Watcher`, updated in `append_*_assume_capacity` and `flush_evictions`/`swap_remove`. `index_of` and the two parent-lookup scans become O(1).

findings.title: O(n^2) Vec::remove(0) inside inner loop of diff_cleanup_semantic_lossless
findings.problem: Inside diff_cleanup_semantic_lossless, the character-by-character right-shift loop calls `edit.remove(0)` and `equality_2.remove(0)` (lines 1214, 1217) on Vec<Unit> every iteration. Each remove(0) memmoves the entire remaining buffer, so for a segment of length k the loop does ~k removes at O(k) each = O(k^2). This sits inside an outer `while pointer < diffs.len()-1` loop over all diff hunks, so a large failing snapshot / toEqual diff with long equal-surrounded edits degrades quadratically.
