Additionally, the outer loop in diff_cleanup_merge (lines 926/936/956/991/999) does `diffs.insert(pointer, ..)` / `diffs.remove(pointer)` on the DiffList Vec, which is O(n) per operation inside an O(n) traversal — also O(n^2) on number of diff hunks.
findings.evidence: diff_match_patch.rs:1211-1234: `while !edit.is_empty() && !equality_2.is_empty() && edit[0] == equality_2[0] { equality_1.push(edit[0]); edit.remove(0); edit.push(equality_2[0]); equality_2.remove(0); ... }` — edit and equality_2 are `Vec<Unit>` built from owned clones at lines 1175-1182. The surrounding function iterates over every equal-delimited edit in the diff list.
findings.proposed_fix: Track sliding indices instead of mutating the Vecs: keep `let mut off = 0;` and compare `edit[off] == equality_2[off]`, then slice `&edit[off..]` / `&equality_2[off..]` when scoring and when snapshotting best_*. This avoids all remove(0) calls and turns the inner loop into O(k). (A VecDeque would also work but the index approach avoids the three upfront clones too.) For diff_cleanup_merge, building into a new Vec and swapping at the end would remove the O(n) insert/remove inside the traversal.

findings.title: http2 request()/respond() iterate headers 2-3x with redundant Object.keys() + toLowerCase()
findings.problem: On every HTTP/2 request and response, the same `headers` object is walked multiple times back-to-back, each walk allocating a fresh `Object.keys(headers)` array and calling `key.toLowerCase()` on every header name. `buildSensitiveNames()` (L3883-3885) does one Object.keys + toLowerCase-per-key. `assertSingleValueHeaders()` (L3850-3852) does another Object.keys + toLowerCase-per-key. The client `request()` path additionally does a third `ObjectKeys(headers)` loop at L5956. These are invoked consecutively on the same object (L3495+3498, L3606+3609, L5950+5956+5973). For a request with ~15 headers this is 2-3 throwaway key arrays and ~30 throwaway lowercased strings per request, purely for validation passes that could share one iteration.
findings.evidence: function buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives) { ... const keys = Object.keys(headers); for (let i = 0; i < keys.length; i++) { const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase(); ... } }  /*3848*/ function assertSingleValueHeaders(headers) { let seen = null; const keys = Object.keys(headers); for (let i = 0; i < keys.length; i++) { const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase(); ... } }  /*respond() 3606-3609*/ const sensitiveNames = buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives); ... if (session[kStrictSingleValueFields] !== false) assertSingleValueHeaders(headers);  /*request() 5950-5973*/ const sensitiveNames = buildSensitiveNames(headers, sensitives); ... const headerNames = ObjectKeys(headers); for (...) {...} ... if (this[kStrictSingleValueFields] !== false) assertSingleValueHeaders(headers);
findings.proposed_fix: Fold the three passes into one: compute `const keys = ObjectKeys(headers)` once, and inside a single loop compute `const lower = keys[i].toLowerCase()` once, then do the sensitive-name check (authorization/cookie), the single-value-header check, and the token/pseudo-header validation together. Return both `sensitiveNames` and perform the throw from that one loop. This removes 1-2 `Object.keys` array allocations and ~n `toLowerCase()` string allocations per request/response. (File already has `//TODO: do this in C++` at L3838, but the JS-side duplication is independently avoidable.)

findings.title: Windows process.env set trap does O(n) scan with n toUpperCase() allocations per assignment
findings.problem: On Windows, every `process.env.X = v` goes through the Proxy `set` trap, which does `!envMapList.includes(p) && !envMapList.some(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)`. The `.some` callback allocates a fresh uppercased string for every existing env-var name on every write. With ~100 env vars, each assignment costs ~100 `toUpperCase()` allocations + two linear scans. `deleteProperty` (L523) repeats the same `findIndex(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)` pattern. Tools that bulk-set env vars (dotenv, test runners, npm lifecycle scripts, cross-env, Next.js) hit this per variable, giving O(n²) total with O(n²) string allocations.
findings.evidence: set(_, p, value) { const k = String(p).toUpperCase(); ... if (!envMapList.includes(p) && !envMapList.some(x => x.toUpperCase() === k)) { envMapList.push(p); } ... }  /*L522*/ deleteProperty(_, p) { const k = String(p).toUpperCase(); const i = envMapList.findIndex(x => x.toUpperCase() === k); if (i !== -1) { envMapList.splice(i, 1); } ... }
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a parallel `Set<string>` (or `Map<upperKey, index>`) of uppercased names alongside `envMapList`. `set`/`defineProperty` become `if (!upperSet.has(k)) { envMapList.push(p); upperSet.add(k); }`; `deleteProperty` becomes `upperSet.delete(k)` plus the list splice keyed by the stored index (or a single linear scan without per-entry allocation). This makes each env write O(1) with zero per-entry string allocation.

findings.title: require.cache Proxy ownKeys() is O(cjs × esm) via array.includes in loop
findings.problem: The `ownKeys` trap for `require.cache` builds `array = [...$requireMap.$keys()]` then iterates every evaluated ESM key doing `if (!array.includes(key))`. That is a linear scan of all CJS keys for every ESM key: O(cjs × esm). In apps with thousands of modules (common for bundler/test-runner workloads), a single `Object.keys(require.cache)` / `for..in require.cache` / `{...require.cache}` costs millions of string comparisons. Tools that clear or inspect the require cache on each reload (Mocha watch, Jest-like isolators, hot-reloaders) call this repeatedly.
findings.evidence: ownKeys(_target) { var array = [...$requireMap.$keys()]; for (const key of $esmRegistryEvaluatedKeys()) { if (!array.includes(key)) { $arrayPush(array, key); } } return array; },
findings.proposed_fix: Build a `Set` from the CJS keys first and use `set.has(key)` inside the loop (O(n+m)), or dedupe via `new Set([...$requireMap.$keys(), ...$esmRegistryEvaluatedKeys()])` while preserving insertion order, then spread back to an array.

findings.title: fs.watchFile clones the path on the success path but only uses it on error
findings.problem: `watch_file` does `let path: Vec<u8> = args.path.slice().to_vec();` before calling `args.create_stat_watcher()`, but `path` is only read inside the `Err` arm (lines 8166-8185) to build the error message. Every successful watchFile call pays an unnecessary heap allocation+copy of the path.
findings.evidence: node_fs.rs:8162 `let path: Vec<u8> = args.path.slice().to_vec();` followed by `match args.create_stat_watcher() { Ok(watcher) => Ok(watcher), Err(err) => { ... uses `path` ... } }`. The clone exists because `create_stat_watcher()` consumes `args`, but it is wasted on the `Ok` path.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep the eager clone only if `create_stat_watcher` truly must consume `args` before error handling; otherwise restructure so the path bytes are captured from the watcher/error itself. Simplest correctness-preserving fix: wrap in `let path = (err case needs it);` — i.e. take a borrow-safe `Box<[u8]>` from `args.path` via `into_owned`/`mem::take` instead of copying, or defer the `to_vec()` into the `Err` arm by having `create_stat_watcher` return the path back on failure. Low impact (watchFile is not per-tick hot) but trivially avoidable allocation.

findings.title: zig_delete_tree allocates a fresh Vec<u8> per directory entry (including plain files)
findings.problem: Inside the per-entry loop of `zig_delete_tree` (the engine behind `fs.rm`/`fs.rmdir` recursive), `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` heap-allocates for every entry. For the common case (non-directory file) the Vec is only passed by reference to `dt_delete_file` and then dropped — a pure per-file heap alloc. The same pattern appears at line 10121 in `zig_delete_tree_min_stack_size_with_kind_hint`. Deleting a tree with 100k files does 100k small allocations.
findings.evidence: Line 9930: `let entry_name: Vec<u8> = entry.name.slice().to_vec();` inside `loop { ... stack[top_idx].iter.next() ... }`. Only the `treat_as_dir` branch (line 9938) actually needs an owned Vec (it moves it into `DeleteTreeStackItem`); the file branch at 9965 does `dt_delete_file(..., &entry_name)` then drops it.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a reusable `let mut entry_name_buf: Vec<u8> = Vec::with_capacity(256);` outside the per-entry loop; in each iteration `entry_name_buf.clear(); entry_name_buf.extend_from_slice(entry.name.slice());`. For the directory push, move it with `std::mem::take(&mut entry_name_buf)` (next iteration re-grows). This turns per-file alloc into per-directory alloc, cutting allocations by ~90%+ for typical trees. Apply the same at line 10121.

findings.title: FdVectorIo::to_thread_safe clones an already-owned Vec<PlatformIoVec> in place
findings.problem: `pub fn to_thread_safe(&mut self) { self.buffers.value.protect(); self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec(); }` — `self.buffers.buffers` is already a heap-owned `Vec<PlatformIoVec>` (see `VectorArrayBuffer { pub buffers: Vec<PlatformIoVec>, .. }` at types.rs:1440, populated via `out.buffers.push(...)`). Re-assigning it to `.as_slice().to_vec()` allocates a brand-new Vec, memcpy's all iovecs, and drops the old one for no semantic effect. This runs once per async `fs.readv`/`fs.writev` call (via `into_thread_safe` at lines 711/1296).
findings.evidence: types.rs:1440 defines `buffers: Vec<PlatformIoVec>`; node_fs.rs:2841 does `self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec();`. No arena/borrowed-storage is involved — the Vec is created with `Vec::new()` in `VectorArrayBuffer::from_js` (types.rs:1518) and populated by `push`. Likely a vestigial port of Zig's `toOwnedSlice()` on a stack-backed ArrayList.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the line `self.buffers.buffers = self.buffers.buffers.as_slice().to_vec();`. If exact-capacity was the intent, use `self.buffers.buffers.shrink_to_fit()` instead (still avoids the copy when cap==len).

findings.title: S3 listObjects allocates a Vec for every ETag even when no unescaping is needed
findings.problem: For each `<Contents>` entry (up to 1000 per listObjects page), the ETag path calls `strings::replace_owned(input, b""", b"\"")`. `replace_owned` unconditionally calls `replacement_size` (full scan) and `Vec::with_capacity` + a second full scan/copy (/workspace/bun/src/bun_core/lib.rs:1305-1318). Line 301 then checks `output.len() != input.len()` to detect the no-op case and throws the allocation away. Real S3 responses normally emit ETags as `"hex"`, so the branch usually keeps the alloc — but for any entry without `"` (e.g. MinIO / R2 responses or already-unquoted ETags) this is a wasted allocation + two scans per object.
findings.evidence: list_objects.rs:299-306 `let output = strings::replace_owned(input, b""", b"\""); if output.len() != input.len() { etag_owned = Some(output); ... } else { etag = Some(input); }`. replace_owned at lib.rs:1309 always does `Vec::with_capacity(replacement_size(...))` regardless of whether `needle` occurs.
findings.proposed_fix: Gate on `strings::index_of(input, b""")` (single SIMD scan) and only call `replace_owned` when found; otherwise take the borrowed `etag = Some(input)` path directly. Alternatively, give `replace_owned` an early-return `input.to_vec()`/Cow::Borrowed when the first `find` misses, but the call-site fix avoids even the one allocation.

findings.title: Glob/brace results are flattened into a single buffer then re-split into per-arg Vecs
findings.problem: Glob/brace expansion produces one argv word per match. The pipeline is: (a) `ShellGlobTask` yields `Vec<Vec<u8>>`; (b) `Expansion::on_glob_walk_done` concatenates every entry into one contiguous `out.buf` and records offsets in `out.bounds` (Expansion.rs:696-701, same shape for brace at 342-347); (c) `Cmd::child_done` walks `out.bounds` and does `me.args.push(out.buf[prev..b].to_vec())` for each word. For N matches totalling M bytes this is N extra allocations and 2M extra bytes of memcpy (flatten + re-split) beyond the N allocations already paid in step (a). This is per-glob, so `rm **/*.ts` or `ls src/**` in a large tree pays it for thousands of entries.
findings.evidence: Expansion.rs:696-701: `for entry in result { ... me.out.buf.extend_from_slice(&entry); }` (flatten). Cmd.rs:404-411: `for &b in &out.bounds { me.args.push(out.buf[prev..b as usize].to_vec()); prev = b as usize; } me.args.push(out.buf[prev..].to_vec());` (re-split).
findings.proposed_fix: Carry multi-word expansion results as `Vec<Vec<u8>>` on `ExpansionOut` (or add an `enum { Single(Vec<u8>), Many(Vec<Vec<u8>>) }`) and in `Cmd::child_done` move the inner Vecs directly into `me.args` (`me.args.extend(words.into_iter())`). The flat `buf`+`bounds` encoding is only useful when the consumer wants a contiguous slice; argv explicitly wants separate allocations.

findings.title: `which` re-copies $PATH and cwd for every argument
findings.problem: `Which::next` is invoked once per argv operand (via `arg_complete` -> `next`), and each call does `Self::path_and_cwd(interp, cmd)` which performs an env-map lookup plus `s.slice().to_vec()` on `$PATH` and `shell.cwd().to_vec()` (which.rs:196-208). `$PATH` is commonly hundreds of bytes to a few KB; for `which a b c ... (N args)` this is N redundant heap allocations and copies of the same immutable strings. `Self::arg` (line 211-213) also does `arg_bytes(idx).to_vec()` even though `arg_bytes` returns a lifetime-decoupled `&'a [u8]` that is valid across interp reborrows (see Builtin.rs:475-488).
findings.evidence: which.rs:89-105: `next` is the per-arg driver and calls `path_and_cwd` on every entry. which.rs:196-209: `fn path_and_cwd(...) -> (Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>) { ... .get(EnvStr::init_slice(b"PATH")).map(|s| { let v = s.slice().to_vec(); s.deref(); v }) ...; (path, shell.cwd().to_vec()) }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Fetch PATH/cwd once in `start()` and store on the `Which` state (the MultiArgs variant) as `Vec<u8>`; reuse across the per-arg loop. Also drop the `to_vec()` in `Self::arg` and pass the `&[u8]` from `arg_bytes` straight through to `which()`/`format_args!`.

findings.title: is_interpolated_position() linearly scans js_string_ranges on every lookup
findings.problem: `is_interpolated_position` iterates all `js_string_ranges` with `.iter().any(...)`. It is called from `parse_assign` (for every Text token containing `=`) and from `if_clause_tok_at` (for every text token peeked inside an if-body). For a tagged-template shell script with N `${...}` interpolations and M text tokens this is O(M·N). The ranges are pushed in strictly increasing, non-overlapping order (line 3468 pushes `{start: self.j, end: self.j'}` and `self.j` is monotone), so the slice is already sorted.
findings.evidence:
parse.rs:1909-1913:
    fn is_interpolated_position(&self, pos: u32) -> bool {
        self.js_string_ranges
            .iter()
            .any(|r| pos >= r.start && pos < r.end)
    }
Ranges are appended only at parse.rs:3468 with monotonically increasing `self.j`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the linear scan with `partition_point`/`binary_search_by` on `r.start`, then test `pos < ranges[idx-1].end`. O(log N) per lookup with no allocation; 3-4 lines of code.

findings.title: braces::Parser::advance/prev() deep-clones every Token, heap-allocating long text segments
findings.problem: `prev()` returns `self.tokens[self.current - 1].clone()` and `advance()` returns `prev()` (or `peek().clone()`). `Token::clone()` for `Text(s)` calls `SmolStr::from_slice(s.slice())` (line 457), which allocates a fresh heap Vec whenever the segment is >15 bytes. `advance()` is invoked once per token in `parse_atom` and again (with the result discarded) inside `r#match`/`match_any`. So every long text atom in a nested brace expansion (`{src/long/path/a,src/long/path/b}`) triggers an extra heap allocation whose bytes already live in `self.tokens`.
findings.evidence:
braces.rs:1068-1069:
    fn prev(&self) -> Token {
        self.tokens[self.current - 1].clone()
    }
braces.rs:451-458 (Token::clone):
    Token::Text(s) => Token::Text(SmolStr::from_slice(s.slice()).expect("OOM cloning SmolStr"))
SmolStr::from_slice (bun_core/string/SmolStr.rs:139) allocates a Vec when len > 15.
findings.proposed_fix: Have the brace parser take `&'a mut [Token]` (its sole caller `expand()` already owns `&mut [Token]`) and in `parse_atom` do `core::mem::replace(&mut self.tokens[idx], Token::Eof)` to move the Text out instead of cloning. `r#match`/`match_any` should just bump `self.current` without constructing the returned Token at all.

findings.title: Archive glob filter keeps running glob::match on positive patterns after one already matched
findings.problem: match_glob_patterns is invoked for every archive entry during .files()/extract (lines 1191 and 1494). Inside the loop over patterns, once a positive pattern matches it sets `matches_positive = true` but continues to evaluate `glob::r#match(pattern, pathname)` for every remaining positive pattern. Negative patterns must still be checked, but re-running the matcher on further positive patterns is pure wasted work. For an archive with E entries and P positive patterns this is O(E*P) glob matches instead of O(E*(1 avg positive + negatives)).
findings.evidence: 1408-1423: `for pattern in patterns { ... } else { has_positive_patterns = true; if glob::r#match(pattern, pathname).matches() { matches_positive = true; } }` — no `!matches_positive &&` guard, no `continue`. Called per-entry at 1191 and 1494.
findings.proposed_fix: Guard the positive branch: `if !matches_positive && glob::r#match(pattern, pathname).matches() { matches_positive = true; }` (still iterate the rest so negative `!` patterns are honored). Trivial change, preserves semantics.

findings.title: Buf::append does byte-by-byte copy+branch for separator normalization with no memcpy fast-path
findings.problem: For `SEP_OPT` ∈ {Auto, Posix, Windows} (i.e. every `AutoAbsPath`/`AutoRelPath`), `Buf::append` loops `for &c in characters` writing one unit at a time with `if c.eq_ascii(b'/') || c.eq_ascii(b'\\')` per byte. On POSIX with `Auto` the target separator is '/', so a typical POSIX path segment containing only '/' separators gets every '/' "replaced" with '/' and every non-sep byte copied individually — identical output to a `copy_from_slice`, but with two branches per byte instead of memcpy. The codebase's own `normalize_slashes_only` (string_paths.rs:298) already demonstrates the correct pattern: SIMD `contains_char_t` for the undesired separator, then memcpy when absent.
findings.evidence: Path.rs:398-407: `PathSeparators::Auto | PathSeparators::Posix | PathSeparators::Windows => { for &c in characters { buf[self.len] = if c.eq_ascii(b'/') || c.eq_ascii(b'\\') { U::from_u8(PathSeparators::from_u8(SEP_OPT).char()) } else { c }; self.len += 1; } }`. Hot callers: `Path::append` → `_buf_append_input` → `Buf::append`, used per-file in install hardlink/copy loops (src/install/isolated_install/Hardlinker.rs:109,112 and FileCopier.rs:116,119 inside directory-entry iteration) and many per-package path builds in Installer.rs.
findings.proposed_fix: Before the byte loop, compute the single `undesired_slash` for the current `SEP_OPT` (e.g. Auto/Posix→'\\', Windows→'/'; the target separator never needs replacing). If `strings::contains_char_t(characters, undesired_slash)` is false, do `buf[self.len..][..characters.len()].copy_from_slice(characters); self.len += characters.len();` and return. Fall through to the existing loop only when replacement is actually needed. This turns the common case (input already has the right separator) from O(n) branchy byte writes into a single memcpy.

findings.title: Log::clone_to_with_recycled deep-clones every message then immediately overwrites the clones
findings.problem: When `recycled == true` (the only value any call site passes — parser.rs, bundler/cache.rs, ParseTask.rs, bundle_v2.rs, RuntimeTranspilerStore.rs all pass `true`), the function first does `other.msgs.extend(self.msgs.iter().map(Msg::clone))` which performs a full deep clone of every message (each `Msg::clone` allocates a notes Vec and calls `Data::clone` → `text.to_vec()` + `Location::clone` → `file.to_vec()` + `line_text.to_vec()`). Then lines 1819-1824 loop again and write `other.msgs[j] = msg.clone_with_builder(...)`, discarding every allocation from the first pass. All the work and allocations of the first `Msg::clone` loop are thrown away.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1799-1825: `other.msgs.extend(self.msgs.iter().map(Msg::clone));` … `if recycled { … for (k, msg) in self.msgs.iter().enumerate() { other.msgs[j] = msg.clone_with_builder(…); } }`. `Msg::clone` at lib.rs:1233 and `Location::clone` at lib.rs:743 both `to_vec()` their byte buffers.
findings.proposed_fix: When `recycled` is true, skip the upfront `extend(map(Msg::clone))` and instead reserve `other.msgs` capacity and push the `clone_with_builder` result directly (or `other.msgs.resize_with(dest_start + self.msgs.len(), Msg::default)` before overwriting). This halves the per-diagnostic allocation count.

findings.title: default_number_to_minified_name allocates a fresh Vec per cross-chunk export
findings.problem: `NameMinifier::default_number_to_minified_name` does `let mut name: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` and returns it. Its only caller, `ExportRenamer::next_minified_name` (src/js_printer/renamer.rs:1101), is invoked inside `for stable_ref in stable_ref_list` in `computeCrossChunkDependencies.rs:470-486` — once per exported symbol per chunk when `minify_identifiers` is on. Each call makes a tiny heap allocation that is immediately copied into the arena (`c.arena().alloc_slice_copy(&r.next_minified_name()…)`) and dropped. The non-default sibling `number_to_minified_name` (nodes.rs:662) already takes a caller-reused `&mut Vec<u8>`; the ExportRenamer path does not use it.
findings.evidence: nodes.rs:682-699 allocates `Vec::new()` per call; renamer.rs:1101 wraps it; computeCrossChunkDependencies.rs:486 calls it inside a per-export loop and copies the result into an arena.
findings.proposed_fix: Give `ExportRenamer` a reusable `Vec<u8>` field and call `NameMinifier::number_to_minified_name(&mut self.name_buf, ..)` with `NameMinifier::DEFAULT_*` constants (or change `default_number_to_minified_name` to take `&mut Vec<u8>`), then `alloc_slice_copy` from the buffer. Same pattern `assign_names_by_frequency` (renamer.rs:437) already uses.

findings.title: Double heap allocation per JSON/TOML/YAML top-level key in `parse_data_loader`
findings.problem: Inside the per-property loop (`for i in 0..n` at line 1978) over a JSON/TOML/YAML object's top-level keys, each key goes through `bun_core::MutableString::ensure_valid_identifier(name)` which **unconditionally** returns a fresh heap `Box<[u8]>` even when the input is already a valid ASCII identifier (`MutableString.rs:186-188` does `Box::<[u8]>::from(remapped)` on the fast path). That Box is then immediately copied into the arena via `arena.alloc_slice_copy(&boxed)` and dropped. So every key pays one heap alloc+free plus one redundant arena copy. JSON keys are almost always already valid identifiers, and `name` itself is already arena-backed (from `.slice(arena)` at line 1988) and is used directly at line 2037 (`alias: bun_ast::StoreStr::new(name)`).
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:2010-2020 vs the existing fast-path precedent in `js_printer/renamer.rs:871-917` (`is_simple_ascii_identifier` + reserved-word check is used precisely to skip `ensure_valid_identifier`'s unconditional Box allocation for the common case).
findings.proposed_fix: Before calling `ensure_valid_identifier`, test `is_simple_ascii_identifier(name) && !lexer_tables::is_strict_mode_reserved_word(name)` (same gate as `renamer.rs:909-917`); when true, use `bun_ast::StoreStr::new(name)` directly (zero allocs). Only fall back to `ensure_valid_identifier` + arena copy when mangling is actually required. This drops 2 allocations per key for typical JSON imports with many top-level keys.

findings.title: gregorian_date computes year by iterating from 1970 one year at a time
findings.problem: `gregorian_date(days)` loops `while d >= 365 + is_leap_year(y) { d -= ...; y += 1; }` starting at y=1970, so converting a timestamp near the MySQL DATETIME max (year 9999) does ~8000 iterations; a typical 2025 date does ~55. This is called from DateTime::from_unix_timestamp (line 626) for every Date/number bound as a MYSQL_TYPE_DATE/DATETIME/TIMESTAMP parameter (from_js at lines 671, 679). A closed-form civil-from-days calculation is O(1).
findings.evidence: MySQLValue.rs:839-844 `let mut y: u16 = 1970; while d >= 365 + is_leap_year(y) as i32 { d -= 365 + is_leap_year(y) as i32; y += 1; }` followed by a month loop. Each iteration also calls is_leap_year twice.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with the standard O(1) civil-date algorithm (e.g. Howard Hinnant's days_from_civil/civil_from_days), or reuse the same JSC helper used on the decode side (`gregorian_date_time_to_ms_utc` has a matching inverse in WTF). This removes the O(year-1970) loop per bound date parameter.

findings.title: Per-stack-frame heap String allocation just to probe whether NameFormatter emits
findings.problem: `print_stack_trace` (and the GitHub-Actions stack printer at line 6360) allocate a fresh `String`, run the full `NameFormatter` Display impl into it, and then only check `!probe.is_empty()` — per frame. A 50-frame error stack does 50 heap allocations + 50 redundant format passes solely to compute a boolean. Looking at `NameFormatter::fmt` (/workspace/bun/src/jsc/ZigStackFrame.rs:233-313), the formatter emits nothing only when `code_type == GLOBAL` or when the catch-all arm is hit with an empty `function_name`; every other arm writes at least one byte. That predicate is computable without any formatting.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `fn is_empty(&self) -> bool` (or `has_output`) on `NameFormatter`/`ZigStackFrame` that returns `self.code_type == ZigStackFrameCode::GLOBAL || (matches!(self.code_type, _other) && self.function_name.is_empty())` mirroring the Display arms, and call that instead at both sites (lines 5067-5072 and 6358-6363). Removes one allocation + one full format pass per frame.

findings.title: console.table: O(rows × columns²) column lookup via linear `.iter().position()`
findings.problem: `collect_row` is invoked once per table row (from the `for_each`/property-iterator loops at lines 1014/1046). Inside, for every property key of the row it does `columns[1..].iter().position(|col| col.name.eql(&col_str))` — a linear scan over all discovered columns with a WTF-string equality per comparison. For a table of R rows that share C distinct column names, this is R × C × O(C) string comparisons, i.e. O(R·C²). Large `console.table` inputs with many columns degrade quadratically.
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a `HashMap<u64 /*name hash*/, usize /*col idx*/>` (or `StringHashMap`) alongside `columns`, populated when a column is appended, and look up via the map instead of the linear `position()` scan. Brings the per-property lookup to O(1), making the whole collect pass O(R·C).

findings.title: Per-cycle heap allocation for eventlist_index snapshot
findings.problem: `this.watchlist.items_eventlist_index().to_vec()` allocates a fresh `Vec<c_int>` on every `watch_loop_cycle` invocation (i.e. every time inotify delivers events). The snapshot is needed for thread-safety (per the comment), but the buffer could be reused across cycles instead of allocated/freed each time. Under heavy watch load (e.g. the referenced `watch-many-dirs.test.ts` with >128 files) this is a hot-loop allocation.
findings.proposed_fix: Store a `Vec<EventListIndex>` (or the HashMap from finding #1) as a field on `INotifyWatcher`; inside the locked region do `buf.clear(); buf.extend_from_slice(this.watchlist.items_eventlist_index())`. Amortizes to zero allocations after the buffer reaches the watchlist high-water mark.

findings.title: Per-test × per-ancestor-hook individual Box::new heap allocations in generate_order_test
findings.problem: generate_order_test() runs once per test. For each test it walks the entire describe-parent chain twice (beforeEach, afterEach) and for every hook at every ancestor level does `bun_core::heap::into_raw(Box::new(unsafe { core::ptr::read(src) }))` — a separate global-allocator call per hook clone. For T tests nested D describes deep with H each-hooks per level this is T*D*H discrete Box allocations (plus T*D parent-chain walks). These clones are later collected into BunTest.cloned_hook_entries only so their Box headers can be freed, so a bump arena (already present: BunTest owns one and Order.rs is invoked from BunTest) could serve them with zero per-clone malloc and make cloned_hook_entries unnecessary.
findings.evidence: Order.rs:145-163 (beforeEach) and 170-183 (afterEach): `let cloned = bun_core::heap::into_raw(Box::new(unsafe { core::ptr::read(src) })); list.prepend(cloned);` inside `while let Some(p_ptr) = parent { ... for/while over p.before_each / p.after_each ... }` inside generate_order_test, which is called once per test entry from generate_order_sub (Order.rs:36). The ownership comment at 152-155 confirms these are bitwise copies whose Drop must never run — exactly the arena-friendly case.
findings.proposed_fix: Allocate the cloned ExecutionEntry copies from a bump arena owned by BunTest (e.g. a typed_arena or the existing bun_alloc::Arena) instead of individual Box::new, since they are never individually dropped (only the Box headers are reclaimed in Drop for BunTest). This turns T*D*H mallocs into a handful of arena page allocations per file and eliminates the cloned_hook_entries bookkeeping Vec. Alternatively, collect all ancestor hooks once per describe scope and reuse the slice across sibling tests instead of re-walking the parent chain per test.

findings.title: inotify IN_IGNORED handler does O(wds) linear scan per removed watch — O(N²) teardown for recursive watchers
findings.problem: `wds: Vec<i32>` (line 670) stores every inotify watch descriptor belonging to a recursive `fs.watch`. When a watched subtree is deleted, the kernel emits one IN_IGNORED per wd; for each, the handler does `w.platform.wds.iter().position(|&x| x == wd)` then `swap_remove(idx)`. With N subdirectories that is ΣO(k)=O(N²) i32 comparisons, all executed while holding `manager.mutex` (blocking every other watcher). For N=10000 dirs this is ~50M comparisons under lock.
findings.evidence: Line 670 `wds: Vec<i32>`; line 959 `if let Some(idx) = w.platform.wds.iter().position(|&x| x == wd) { w.platform.wds.swap_remove(idx); }` inside the `IN::IGNORED` branch of the per-event loop inside `thread_main`. `add_one` at line 790 pushes wds without bound for recursive watches.
findings.proposed_fix: Change `platform.wds` from `Vec<i32>` to a hash set (e.g. `bun_collections` int set / `AHashSet<i32>`): `add_one` does `wds.insert(wd)`, IN_IGNORED handler does `wds.remove(&wd)`, and `remove_watch` (line 821) iterates `wds.iter()`. This makes per-event removal O(1) and leaves `remove_watch` O(N).

findings.title: Header EntryList is cloned twice per fetch() (three live copies)
findings.problem: `FetchTasklet` owns `request_headers.entries` (the MultiArrayList of name/value StringPointer pairs). At line 2005 it clones this list to pass by value to `AsyncHTTP::init`, and `AsyncHTTP::init` then clones it again (/workspace/bun/src/http/AsyncHTTP.rs:477) so both `AsyncHTTP.request_headers` and `client.header_entries` get their own copy. Net: 3 heap copies of the same entry list per fetch(). The inline comment already calls this out ("AsyncHTTP::init clones again for the client"). Each entry is 16 bytes, so this is ~hundreds of bytes and two small allocations per request — not huge, but it is on the per-request hot path.
findings.evidence: FetchTasklet.rs:2003-2005 `// MultiArrayList owns its allocation, so clone; AsyncHTTP::init clones again for the client.  let header_entries = bun_core::handle_oom(fetch_tasklet.request_headers.entries.clone());` and AsyncHTTP.rs:475-490 `// the same headers value goes in both AsyncHTTP.request_headers and client.header_entries; MultiArrayList owns its allocation, so clone here.  headers.clone().expect("OOM"), ... request_headers: headers,`.
findings.proposed_fix: Eliminate one copy: either (a) have FetchTasklet stop keeping its own `entries` after init and read through `self.http.request_headers` in `skip_chunked_framing` (or, better, cache the two header lookups as a bool — see the first finding — at which point FetchTasklet no longer needs `request_headers.entries` at all and can `core::mem::take` it into `AsyncHTTP::init`), or (b) change `AsyncHTTP` so `client.header_entries` borrows/shares the list instead of owning a second clone.

findings.title: MatchedRoute.scriptSrc pre-allocates MAX_PATH_BYTES (≈96 KB on Windows) for a short URL string
findings.problem: get_script_src builds the public URL by writing into `String::with_capacity(MAX_PATH_BYTES)`. MAX_PATH_BYTES is 32767*3+1 ≈ 96 KB on Windows (4 KB on Linux, 1 KB on macOS). The resulting string is typically a few dozen bytes. A new MatchedRoute is created on every router.match() call (per request), so every request that reads `.scriptSrc`/`.src` triggers a 96 KB heap allocation on Windows that is immediately thrown away after `zs_to_js` copies the bytes into a JS string.
findings.evidence: 993: `let mut writer = String::with_capacity(MAX_PATH_BYTES);` with MAX_PATH_BYTES defined in src/bun_core/util.rs:736-744 as `32767 * 3 + 1` on Windows. The getter is `cache: true` in filesystem_router.classes.ts so it fires once per MatchedRoute, and each router.match() call constructs a fresh MatchedRoute (line 653).
findings.proposed_fix: Use a small initial capacity (e.g. `String::with_capacity(256)`) and let it grow in the rare long-path case, or write into a pooled `path_buffer_pool::get()` byte buffer via a `fmt::Write` adaptor and pass the used slice to `zs_to_js`.

findings.title: Rope::append walks the whole chain on every call; callers build ropes in O(n²)
findings.problem: `Rope::append` locates the tail by `while let Some(next) = …tail.next… { tail = next }` starting from `self`. `Rope` has no cached `end` pointer (unlike `EString`, which does at e.rs:1997). The INI parser builds ropes by repeatedly calling `rope_head.append(..)` in a loop (src/ini/lib.rs:1065-1079 `str_to_rope` and 1040 `commit_rope_part`), discarding `append`'s returned tail pointer each time. With up to `MAX_SECTION_ROPE_SEGMENTS = 512` segments that is ~130K pointer chases for an adversarial `.npmrc` section header — exactly the input the comment at e.rs:1262-1264 says the iterative rewrite was meant to harden against.
findings.evidence: e.rs:1269-1278 `let mut tail = StoreRef::from_bump(self); while let Some(next) = …tail.next… { tail = next; } … tail.next = rope; Ok(rope)`. ini/lib.rs:1077 `let _ = rope_head.append(Expr::init(...), ropealloc)?;` inside `while dot_idx + 1 < key.len() { … }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) add an `end: *mut Rope` field to `Rope` and update it in `append` (mirroring `EString::push` at e.rs:1995-2007), or (b) have callers thread the returned `*mut Rope` as the next `self` so each append is O(1). Option (b) needs no struct change.

findings.title: O(n·m) linear scan: `is_pending_import` called inside the import-records loop
findings.problem: `ParseResult::is_pending_import(id)` does a linear `.iter().any(|p| p.import_record_id == id)` over `pending_imports`. Its only caller, `Linker::link` (`/workspace/bun/src/bundler/linker.rs:424-428`), invokes it inside a loop over every `ast.import_records` entry when `IS_BUN && is_deferred`. That is O(import_records × pending_imports). When the AsyncModule deferred auto-install path populates `pending_imports` (which can approach the number of import records), this degrades toward quadratic per-module link work.
findings.evidence: transpiler.rs:946-952: `self.pending_imports.iter().any(|p| p.import_record_id == id)`; linker.rs:423-428: `let len = result.ast.import_records.as_slice().len(); for record_i in 0..len { ... let skip_deferred = IS_BUN && is_deferred && !result.is_pending_import(record_index); ... }`.
findings.proposed_fix: In `Linker::link`, when `is_deferred` is true, build a one-shot bitset (or `bun_collections` hash set) of `pending_imports[*].import_record_id` before the loop (O(m)), then do O(1) membership per record. Alternatively, sort `pending_imports` by `import_record_id` once and walk it in lockstep with the import-record loop.

findings.title: IPC SendQueue uses Vec with remove(0)/insert(0) on the per-message hot path
findings.problem: `SendQueue.queue` is a `Vec<SendHandle>` (line 830). `_on_write_complete` pops the head with `self.queue.remove(0)` on every completed write (lines 1270, 1305, 1310), and `insert_message` prepends with `self.queue.insert(0|1, ...)` (lines 1147/1151). Each is O(n) memmove of the remaining `SendHandle`s (each of which embeds a `StreamBuffer`, `Option<Handle>`, and `CallbackList`). When fd-carrying messages are queued (each forces a separate `SendHandle` and an ACK round-trip, defeating the last-slot coalescing in `start_message`), draining N queued messages costs O(N²) moves. `VecDeque<SendHandle>` would make `pop_front`/`push_front` O(1).
findings.evidence:
pub queue: Vec<SendHandle>,
...
let item = self.queue.remove(0);   // _on_write_complete, per completed write
...
self.queue.insert(0, message);      // insert_message, on NACK retry / ack reply
findings.proposed_fix: Change `queue: Vec<SendHandle>` to `VecDeque<SendHandle>`; replace `remove(0)` with `pop_front().unwrap()`, `insert(0, m)` with `push_front(m)`, index `[0]` with `.front()/.front_mut()`, `[len-1]` with `.back_mut()`, and `push` with `push_back`. `insert(1, m)` becomes `let f = q.pop_front().unwrap(); q.push_front(m); q.push_front(f);` (still O(1)).

findings.title: StreamBuffer::maybe_shrink reallocates on every full drain, causing grow/shrink thrashing for large writes
findings.problem: `StreamBuffer::maybe_shrink()` is invoked from `reset()` (line 1790) and from `PosixStreamingWriter::_on_write` (line 783) every time the outgoing buffer fully drains. If capacity exceeds one page (4 KB / 16 KB), it calls `truncate(page); shrink_to(page)`, which is a realloc + memcpy down to page size. A workload that repeatedly writes buffers larger than a page (e.g. 64 KB per `writer.write(...)`) therefore oscillates: extend_from_slice grows the Vec past page size, the write drains, `maybe_shrink` reallocs back down to page size, and the next write must grow again — two reallocs per large-write cycle with no hysteresis.
findings.proposed_fix: Add hysteresis: only shrink when `capacity > N * page` (e.g. N=8 or N=16) so typical write sizes stay within retained capacity, or track a high-water-mark and shrink only when the retained capacity has gone unused for several drains. Alternatively, skip `maybe_shrink()` in `_on_write` entirely and let `reset()` (called on EOF/error) handle reclamation, accepting a slightly larger steady-state footprint in exchange for eliminating per-write reallocs.

findings.title: Per-batch Vec allocation for deduped events (KEvent & Windows) — INotify already avoids it
findings.problem: `this.watch_events[0..out_len].to_vec()` heap-allocates a `Vec<WatchEvent>` on every kqueue cycle (and the same pattern at WindowsWatcher.rs:535). The comment says it's a borrowck reshape, but `process_inotify_event_batch` (INotifyWatcher.rs:538) demonstrates the allocation-free alternative: drop the `&mut` borrow, take the mutex, then re-slice `&mut this.watch_events[..n]` in place for the `on_file_update` call. The divergence means macOS/Windows allocate per event batch while Linux does not.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the INotify approach: end the existing `&mut this.watch_events` borrow (`let _ = watchevents;`), then after `this.mutex.lock_guard()` re-borrow `&mut this.watch_events[..out_len]` and pass that slice to `write_trace_events` / `on_file_update`. `write_trace_events` only needs `&self.watchlist`, so the disjoint-field borrow is fine (or inline the call as INotify does at line 540).

findings.title: O(N²) memmove draining node:net write buffer under backpressure
findings.problem: `internal_flush` (called from every `on_writable` event) writes from the head of `buffered_data_for_node_net` and then shifts the remainder down with `b.copy_within(written.., 0); b.truncate(len)`. Under sustained TCP backpressure a buffer of N bytes drained W bytes per writable event does N/W memmoves averaging N/2 bytes each → ~N²/(2W) bytes of copying to deliver N bytes. The same shift-down pattern appears at lines 2632-2636 and 2670-2683 in `write_or_end_buffered`. No read-offset is tracked; every partial drain pays O(remaining).
findings.proposed_fix: Track a `drain_offset: Cell<usize>` alongside the Vec and write `&buf[offset..]`; only `copy_within`/shrink when `offset > buf.len()/2` (amortized O(1) per byte). Alternatively store the buffer as a `VecDeque<u8>`/ring so partial drains are pointer advances. This keeps total copy work O(N) regardless of how many writable ticks it takes to drain.

findings.title: Per-file deep copy of source-map VLQ buffer on every bundle/reload
findings.problem: Inside the per-compiled-file loop of `finalize_bundle` (runs once per file in every initial bundle and every HMR rebuild), `compile_result.source_map_chunk()` returns `Option<&bun_sourcemap::Chunk>` and the code calls `.clone()` on it. `Chunk` derives `Clone` and contains `buffer: MutableString { list: Vec<u8> }`, so this deep-copies the entire VLQ mapping buffer (typically KB-tens of KB per file, aggregate MB for a full route bundle). The cloned `Chunk` is then passed by value into `receive_chunk`, which only ever calls `buffer.to_owned_slice()` (a `mem::take` + `into_boxed_slice`) on it — so the sole purpose of the clone is to obtain owned bytes from a borrowed `&Chunk`. The data is copied once here and then moved out; the copy could be eliminated entirely.
findings.evidence: DevServer.rs:4045-4109 loops `for (part_range, compile_result) in js_chunk.content.javascript().parts_in_chunk_in_order.iter().zip(js_chunk.compile_results_for_chunk.iter())` and at :4054 `Some(c) => c.clone()`. `bun_sourcemap::Chunk` at /workspace/bun/src/sourcemap/Chunk.rs:10-27 is `#[derive(Clone)]` with `buffer: MutableString` (= `Vec<u8>`, /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/MutableString.rs:29-32). Consumer `PackedMap::new_non_empty` at /workspace/bun/src/runtime/bake/dev_server/packed_map.rs:31-42 only reads `chunk.buffer` (via `to_owned_slice()` = `mem::take`) and two `i32` fields — it does not need an owned clone of the whole `Chunk`.
findings.proposed_fix: Iterate `js_chunk.compile_results_for_chunk` mutably (or add/ use a `source_map_chunk_mut()` accessor) and `core::mem::take` the `Chunk` instead of cloning it — the compile results are discarded after `finalize_bundle` so the move is safe. This removes one full `Vec<u8>` allocation + memcpy per bundled file per reload. If a mutable iterator is not feasible, change `ReceiveChunkSourceMap` to carry `&'a Chunk` and have `PackedMap::new_non_empty` take `&Chunk` and build `vlq_` with `Box::<[u8]>::from(chunk.buffer.list.as_slice())` (same single copy, no intermediate `Vec` clone).

findings.title: Route pattern re-parsed byte-by-byte on every match attempt (per-request × per-dynamic-route)
findings.problem: In the per-request hot path `Routes::match_dynamic` (line 555-563) iterates every dynamic route and calls `Pattern::match_` for each. Inside `Pattern::match_`, the `while` loop calls `Pattern::init(match_name, offset)` (line 1565, and again at 1608) which re-tokenizes the immutable route's `match_name` byte-by-byte (`init_maybe_hash` scans for '/' and '[' at lines 1778-1907) and wyhashes each static segment via `HashedString::init` (lines 1805/1814/1910 → HashedString.rs:24 `hash(buf)`). The route patterns never change after `RouteLoader::load_all`; `Pattern::validate` already parses them once at load time (line 1677 `Pattern::init_unhashed`) but the result is discarded except for `param_count`/`kind`. Result: for N dynamic routes with S segments of length L, every `router.match()` call does O(N·S·L) redundant byte-scanning + O(N·S_static) redundant wyhash calls on data that is constant for the process lifetime.
findings.evidence: Call chain confirmed per-request: filesystem_router.rs:634 `match_page_with_allocator` → lib.rs:579-582 `match_` → lib.rs:555-563 `match_dynamic` loop → lib.rs:1565 `Pattern::init(match_name, offset).expect("unreachable")` inside `while (offset as usize) < name.len()`. `Pattern::init` → `init_maybe_hash::<true>` (lib.rs:1766-1912) iterates bytes and builds `Value::Static(HashedString::init(...))`. `HashedString::init` (src/bun_core/string/HashedString.rs:20-26) computes `hash: hash(buf) as u32`. The `Route` struct (lib.rs:980-1007) stores only `kind`/`param_count`, not the parsed segment list — no caching one level up.
findings.proposed_fix: Precompute the segment list once at load time: store a `Box<[pattern::Value]>` (or a packed `[(Tag, TinyPtr, u32 hash)]`) on `Route` (it is already heap-boxed, so no extra indirection), filled from the existing `Pattern::validate` pass. Change `Pattern::match_` to iterate that precomputed slice instead of calling `Pattern::init` in the loop. This removes all per-request route-name byte-scanning and route-side wyhash calls, reducing dynamic-match cost to one URL-segment split + one compare per segment per candidate route.

findings.title: Per-fetch() header serialization does 2×(O(N²) lookups + sort + Vector<String> alloc) via spec iterator
findings.problem: WebCore__FetchHeaders__copyTo (line 1871) and WebCore__FetchHeaders__count (line 1917) both call headers->createIterator() and drain it with iter.next(). FetchHeaders::Iterator is the Fetch-spec sorted iterator: on first next() it heap-allocates a Vector<String> m_keys, copies every header name into it, std::sorts it, then for every subsequent next() it calls m_headers.get(key) which is findHTTPHeaderName (gperf hash) + a linear scan over the common/uncommon vector. A full drain is therefore O(N log N) sort + O(N²) comparisons + N gperf lookups. from_fetch_headers() (src/http_jsc/headers_jsc.rs:31,83) calls count() then copy_to() back-to-back for every outgoing fetch() request (src/runtime/webcore/fetch.rs:1401,1648) and for every StaticRoute/FileRoute response, so each request pays this twice.
findings.evidence: bindings.cpp:1873 `auto iter = headers->createIterator(false);` + line 1876 `for (auto pair = iter.next(); pair; pair = iter.next())`; bindings.cpp:1919-1921 same pattern in __count. FetchHeaders.cpp:341-352 builds+sorts m_keys; FetchHeaders.cpp:386 `auto value = m_headers->m_headers.get(key);` inside the per-step while. HTTPHeaderMap.cpp:101-107 get(StringView) = findHTTPHeaderName + linear findIf. Caller chain: headers_jsc.rs:31 `(*h).count(...)` and :83 `(*h).copy_to(...)` inside from_fetch_headers(), invoked per fetch() at fetch.rs:1401.
findings.proposed_fix: copyTo/count do not need spec-sorted order — they just flatten into a byte buffer for the HTTP client. Rewrite both to iterate headers->internalHeaders().commonHeaders(), .uncommonHeaders(), and .getSetCookieHeaders() directly (each entry already carries both key and value). That is O(N) with zero heap allocation, no sort, no gperf re-lookup, and no linear scan per key. The same direct-iteration pattern is already used in writeFetchHeadersToUWSResponse (NodeHTTP.cpp:776-848) and getInternalProperties (JSFetchHeaders.cpp:673-709).

findings.title: Per-query full-string toUpperCase()+trim() just to check a prefix in checkUnsafeTransaction
findings.problem: For every Postgres/MySQL query executed through the default pool, `checkUnsafeTransaction` allocates a full uppercase copy of the entire SQL string (via `sql.toUpperCase().trim()`) only to test whether it starts with "BEGIN" or "START TRANSACTION". For bulk inserts generated by `normalizeQuery` (e.g. 1000 rows × 10 cols of `$n` placeholders → tens of KB), this is an O(n) allocation+copy per query where an O(1) case-insensitive prefix check on the first ~17 chars after leading whitespace would suffice.

findings.title: Wasted ERR_clear_error/ERR_get_error on every Bun.CryptoHasher#update() call
findings.problem: `EVP::update()` unconditionally calls `boringssl::ERR_clear_error()` before every `EVP_DigestUpdate`, and `CryptoHasher::update()` (src/runtime/crypto/CryptoHasher.rs:604) unconditionally calls `ERR_get_error()` afterward. BoringSSL's `EVP_DigestUpdate` always returns 1 and never pushes to the error queue (it is `ctx->digest->update(...); return 1;`), so both calls are dead work executed on every chunk fed to `Bun.CryptoHasher#update()` for the common EVP-backed algorithms (md5/sha1/sha224/sha256/sha384/sha512/sha512-256/ripemd160/blake2b). `ERR_clear_error()` is not free: it performs a TLS lookup (`CRYPTO_get_thread_local`), loops over ERR_NUM_ERRORS=16 slots calling `free(error->data)` + `memset(0, 24)` on each, then one more `free(state->to_free)`. That is 17 libc `free(NULL)` calls + ~384 bytes zeroed + two TLS lookups per `update()`, independent of chunk size. For streaming/per-chunk hashing this repeated fixed cost is pure overhead.
findings.evidence: EVP.rs:253-261: `pub fn update(&mut self, input: &[u8]) { unsafe { boringssl::ERR_clear_error(); let _ = boringssl::EVP_DigestUpdate(...); } }`.  CryptoHasher.rs:602-609: `inner.with_mut(|e| e.update(buffer.slice())); let err = boring_ssl::ERR_get_error(); if err != 0 { ... }`.  vendor/boringssl/crypto/fipsmodule/digest/digest.cc.inc:167-170: `int EVP_DigestUpdate(...) { ctx->digest->update(ctx, data, len); return 1; }` (cannot fail, never touches error queue).  vendor/boringssl/crypto/err/err.cc:265-280: `ERR_clear_error()` loops `for (i = 0; i < ERR_NUM_ERRORS; i++) err_clear(&state->errors[i]); free(state->to_free);` where `err_clear` = `free(error->data); OPENSSL_memset(error, 0, sizeof(struct err_error_st));` and ERR_NUM_ERRORS=16 (err.h:399). The same dead `ERR_get_error()` check applies to the Hmac arm at CryptoHasher.rs:619 since `HMAC_Update` just wraps `EVP_DigestUpdate` (vendor/boringssl/crypto/fipsmodule/hmac/hmac.cc.inc:157-159).
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the `ERR_clear_error()` from `EVP::update()` (EVP_DigestUpdate cannot error), and drop the `ERR_get_error()`+throw block from the `Evp` and `Hmac` arms of `CryptoHasher::update()` (CryptoHasher.rs:604-624). Error-queue clearing is already performed at the boundaries that can actually fail (`init`, `hash`, `final`, `copy`). If defensive clearing is desired, do it once at hasher construction / `reset()`, not per-chunk.

findings.title: Two heap allocations per UDP destination address in send()/sendMany()
findings.problem: `parse_addr` converts the JS address string with `to_bun_string()` (WTFStringImpl alloc) and then `str.to_owned_slice_z().into_vec_with_nul()` (second heap alloc for a NUL-terminated Vec). It is called once per packet from the `send_many_impl` loop (line 1399 inside `while let Some(val) = iter.next()`) and once per `send()` (line ~1509 path). IP address strings are bounded (≤ INET6_ADDRSTRLEN+IF_NAMESIZE ≈ 62 bytes) so a fixed stack buffer suffices; the current code allocates on the heap per datagram.
findings.proposed_fix: Obtain the UTF-8 bytes via a borrowing slice (`to_slice`/`to_utf8`) and copy into a stack `[u8; INET6_ADDRSTRLEN + IF_NAMESIZE + 2]` with a trailing NUL; reject over-length inputs (they cannot be valid IPs). This removes both per-packet heap allocations on the `dgram` send hot path.

findings.title: Fresh ArrayHashMap allocated per bundled file in import diffing
findings.problem: `process_chunk_dependencies` is called once per file in the bundle output (DevServer.rs:4274-4303 loops over `parts_in_chunk_in_order`, HTML chunks, CSS chunks and calls it for each). The first thing it does is `let mut quick_lookup: ArrayHashMap<FileIndex<SIDE>, TempLookup> = ArrayHashMap::default();` and then populates it from the file's existing import edges. This allocates fresh backing storage (4 Vecs: keys/values/hashes/index) for every file, uses it for diffing, and drops it. For an initial route bundle with hundreds-thousands of modules this is hundreds-thousands of small heap alloc/free pairs per reload, all of which could share one cleared-and-reused map.
findings.evidence: incremental_graph.rs:856 `let mut quick_lookup: ArrayHashMap<FileIndex<SIDE>, TempLookup> = ArrayHashMap::default();` followed by a `while let Some(edge_index) = it { ... quick_lookup.put_no_clobber(...) }` fill, used once, then dropped at fn end. Call sites looping per-file: /workspace/bun/src/runtime/bake/DevServer.rs:4274 `for part_range in js_chunk.content.javascript().parts_in_chunk_in_order.iter() { ... process_chunk_dependencies(...) }`, :4288 (HTML chunks), :4296 (CSS chunks).
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a reusable `quick_lookup` map into `HotUpdateContext` (or a local in `finalize_bundle`) and pass it by `&mut` into `process_chunk_dependencies`, calling `.clear_retaining_capacity()` at the top of each call instead of constructing a new map. This turns N×4 Vec allocations into at most a handful of grow-once allocations for the whole reload.

findings.title: URL::parse scans the entire post-scheme URL for '@' and ':' on every parse (per-fetch / per-HTTP-request)
findings.problem: After stripping `scheme://`, `parse` unconditionally runs two separate `strings::index_of_char` scans over `&base[offset..]` — the *entire remainder of the URL* (host + path + query + fragment) — looking for `@` and `:`. For the overwhelmingly common case (no userinfo), the `@` scan traverses the full URL and misses; the `:` scan likewise traverses the full URL when there is no port. The bounding `/` check at line 638 only runs *after* both full scans have completed. `URL::parse` is invoked once per `fetch()` (src/runtime/webcore/fetch.rs:473, :275, :1927, :2068), once per HTTP-client redirect (src/http/lib.rs:5290/5350/5372), and once per package-manager network task (src/install/NetworkTask.rs:630/844) — all per-request hot paths.
findings.proposed_fix: Bound the userinfo search to the authority segment: compute `authority_end = strings::index_of_any(&base[offset..], b"/?#")` first (SIMD-backed, src/bun_core/string/immutable.rs:483), then search for `@` and `:` only within `base[offset..offset+authority_end]`. This shrinks the two scans from O(url_len) to O(authority_len) (~10-40 bytes typical). The `authority_end` result can additionally be reused to seed `parse_host` and the path offset, eliminating the re-scan in `parse_host`'s byte loop at lines 859-874.

findings.title: O(n²) linear name-dedup inside per-param loop in QueryStringMap::init / init_with_scanner (per-request FileSystemRouter path)
findings.problem: Inside the `while let Some(result) = scanner.next()` loop that runs once per query-string parameter, the code calls `list.iter().position(|p| p.name_hash == name_hash)` — a linear scan over all previously-inserted params — making the whole build O(k²) in param count. The same pattern is at line 1103 in `init_with_scanner`. k is capped at MAX_QUERY_STRING_PARAMS = 2048, so adversarial input yields ~2M u64 comparisons per request. Invoked per-request from `Bun.FileSystemRouter` match results when `.query`/`.params` is accessed (src/runtime/api/filesystem_router.rs:1033/1061/1064), whenever any param contains `%` or `+` (which bypasses the no-decode fast path at line 1178).
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist a hash-keyed lookup outside the loop: a `HashMap<u64, u32>` (name_hash → first list index) with an identity/nop hasher, since `name_hash` is already a wyhash. O(1) lookup+insert per param → O(k) total. Preserves the `index < route_parameter_begin` check in `init_with_scanner` by storing the first-seen index.

findings.title: Static-segment compare wyhashes both sides then memcmps anyway
findings.problem: In the same per-request × per-dynamic-route × per-segment inner loop, static segments are compared via `str_.eql_bytes(segment)` (line 1572). `str_` was just built by `Pattern::init` which wyhashed the route segment (cost #1), and `HashedString::eql_bytes` (src/bun_core/string/HashedString.rs:41-45) then wyhashes the URL segment (cost #2) before finally doing `self.str() == other` (memcmp). For typical path segments (<32 bytes) the length-guarded memcmp alone is as cheap as one wyhash, so the two hashes are pure overhead — they cannot short-circuit anything memcmp wouldn't, and when lengths match the memcmp runs regardless. Net: two wyhash computations per static segment per dynamic route per request that buy nothing.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1569-1575: `Value::Static(str_) => { let segment = &path_[0..path_.iter().position(..).unwrap_or(..)]; if !str_.eql_bytes(segment) { ... } }`. HashedString.rs:41-45: `pub fn eql_bytes(&self, other: &[u8]) -> bool { (self.len as usize) == other.len() && (hash(other) as u32) == self.hash && self.str() == other }`. `self.hash` was populated at lib.rs:1805/1814/1910 via `HashedString::init` which calls `bun_wyhash::hash`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `str_.eql_bytes(segment)` with a direct length+bytes compare (`str_.len as usize == segment.len() && strings::eql(str_.str(), segment)` or simply `str_.str() == segment`), and build the static segment with `HashedString::init_no_hash` (or, if Finding 1's precompute is adopted, store the precomputed route-side hash and compare only that one hash against a URL-segment hash computed once per request rather than once per candidate route).

findings.title: match_dynamic linearly tests every dynamic route with no first-segment pruning
findings.problem: `match_dynamic` (per-request, after the static hash-table miss) iterates the entire sorted dynamic-route slice and runs `Pattern::match_` on each candidate until one returns true. There is no index on the first path segment, so a URL like `/blog/123` is fully pattern-matched against `/admin/[id]`, `/api/[x]/[y]`, `/shop/[slug]`, etc. — each of which fails on the very first static segment but only after the re-parse + two wyhashes described above. This is O(N_dynamic_routes) per request where a first-static-segment `StringHashMap<Vec<usize>>` (built once at load time, keyed on the lowercase first segment when it is static, with a separate small list for routes whose first segment is itself dynamic) would prune to O(candidates_with_matching_prefix). The existing sort order within each bucket preserves Next.js priority semantics.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:550-565: `let dynamic = &self.list.items_route()[start..end]; ... for ((case_sensitive_name, name), route) in dynamic_names.iter().zip(...).zip(...) { if Pattern::match_::<true>(path, &case_sensitive_name[1..], name, params) { return Some(...); } }`. No filtering before the loop; `Route`/`Routes` store no per-first-segment index (lib.rs:405-433, 980-1007). Static routes already use a hash map (`self.static_`, line 579), but dynamic routes do not.
findings.proposed_fix: At `RouteLoader::load_all` time, bucket dynamic-route indices into a `StringHashMap<SmallVec<u32>>` keyed by their first static segment (lower-cased), plus one bucket for routes whose first segment is dynamic/catch-all. In `match_dynamic`, look up the URL's first segment and iterate only that bucket (in stored order) followed by the dynamic-first bucket. Reduces per-request work from O(N_dynamic) to ~O(matching_prefix_count).

findings.title: Redundant Box<[u8]> allocation for every RESP3 Push kind (per pub/sub message)
findings.problem: In the RESPType::Push arm of read_value_with_depth, the first element (the push kind, e.g. "message"/"subscribe") is parsed via read_value_with_depth(), which already heap-allocates a Box<[u8]> inside RESPValue::SimpleString/BulkString. The code then borrows that box as &[u8] and immediately allocates a SECOND Box<[u8]> via Box::<[u8]>::from(push_type_str), after which the first box is dropped. The comment "Copy the push type string since the original will be freed" is a leftover from the Zig port (where `defer push_type.deinit()` existed); in Rust the owned Box can simply be moved out of the enum. This runs once for every Push frame received — i.e. once per pub/sub message, subscribe ack, and invalidation notice — so it is on the per-message hot path.

findings.title: create_null_delimited_env_map: N+2 heap allocations per subprocess spawn (could be 2)
findings.problem: For every subprocess spawn that inherits the parent env (Bun.spawn default path at src/runtime/api/bun/js_bun_spawn_bindings.rs:922, shell subprocess at src/runtime/shell/subproc.rs:600, install lifecycle scripts at src/install/PackageManager/PackageManagerLifecycle.rs:454), `create_null_delimited_env_map` iterates all env vars and does one `vec![0u8; klen+vlen+2].into_boxed_slice()` heap allocation per entry, plus two Vec allocations for `storage` and `envp_buf`. With a typical 50-200 process env vars this is 52-202 allocations per spawn. The total byte length is computable in one prior pass (the map is already iterated), so all `K=V\0` strings can live in a single contiguous `Vec<u8>` with `envp_buf` pointing into it — reducing to 2 allocations regardless of env size. The per-entry buffers are also zero-initialized by `vec![0u8; n]` and then fully overwritten except for the trailing NUL, wasting a memset per entry.
findings.proposed_fix: First pass: sum `klen + vlen + 2` over all entries into `total_bytes`. Allocate one `Vec<u8>` with that capacity (uninitialized or via `extend_from_slice`/`push` so no pre-zeroing). Second pass: for each entry, record the current `buf.len()` offset, push key bytes, `b'='`, value bytes, `0u8`. After filling, convert offsets to pointers (`buf.as_ptr().add(off)`) into `envp_buf`. Change `NullDelimitedEnvMap._storage` from `Vec<Box<[u8]>>` to a single `Vec<u8>` (or `Box<[u8]>`). Result: 2 allocations (storage + envp_buf) instead of envp_count+2, and no redundant zero-fill.

findings.title: 64KB stack buffer zero-initialized on every TLS data chunk in SSLWrapper::handle_traffic
findings.problem: `handle_traffic()` declares `let mut buffer = [0u8; BUFFER_SIZE];` (BUFFER_SIZE = 65536) on every invocation. This compiles to a 64KB `memset(0)` that LLVM cannot elide because the buffer is immediately passed as `*mut c_void` to extern-"C" `SSL_read`/`BIO_read`, which the optimizer must assume may read before writing. `handle_traffic()` is called once per `receive_data()` (every encrypted chunk from the network), once per `write_data()` (every plaintext write), and once per `flush()`. The zeroing is unnecessary: `handle_reading`/`handle_writing` only ever read `buffer[0..read]`, which has been fully filled by `SSL_read`/`BIO_read`.
findings.evidence: src/uws/lib.rs:1148 `let mut buffer = [0u8; BUFFER_SIZE];` inside `handle_traffic()`. Called per-chunk from `receive_data` (line 736) and `write_data` (lines 753/771/776/786). Hot-path trace: `/workspace/bun/src/http/lib.rs:3909` `HTTPClient::on_data` → `ProxyTunnel::receive` → `/workspace/bun/src/http/ProxyTunnel.rs:755` `wrapper.receive_data(buf)` → `handle_traffic()`. Also hit per-chunk via `UpgradedDuplex` (node:tls over Duplex, `/workspace/bun/src/runtime/socket/UpgradedDuplex.rs:228,386`) and `WindowsNamedPipe` (`/workspace/bun/src/runtime/socket/WindowsNamedPipe.rs:295,1126`). By contrast, the equivalent uSockets C path allocates the SSL read buffer once per event loop and reuses it: `/workspace/bun/packages/bun-usockets/src/crypto/openssl.c:695` `loop_ssl_data->ssl_read_output = ssl_alloc_read_output();`.
findings.proposed_fix: Avoid zeroing the scratch buffer on each call. Two options: (a) use `let mut buffer = MaybeUninit::<[u8; BUFFER_SIZE]>::uninit();` and pass `buffer.as_mut_ptr().cast()` / length to `SSL_read`/`BIO_read`, slicing only the written prefix for the callbacks (the FFI functions never read the destination, they only write); or (b) move the buffer to a `Box<[u8; 65536]>` field on `SSLWrapper` allocated once in `init_with_ctx` and reused across `handle_reading`/`handle_writing`/`flush_pending_events`, mirroring openssl.c's per-loop `ssl_read_output`. Option (b) also removes the 64KB stack frame the existing `// PERF:` comment flags.

findings.title: DeferredTaskQueue::run() re-hashes keys on removal though index is already known (stale TODO)
findings.problem: Inside the per-microtask-drain loop, removals use `self.map.swap_remove(&key)` which calls `get_index(key)` (hash + probe) to find the slot, even though the loop already holds the slot index `i`. The in-file PERF comment (lines 66-72) says to switch to `swap_remove_at` "when bun_collections::ArrayHashMap grows it" — that method now exists (`ArrayHashMap::swap_remove_at(index)` at /workspace/bun/src/collections/array_hash_map.rs:984) but the code was never updated.
findings.evidence: DeferredTaskQueue.rs:73-94 iterates `i` over `self.map.keys()` and at lines 78 and 90 calls `self.map.swap_remove(&key)`; the comment at 66-72 reads: "PERF: swap_remove(&K) re-hashes; restore swap_remove_at when bun_collections::ArrayHashMap grows it." `ArrayHashMap::swap_remove_at` is defined at /workspace/bun/src/collections/array_hash_map.rs:984. `run()` is invoked from `drain_microtasks_with_global` (/workspace/bun/src/jsc/event_loop.rs:342), whose own comment at line 319 notes it runs ~2x per HTTP request — so this is a per-tick hot path, and each removal pays an unnecessary hash + table lookup.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace both `self.map.swap_remove(&key)` calls with `self.map.swap_remove_at(i)` and drop the stale comment. Loop structure stays the same (re-read `self.map.len()` after removal, don't advance `i`).

findings.title: FetchHeaders::Iterator::next() does a linear HTTPHeaderMap::get() per step → O(N²) full iteration
findings.problem: Iterator::next() stores only the sorted key names in m_keys (built at line 341-352), then on each step re-resolves the value with `m_headers->m_headers.get(key)`. get(StringView) runs findHTTPHeaderName (gperf) and then a linear findIf over m_commonHeaders or m_uncommonHeaders. Iterating N headers via entries()/forEach()/keys()/values() therefore costs N gperf hashes + N linear scans = O(N²). Besides being the root cause of the copyTo/count issue above, this iterator backs the JS Headers iterator (JSDOMIterator.h:118,223) and is drained per WebSocket connect (WebSocket.cpp:273,634; JSWebSocket.cpp:317).
findings.evidence: FetchHeaders.cpp:373-389 `while (m_currentIndex < m_keys.size()) { auto key = m_keys[m_currentIndex]; ... auto value = m_headers->m_headers.get(key); }`. m_keys built at 341-354 by walking HTTPHeaderMapConstIterator and storing only names, discarding the value that iterator already produced (HTTPHeaderMap.h:145-162 updateKeyValue sets both key and value).
findings.proposed_fix: When building the snapshot at lines 341-352, capture key+value pairs (Vector<KeyValuePair<String,String>>) instead of only keys, sort by key, and have next() return the cached pair directly. This makes next() O(1), eliminates N redundant gperf lookups and the O(N²) scans, and matches the mutation-safety semantics already provided by m_updateCounter (the snapshot is rebuilt on mutation anyway).

findings.title: normalize_label allocates a fresh Vec<u8> on every reference-link lookup
findings.problem: `normalize_label` (ref_defs.rs:40-84) does `let mut result: Vec<u8> = Vec::new();` and returns it by value. It is called from `lookup_ref_def` (ref_defs.rs:97) which is invoked up to 5 times per `[`/`![` bracket in the document: `try_match_bracket_link` (links.rs:623,759,775) runs during `collect_emphasis_delimiters` for every bracket, and `process_link` (links.rs:536,571) runs again during the emit phase. In any document that contains at least one reference definition (`!self.ref_defs.is_empty()` gate at ref_defs.rs:89 passes), every bracket triggers a heap allocation + free just to normalize the label for a hash lookup.
findings.evidence: ref_defs.rs:40-42: `pub fn normalize_label(&mut self, raw: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8> { let mut result: Vec<u8> = Vec::new(); ... }`; ref_defs.rs:97: `let normalized = self.normalize_label(raw_label);` inside `lookup_ref_def`; links.rs:623/759/775 call `lookup_ref_def` from `try_match_bracket_link`, which inlines.rs:716 calls for every `[`/`![` during `collect_emphasis_delimiters`. The Parser already recycles scratch buffers for other per-block work (`bracket_pairs`, `label_frames`, `buffer`) but not this one.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `normalize_label_buf: Vec<u8>` field to `Parser` (like the existing `bracket_pairs`/`label_frames` recycled buffers) and have `normalize_label` clear-and-fill it, returning `&[u8]`. `lookup_ref_def` only needs a borrow for the `StringSet` lookup. The two definition-time callers (blocks.rs:962, ref_defs.rs:414) that need ownership can `Box::from(slice)` afterward — those run once per ref-def, not per bracket.

findings.title: is_unicode_punctuation_extended does a linear scan over ~300 sorted ranges per flanking check
findings.problem: `is_unicode_punctuation_extended` iterates `UNICODE_PUNCTUATION_RANGES` (~300 `[lo,hi]` pairs, already sorted ascending) front-to-back with an early-out only when `codepoint < range[0]`. For high codepoints (emoji at U+1F300+, CJK symbols at U+3000+) it walks 150–300 entries. This is reached from `is_unicode_punctuation` → `is_left_flanking`/`is_right_flanking` → `can_open_emphasis`/`can_close_emphasis` (inlines.rs:743-744, 1240-1268), which run for EVERY `*`/`_`/`~` delimiter run in the document and may evaluate the before/after codepoint up to 4× each. Any markdown that mixes emphasis with non-ASCII neighbours (emoji, CJK, accented Latin) pays O(300) per check where O(log 300)≈9 suffices.
findings.evidence: helpers.rs:99-109: `fn is_unicode_punctuation_extended(codepoint: u32) -> bool { for range in UNICODE_PUNCTUATION_RANGES { if codepoint >= range[0] && codepoint <= range[1] { return true; } if codepoint < range[0] { return false; } } false }`. Table at helpers.rs:276-361 is sorted by `range[0]`. Call chain: inlines.rs:743-744 `can_open_emphasis`/`can_close_emphasis` per delimiter → inlines.rs:1197-1268 `is_left_flanking`/`is_right_flanking` → helpers.rs:50-56 `is_unicode_punctuation` → `is_unicode_punctuation_extended` for any codepoint ≥ 128.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the linear loop with `UNICODE_PUNCTUATION_RANGES.partition_point(|r| r[1] < codepoint)` (or `binary_search_by`) and test containment on the found slot. The table is static, sorted and non-overlapping, so this is a drop-in O(log n) change with no behavioural difference.

findings.title: build_ref_def_hashtable copies every paragraph's bytes into self.buffer even when it cannot be a ref-def
findings.problem: For every `BlockType::P` in the document, `build_ref_def_hashtable` unconditionally merges all of its lines into `self.buffer` (ref_defs.rs:380-399) and then calls `parse_ref_def(&merged, 0)`, which immediately returns `None` at ref_defs.rs:111 unless the very first byte is `[`. The setext path in blocks.rs:885 already guards on `self.text[lines[0].beg] == b'['` before doing the identical merge, but this pass does not — so a typical document where most paragraphs don't start with `[` memcpy's essentially the entire document text through `self.buffer` for nothing.
findings.evidence: ref_defs.rs:380-399 merges lines with no precondition; ref_defs.rs:408-411: `while pos < merged.len() { let Some(result) = self.parse_ref_def(&merged, pos) else { break; }; ... }`; ref_defs.rs:111: `if p >= text.len() || text[p] != b'[' { return None; }` — instant reject at pos 0. Compare blocks.rs:881-886 which gates the same merge on `self.text[self.current_block_lines[0].beg as usize] == b'['`.
findings.proposed_fix: Before the merge loop, read the first `VerbatimLine` at `lines_off`, and `continue` if `self.text[vline.beg as usize] != b'['` (with the same `beg>end`/`end>size` validity guard). This skips the O(paragraph_bytes) memcpy for the overwhelmingly common case and matches the guard already used in blocks.rs.

findings.title: process_inline_content clones the entire emph_delims Vec (and allocates a fresh prev_candidate Vec) per block and per link label
findings.problem: `process_inline_content` does `let mut resolved: Vec<EmphDelim> = self.emph_delims.clone();` at line 218 and again inside `enter_label!` at line 243 for every link/image/wikilink label. `EmphDelim` is ~64 bytes, so this is a full heap allocation + memcpy of `delims.len()*64` bytes once per leaf block and once per nested label. Separately, `resolve_emphasis_delimiters` allocates `let mut prev_candidate: Vec<usize> = (0..len).map(..).collect();` (line 793) fresh on every call with no recycling. The Parser already recycles `bracket_pairs`, `label_frames` and `buffer` via `core::mem::take` for exactly this reason, but these two per-block allocations were missed.
findings.evidence: inlines.rs:217-218: `// Copy resolved delimiters locally (label frames reuse emph_delims)\n let mut resolved: Vec<EmphDelim> = self.emph_delims.clone();`; inlines.rs:243 (inside enter_label! macro, run per link/image): `resolved = self.emph_delims.clone();`; inlines.rs:793: `let mut prev_candidate: Vec<usize> = (0..len).map(|i| i.wrapping_sub(1)).collect();` inside `resolve_emphasis_delimiters`, which is called at inlines.rs:215 and 242 (per block + per label).
findings.proposed_fix: Store a second recycled `Vec<EmphDelim>` (and a `Vec<usize>` for `prev_candidate`) on `Parser` alongside `bracket_pairs`/`label_frames`. Replace `self.emph_delims.clone()` with `core::mem::swap(&mut resolved, &mut self.emph_delims)` so both vectors keep their capacity in steady state; have `resolve_emphasis_delimiters` `clear()`+`extend` the recycled `prev_candidate` instead of `.collect()`ing a new one.

findings.title: Per-TLS-record JS property lookup for `write` on UpgradedDuplex
findings.problem: `call_write_or_end` is invoked from `internal_write` (the SSLWrapper `write` callback, line 203/213/335/370) for every encrypted TLS record emitted through a JS Duplex. Each call does `duplex.get(&global, name)` — a full `JSC__JSValue__getIfPropertyExistsImpl` FFI lookup with a byte-slice key — to fetch the same `duplex.write` function every time. The function is never cached, so each outbound TLS chunk pays a dynamic string-keyed prototype-chain lookup plus identifier construction.
findings.proposed_fix: Resolve `duplex.write` and `duplex.end` once in `UpgradedDuplex::from()` and store them as `Strong`/`StrongOptional` fields next to `origin`; `call_write_or_end` then reads the cached callable directly. This removes a JS property lookup per TLS write on the TLS-over-Duplex path (e.g. `tls.connect({ socket: someDuplex })`).

findings.title: IPv4 address_to_string heap-allocates a 128-byte String via create_format per address
findings.problem: The AF_INET branch of address_to_string builds the dotted-quad string with BunString::create_format(format_args!("{}.{}.{}.{}", ...)). create_format is documented (src/bun_core/string/mod.rs:364) as a cold-path helper: it heap-allocates a std::string::String::with_capacity(128), runs the core::fmt virtual-dispatch machinery, then copies again into a WTF string. This runs once per IPv4 address in every DNS result (via options_jsc::result_to_js -> address_to_js -> address_to_string, and cares_jsc::addr_info_to_js_array). The adjacent AF_INET6 branch (lines 378-401) already does it the efficient way: writes into a 64-byte stack buffer and calls clone_latin1 once.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the AF_INET6 branch: write into a 16-byte stack buffer (max "255.255.255.255" = 15 bytes) using either bun_fmt::print_int for each octet or std::io::Write into &mut [u8], then return BunString::clone_latin1(&buf[..n]). This removes one heap allocation + the core::fmt dispatch per IPv4 address and leaves a single WTF-string copy, matching the v6 path.

findings.title: EventLoopTimer::less() makes two cross-crate extern calls per compare when a local tag match suffices
findings.problem: `less()` is the pairing-heap comparator (invoked from `meld`, i.e. once per timer insert and O(log n) per delete_min — per-setTimeout/clearTimeout work). Whenever `a.next.sec == b.next.sec` (the common case for sub-second JS timers), it unconditionally calls `a.js_timer_epoch()` and `b.js_timer_epoch()` — each a `#[no_mangle]` cross-crate `extern "Rust"` call into bun_runtime that does a tag match + container_of + field load. But the only thing used from the result in the non-tie-break path is `is_some()`, which is fully determined by `self.tag` (Some iff tag is TimeoutObject/ImmediateObject/AbortSignalTimeout — see /workspace/bun/src/runtime/timer/mod.rs:579-595). The actual epoch `u32` is only consumed inside the `a_ns == b_ns` tie-break at line 110-129.
findings.evidence: EventLoopTimer.rs:90-133: after the `sec` early-return, lines 97-98 call `a.js_timer_epoch()`/`b.js_timer_epoch()` and lines 101-106 use only `.is_some()` to decide nsec-to-ms rounding; the epoch value itself is only read at 110-128. `js_timer_epoch` → `__bun_js_timer_epoch` (extern "Rust" #[no_mangle] at /workspace/bun/src/runtime/dispatch.rs:1118) → `js_timer_flags_ptr` which matches on `(*t).tag`. `less` is reached via `HeapContext::less` (/workspace/bun/src/runtime/timer/mod.rs:288) from `Intrusive::meld` (/workspace/bun/src/io/heap.rs:209) on every insert/remove/delete_min. The existing PERF note at EventLoopTimer.rs:28-30 acknowledges this extern sits on the heap-compare path.
findings.proposed_fix: Add an `#[inline] fn is_js_timer_tag(tag: Tag) -> bool { matches!(tag, Tag::TimeoutObject | Tag::ImmediateObject | Tag::AbortSignalTimeout) }` in this crate and use it for the nsec-rounding decision (lines 101-106). Only call `a.js_timer_epoch()`/`b.js_timer_epoch()` inside the `order == Equal` tie-break block (lines 109-130). This removes both cross-crate calls (and their container_of + load) from every compare where the ms-rounded nsec values differ, without the heavier denormalize-epoch refactor suggested in the existing comment.

findings.title: detectCommand lowercases/trims the entire accumulated query and prepends char-by-char per SQL helper
findings.problem: `detectCommand` (invoked per `SQLHelper` via `adapter.getHelperCommand(query)` at shared.ts:423) calls `query.toLowerCase().trim()` on the entire query-built-so-far, then scans backwards building tokens with `token = char + token` (line 307) — a fresh string allocation per character. The goal is only to find the last keyword preceding the helper, so lowering/copying the whole prefix is unnecessary, and the prepend pattern is O(token_len²) per token. `sqlite.ts:63` has the same `toUpperCase().trim()` pattern in `parseSQLQuery`.
findings.proposed_fix: Scan backwards over the original `query` without lowercasing the whole string: skip trailing whitespace, record the token bounds (start/end indices) while walking back to the previous whitespace, then do a case-insensitive compare of `query.slice(start, end)` against the small fixed keyword set. This avoids the full-string toLowerCase() copy and the per-char prepend allocations. Same fix applies to `parseSQLQuery` in sqlite.ts.

findings.title: lexer::is_identifier() has no ASCII fast path; called for every EDot and object property key
findings.problem: `is_identifier()` decodes every byte through `CodepointIterator::next` (stateful cursor: pos = i + width, load, branch, store i/width/c) and then calls `is_identifier_part()` per codepoint.
