The cursor dependency chain serializes the loop so LLVM cannot vectorize it (~10 ops/byte). In the printer hot loop this is invoked at /workspace/bun/src/js_printer/lib.rs:3687 for every `EDot` node (`a.b` property access — one of the top-3 most frequent expr kinds) and at :4912 for every object-literal property key. >99.9% of `e.name` values are pure ASCII, so the full WTF-8 decode + Unicode-table path is paid on every dot for no benefit.
findings.evidence: Call site (per-iteration body, no guard): src/js_printer/lib.rs:3687 `if lexer::is_identifier(&e.name) { ... }` inside `ExprData::EDot(e)`. Implementation at src/bun_core/string/identifier.rs:28-43 loops `while iter.next(&mut cursor)` with no `is_all_ascii` short-circuit. Contrast with src/js_printer/lib.rs:6850 where `print_identifier_ascii_only` was explicitly given a SIMD `strings::is_all_ascii(identifier)` fast path for the same distributional reason, and with `is_latin1_identifier` (src/ast/lexer_tables.rs:823) which is a straight vectorizable byte-range loop.
findings.proposed_fix: Prepend an ASCII fast path to `bun_core::identifier::is_identifier`: if `strings::is_all_ascii(text)` (or `text.iter().all(|&b| b < 0x80)`), check `text[0]` against `[a-zA-Z_$]` and `text[1..]` against `[a-zA-Z0-9_$]` via a 256-entry `static` bitmap or the existing range match (both auto-vectorize). Fall through to the current `CodepointIterator` loop only when a non-ASCII byte is seen. This fixes all call sites (EDot, print_property, EImportIdentifier alias, ECommonjsExportIdentifier) at once with no AST changes.

findings.title: Body buffer pre-reserves only one packet instead of Content-Length
findings.problem: In handle_response_body_from_multiple_packets (the per-TCP-read path for Content-Length bodies), the first-packet pre-allocation is `try_reserve_exact(incoming_data.len())` — i.e. one TCP read's worth. Every subsequent packet falls through to `buffer.write(remainder)` → `Vec::extend_from_slice`, which grows by doubling. For an N-byte download that arrives in ~64KB reads this is ~log2(N) reallocs and ~2N bytes of extra memcpy even though `content_length` is known up-front. The guard `incoming_data.len() < PREALLOCATE_MAX` (256 MB) is effectively always true for a socket read, which strongly suggests the intended comparand was `content_length`, not the single-packet length. Verified there is no other pre-reserve for `body_out_str`/`compressed_body` anywhere on this path; the same shape exists in the original Zig (so it is 'battle-tested', but still wasted work on every large download, e.g. `bun install` tarballs).

findings.title: encodeInto(utf16) partial-fill path is pure scalar, no SIMD ASCII fast-path
findings.problem: `copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_utf8_len` (the function `TextEncoder__encodeInto16` and `encode16_impl` both reach via `strings::copy_utf16_into_utf8{,_with_utf8_len}`) only tries the simdutf bulk convert when `utf8_len <= buf.len()` and the input is surrogate-free. In every other case — output buffer smaller than the full encoding (the canonical `encodeInto` streaming use), or a single unpaired surrogate anywhere in the input — it falls through to a per-codepoint scalar loop (`decode_utf16_with_fffd` + `encode_wtf8_rune` + slice copy) starting from index 0. ASCII runs are therefore narrowed one u16 at a time instead of via SIMD. The sibling implementation in `string/immutable/unicode.rs::copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_buffer_impl` already has the faster algorithm: `first_non_ascii16` SIMD scan + `copy_u16_into_u8` per segment, with simdutf retried around each surrogate. Only the slower variant is wired into `TextEncoder`.
findings.evidence: /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/lib.rs:1796-1825 — `if utf8_len > 0 && utf8_len <= buf.len() { simdutf… if SUCCESS return }` followed by `while read < utf16.len() { let (cp, adv) = decode_utf16_with_fffd(&utf16[read..]); let n = encode_wtf8_rune(&mut tmp, cp); … }`. Callers: /workspace/bun/src/runtime/webcore/TextEncoder.rs:283 (`TextEncoder__encodeInto16` → `strings::copy_utf16_into_utf8`) and TextEncoder.rs:100 (`encode16_impl` → `strings::copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_utf8_len`). Re-export chain: /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/immutable.rs:374 → `crate::strings_impl::copy_utf16_into_utf8{,_with_utf8_len}`. Contrast with /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/immutable/unicode.rs:1083-1175 which uses `first_non_ascii16` + `copy_u16_into_u8`.
findings.proposed_fix: Route `TextEncoder`'s 16-bit paths through the segment-SIMD implementation (`copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_buffer_impl::<false>`) or port its `first_non_ascii16` + `copy_u16_into_u8` loop into `strings_impl::copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_utf8_len`'s fallback so partial-buffer fills and surrogate-containing inputs still get SIMD treatment for ASCII runs. On simdutf `SURROGATE` failure, use `r.count` to resume from the failing position instead of restarting at 0.

findings.title: openat_a on Windows: redundant 98 KB PathBuffer + memcpy that is immediately discarded
findings.problem: `openat_a` unconditionally materializes a `bun_paths::PathBuffer` (98 302 bytes on Windows), memcpys the incoming `&[u8]` into it, writes a NUL, builds a `ZStr`, and calls `openat(dir, z, ...)`. On Windows, `openat` (lib.rs:3960) immediately calls `super::openat_windows_a(dir, path.as_bytes(), ...)` — i.e. it strips the NUL right back off and hands the raw `&[u8]` to the UTF-8→UTF-16 converter. The entire PathBuffer copy + NUL step is dead work on Windows, done once per open. The 98 KB stack local also forces `__chkstk` stack probing (~24 page touches) per call. `File::openat`, `Dir::open_at`, `open_dir_at`, and the `delete_tree` inner loop all route through this (≈100 call sites in the tree).
findings.evidence: lib.rs:6381-6391: `let mut buf = bun_paths::PathBuffer::default(); ... buf.0[..path.len()].copy_from_slice(path); buf.0[path.len()] = 0; let z = ZStr::from_buf(&buf.0[..], path.len()); openat(dir, z, flags, perm)`.  Windows `openat` at lib.rs:3960-3968: `super::openat_windows_a(dir, path.as_bytes(), flags, mode)`.  `MAX_PATH_BYTES` on Windows = 32767*3+1 = 98 302 (bun_core/util.rs:736-738). `PathBuffer::default()` is uninit (no memset) but still a ~96 KB stack frame.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `#[cfg(windows)]` fast-path in `openat_a` that returns `openat_windows_a(dir, path, flags, perm)` directly, bypassing the PathBuffer/ZStr round-trip. Keep the existing body under `#[cfg(not(windows))]` where the NUL-terminated C string is actually required by `libc::openat`.

findings.title: delete_tree: per-entry unlink/open re-copies name into a PathBuffer when a zero-copy ZStr is already available (POSIX)
findings.problem: Inside the per-entry hot loop of `Dir::delete_tree`, each file is removed via `unlinkat_a(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.slice_u8(), 0)` and each subdirectory is opened via `openat_a(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.slice_u8(), ...)`. Both `*_a` helpers allocate a stack `PathBuffer` (4 KB Linux / 1 KB macOS / 98 KB Windows), memcpy the name into it, and append a NUL — solely to produce a `&ZStr`. But on POSIX the kernel already wrote `d_name` NUL-terminated inside the iterator's getdents buffer, and `dir_iterator::Name::as_zstr()` (lib.rs:237) returns that `&ZStr` with zero copying. So every file deleted pays a 1–4 KB stack bump + memcpy of the name that is provably redundant. For `rm -rf node_modules` this is 10^5+ redundant copies.
findings.evidence: dir.rs:178 `unlinkat_a(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.slice_u8(), 0)` and dir.rs:152-157 `openat_a(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.slice_u8(), ...)`. `unlinkat_a` at dir.rs:303-311 creates `PathBuffer::default()` and copies. `Name::as_zstr()` at lib.rs:236-241: `unsafe { bun_core::ZStr::from_raw(self.ptr.as_ptr(), self.len) }` — zero-copy borrow of the kernel-written NUL-terminated `d_name` (see `Name::borrow` debug_assert at lib.rs:190).
findings.proposed_fix: On `cfg(not(windows))`, call `unlinkat_with_flags(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.as_zstr(), 0)` and `openat(top.iter.dir(), entry.name.as_zstr(), O::DIRECTORY|..., 0)` directly, skipping `unlinkat_a`/`openat_a`. Keep the `*_a` path only for Windows (where `as_zstr()` returns `&WStr` and a different wide-path helper would be needed to avoid the UTF-8 round-trip there too).

findings.title: AbortSignal is heap-allocated in C++ unconditionally for every request
findings.problem: prepare_js_request_context() calls `jsc::AbortSignal::new(global)` on every request. This crosses FFI into `WebCore__AbortSignal__new` (bindings.cpp:5534) which does `WebCore::AbortSignal::create(context)` — a RefCounted C++ heap allocation — then two more FFI hops (`pending_activity_ref()` and `ref_()`). There is no fast-path: the signal is created even when the user never reads `request.signal` and the request completes synchronously without aborting.
findings.evidence: mod.rs:728-735: `let signal = jsc::AbortSignal::new(global); ctx_mut.signal = NonNull::new(signal); opaque_deref_mut(signal).pending_activity_ref(); let signal_ref = unsafe { AbortSignalRef::adopt(opaque_deref_mut(signal).ref_()) };`. The Request already supports lazy signal creation — Request.rs:787 `// Lazy create default signal` in `get_signal` — so `signal: None` is a supported state for the struct.
findings.proposed_fix: Defer AbortSignal creation. Pass `None` to `Request::init` and leave `ctx.signal = None`. Create the signal on first `request.signal` access (Request::get_signal already does this) and have the lazy path also register the pointer back into the live `AnyRequestContext` so `on_abort` can fire it. `on_abort`/`set_signal_aborted` already no-op when `self.signal` is None (RequestContext.rs:618), so a never-observed signal correctly fires nothing. Edge case to cover: if abort happens before first `.signal` access, the lazy getter must return an already-aborted signal (check `ctx.flags.aborted()` when materializing).

findings.title: webcore::Request is Box::new'd per request while its siblings are pooled
findings.problem: Every request heap-allocates a `webcore::Request` via `bun_core::heap::into_raw(Request::new(Request::init(..)))` where `Request::new` is literally `Box::new(v)` (Request.rs:144). The surrounding code goes to great lengths to pool the other per-request structs — `RequestContext` comes from a 2048-slot `HiveArray::Fallback` (mod.rs:689) and the body `Value` comes from a 256-slot hive (Body.rs:553) — but the `Request` itself hits mimalloc alloc/free every time (freed in Request::finalize via `Box::from_raw`).
findings.evidence: mod.rs:742-749 `let request_object: *mut Request = bun_core::heap::into_raw(crate::webcore::Request::new(crate::webcore::Request::init(..)));`  and Request.rs:144 `pub fn new(v: Request) -> Box<Request> { Box::new(v) }`. Grep for `Request.*pool` in Request.rs returns nothing; `finalize` (Request.rs:835) does `drop(Box::from_raw(this))`.
findings.proposed_fix: Allocate `Request` from a per-VM `HiveArray::Fallback` exactly like `Body::Value` (Body.rs:542-561). `Request::finalize` returns the slot to the pool instead of `Box::from_raw`. Request is already designed to be re-initialized in place (`finalize_without_deinit` resets every field), and it is always created/destroyed on the JS thread, so no Send concerns.

findings.title: update_counts() does pointer-chase + arithmetic even when delta == 0
findings.problem: update_counts() runs on every tick_concurrent_with_count() call (≥3× per EventLoop::tick()). After `concurrent_ref.swap(0, SeqCst)`, it unconditionally evaluates `self.vm_ref().platform_loop_opt().expect("event_loop_handle")` (Option load + panic-branch) and then falls into the `else` arm doing `loop_.num_polls -= 0`, `u32::try_from(0).expect(...)`, and `loop_.active.saturating_sub(0)` — two pointer-chased load+stores and two `.expect()` branches — when `delta == 0`, which is the overwhelming steady-state case (only ref_concurrently/unref_concurrently bump it). There is no fast-path guard.
findings.evidence: Lines 540-569: `let delta = self.concurrent_ref.swap(0, Ordering::SeqCst); let loop_ = self.vm_ref().platform_loop_opt().expect("event_loop_handle"); ... if delta > 0 { ... } else { loop_.num_polls -= -delta; loop_.active = loop_.active.saturating_sub(u32::try_from(-delta).expect("int cast")); }` — no `if delta == 0 { return }`. Caller chain: tick() lines 633/643/654/662 → tick_concurrent() line 460 → tick_concurrent_with_count() line 483 → update_counts(). The file already documents at line 316-322 and line 1040-1044 that these per-tick costs are being counted at the instruction level for node:http perf.
findings.proposed_fix: Insert `if delta == 0 { return; }` immediately after the swap (before `platform_loop_opt()`). Optionally also gate the swap itself with a relaxed load-first (`if self.concurrent_ref.load(Relaxed) == 0 { return }`) to avoid the SeqCst RMW in the common case; correctness is preserved because a missed delta is picked up on the next tick_concurrent call.

findings.title: StructuralIndex::at() re-bounds-checks self.win[] after the refill gate — LLVM cannot elide it
findings.problem: `at()` is the hottest stage-2 primitive (every `pos_at`/`peek`/`run`/`string_body_at` call goes through it, ~6-10× per JSON value). It does `if logical - self.base >= self.win.len() { self.fill_to(logical); } self.win[logical - self.base]`. Because the cold `fill_to(&mut self)` call is merged back into the fall-through, LLVM inserts a phi and re-checks `i < win.len()` on the hot path even though the `if` already proved it. Release LLVM IR confirms: 53 surviving `panic_bounds_check` calls targeting json_index.rs:99 across the crate, 11 inside `parse_object` and 8 inside `parse_array` (i.e. one extra compare+branch per index lookup, per token, per document).
findings.evidence: Release IR (`target/release/deps/bun_parsers-*.ll`): in `parse_object` the inlined `at()` expands to `bb3.i12.i290: %_17.i16.i294 = icmp ult %_10.i14.i292.pre-phi, %_15.i15.i293 ; br ... panic.i17.i295` — the bounds check survives after the `_3.not.i10.i288` gate. `grep -c alloc_e23b886646e8214a9c61311ab94c09e9` (the Location for json_index.rs:99) → 53 total, 11 in parse_object, 8 in parse_array.
findings.proposed_fix: Restructure `at()` so the hot path returns before the cold call, so no phi merges the two: `let i = logical - self.base; if let Some(&p) = self.win.get(i) { return p as usize; } self.fill_to(logical); self.win[logical - self.base] as usize`. (Or `get_unchecked` after the explicit `<` check.) This removes one compare+branch from every index lookup in the per-token loop.

findings.title: JSX lexer loops reload `self.contents` per byte (the `step_with` optimization was never applied to JSX)
findings.problem: `next_inside_jsx_element` (23 sites), `parse_jsx_string_literal` (4 sites, per-byte loop), and `next_jsx_element_child` all call `self.step()` instead of binding `let contents = self.contents` once and calling `self.step_with(contents)`. Each inlined `self.step()` re-emits the `self.contents` struct-field load that the authors explicitly measured at ~7.7% of `next()` samples and fixed there. The JSX scanners were never given the same treatment, so every JSX whitespace byte, attribute-name byte, JSX-string body byte, and JSX text byte pays a dependent `mov 0x80(%rbx),%rsi` that LLVM cannot hoist across the intervening `&mut self` writes.
findings.evidence: Lines 1171-1177 document the problem and fix for `next()`: "every inlined site re-emitted `mov 0x80(%rbx),%rsi` ... ~7.7% of `next()` samples ... LLVM couldn't prove the field load loop-invariant across the intervening `&mut self` writes". Line 1534 shows `next()` binding `let contents: &[u8] = self.contents;` once. `grep` of lines 2906-3470 (all JSX scanners) shows 0 uses of `step_with` and 32 uses of `self.step()`. No local `contents` binding exists in `next_inside_jsx_element`, `parse_jsx_string_literal`, or `next_jsx_element_child`.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the `next()` pattern: at the top of `next_inside_jsx_element`, `parse_jsx_string_literal`, and `next_jsx_element_child`, add `let contents: &[u8] = self.contents;` and replace every `self.step()` in their bodies with `self.step_with(contents)`. Same for `scan_reg_exp`/`scan_reg_exp_validate_and_step` if desired, though regex is rarer.

findings.title: `parse_string_literal_inner` re-slices + bounds-checks + compares `>= 4096` on every ordinary body byte
findings.problem: The `_ =>` arm — hit for every plain ASCII character inside a '"' / '\'' string literal — executes `let remainder = &contents[self.current..]; if remainder.len() >= 4096 { ...SIMD skip... }`. This is inside the per-byte body loop with no fast-path around it. Because `next_codepoint_multibyte` is `#[cold] #[inline(never)]`, LLVM cannot prove `self.current <= contents.len()` and must keep the slice bounds check; it also recomputes `contents.len() - self.current` each iteration. `remainder.len()` can only decrease, so once the check fails (always true for files < 4 KB, and for the last 4 KB of every file) the slice + bounds-check + sub + compare is pure per-byte waste for the remainder of every string literal. The same pattern exists in `scan_multi_line_comment_body` at line 2453-2454 with a 512-byte threshold.
findings.evidence: Lines 1070-1098: the `_ =>` arm falls through to `self.step_with(contents)` at line 1102 after doing `let remainder = &contents[self.current..]; if remainder.len() >= 4096 { ... }` unconditionally for `QUOTE == 0x22 || QUOTE == 0x27`. No flag caches whether the SIMD window has been exhausted; there is no early-out once `remainder.len() < 4096`.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the threshold once before the loop: `let simd_cutoff = contents.len().saturating_sub(4096);` (and `512` for the comment scanner), then replace the body check with `if self.current <= simd_cutoff { let remainder = unsafe { contents.get_unchecked(self.current..) }; ... }`. This collapses the per-byte work from bounds-check + sub + cmp into a single register-register `cmp` whose operand is loop-invariant, and removes the slice construction entirely from the miss path.

findings.title: print_non_negative_float falls back to core::fmt::write for every non-integer number literal
findings.problem: The non-integer branch of `print_non_negative_float` does `self.fmt(format_args!("{}", float))`. That routes through the `FmtAdapter` struct (src/js_printer/lib.rs:1877-1904) → `core::fmt::write` → an un-inlinable function-pointer call to `<f64 as Display>::fmt` → at least one dyn `write_str` back into `FmtAdapter`. This is the standard Rust `format!` slow path, paid for every `ENumber` whose value is fractional or ≥ 2^52 (the integer fast path at :2617 only covers `floor(x)==x && x<2^52`).
findings.evidence: src/js_printer/lib.rs:2611-2635: integer arm uses stack `ItoaBuf` + `self.print(itoa(...))`; the else arm is `let _ = self.fmt(format_args!("{}", float));`. A direct stack-buffer formatter already exists in-tree: `bun_core::fmt::FormatDouble::dtoa(&mut [u8;124], f64) -> &[u8]` (src/bun_core/fmt.rs:3310), backed by WebKit's `WTF__dtoa`. `print_number` (:6925) calls `print_non_negative_float` for every `ENumber` node, so this sits squarely inside `print_expr`'s per-node body.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace line 2634 with a stack buffer + direct print, matching the integer arm: `let mut buf = [0u8; 124]; self.print(bun_core::fmt::FormatDouble::dtoa(&mut buf, float));`. This removes the `core::fmt` indirection and the `FmtAdapter` Result bookkeeping entirely. (If JS-exact exponent formatting is desired this is also more correct than Rust `Display`; if the existing no-exponent behavior must be preserved, use `ryu::Buffer` with `format_finite`, which is likewise a single stack write.)

findings.title: `findchar_fast` is SSE4.2-only; header-name tokenization is byte-by-byte on aarch64
findings.problem: `findchar_fast()` (the SIMD scan used by `parse_token` for header names and `ADVANCE_TOKEN` for the request path) is gated entirely on `#if __SSE4_2__`. Bun's aarch64 targets compile with `-march=armv8-a+crc` / `-mcpu=apple-m1`, so `__SSE4_2__` is undefined and the function degenerates to `*found = 0; return buf;`. `parse_token()` then drops into `while(1){ if(*buf==next_char) break; else if(!token_char_map[*buf]) return err; ++buf; }` — one dependent table load + two branches per byte of every header name, with no unrolling (unlike `get_token_to_eol`, whose `#else` path is 8× unrolled). This is the inner per-byte loop of `parse_headers()` and has no fast path around it on Apple Silicon / Graviton.
findings.evidence: Lines 105–132: body is `#if __SSE4_2__ … #else (void)buf_end; (void)ranges; (void)ranges_size; #endif; return buf;`. `grep -n 'arm_neon|__ARM_NEON|vld1q' picohttpparser.c` returns nothing. Build edge in `build/debug/build.ninja` shows only `-march=haswell` on x64; aarch64 flags in `scripts/build/flags.ts:51-68` are `armv8-a+crc`. `parse_token` fallback loop at lines 264–273 has no unroll.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a NEON (or SWAR) path for `findchar_fast`. A 16-byte `vld1q_u8` + table-lookup (`vqtbl1q_u8`) or simple range-compare chain (`vcgeq_u8`/`vcleq_u8`) mirroring the SSE `_mm_cmpestri` ranges would restore 16-byte strides on ARM. Alternatively, give `parse_token` the same 8× manual unroll that `get_token_to_eol` already has for its non-SSE path.

findings.title: `last_len` is always 0: picohttp’s incremental-reparse guard never fires, headers re-scanned from byte 0 on every fragment
findings.problem: `Response::parse_parts` accepts `offset: Option<&mut usize>`, immediately `.unwrap()`s it, passes `*offset` as `last_len` to `phr_parse_response`, and on `ShortRead` writes `*offset += buf.len()` so the next call can skip already-scanned bytes via `is_complete()` (picohttpparser.c:489). But the only caller (`src/http/lib.rs:3694`) declares `let mut amount_read: usize = 0;` *inside* the per-read loop and discards it on return, so `last_len` is 0 on every call. `is_complete()` is therefore dead and each socket read that delivers a partial header block re-tokenizes the entire accumulated buffer — method, status line, and every header byte already seen — from position 0. Work is O(fragments × total_header_bytes) instead of O(total_header_bytes).
findings.evidence: lib.rs:601 `let offset = offset.unwrap();`, lib.rs:615 `*offset` passed as last_len, lib.rs:625 `*offset += buf.len();`. Caller at `/workspace/bun/src/http/lib.rs:3694`: `let mut amount_read: usize = 0;` is local to the `loop{}` body and the function returns on `ShortRead` (3741). `Response::parse` (lib.rs:657) also hard-codes `offset = 0`. `grep -rn "parse_parts" src/` shows a single call site.
findings.proposed_fix: Persist the previous buffer length across `handle_short_read` cycles (e.g. store it on `self.state`) and pass it into `parse_parts` so `phr_parse_response` can take the `is_complete()` fast path. Also drop the `Option<>` wrapper on `offset` since it is unconditionally unwrapped.

findings.title: consume_comment scans byte-by-byte with no SIMD fast-path for ASCII runs
findings.problem: The comment body loop (`while !self.is_eof() { match self.next_byte_unchecked() { b'*' => ..., b'\n'|FF|'\r' => ..., 0x80..=0xBF => ..., 0xF0..=0xFF => ..., _ => self.advance(1) } }`) examines one byte per iteration. CSS from frameworks (Bootstrap/Tailwind/normalize) routinely contains multi-KB license-header comments that are almost entirely plain ASCII, so thousands of iterations do nothing but `self.position += 1`. The same pattern appears in `skip_whitespace`/`consume_whitespace` (lines 4539, 4938) for indentation runs.
findings.evidence: Lines 5371-5394. The `_ => self.advance(1)` arm is the hot one for ASCII comment bytes. bun_core already ships a SIMD primitive for exactly this class of scan: `bun_core::strings::index_of_newline_or_non_ascii` (src/bun_core/string/immutable.rs:1570) and highway-backed `strings::index_of_any` (immutable.rs:483). These could bulk-advance past uninteresting ASCII until the next `*` / newline / >=0x80 byte, then fall back to the byte-at-a-time handling only for the interesting byte.
findings.proposed_fix: Inside the loop, before the match, call a SIMD helper that returns the offset of the first byte in `src[position..]` that is `*`, `\n`, `\r`, FORM_FEED, or >=0x80; bulk-advance `self.position` by that offset (no line/column bookkeeping needed for the skipped plain-ASCII bytes), then run one iteration of the existing match to handle the interesting byte. `strings::index_of_any(slice, b"*\n\r\x0C")` plus a separate >=0x80 check (or a dedicated 256-bit mask) covers it.

findings.title: to_utf8_from_latin1 scans the ASCII prefix twice on non-ASCII input
findings.problem: For every JS→Rust 8-bit string conversion (WTFStringImplExt::to_utf8 / to_utf8_borrowed / to_utf8_without_ref, ZigString::to_slice), `to_utf8_from_latin1` first calls `is_all_ascii(latin1)` (simdutf validate_ascii, which scans until the first non-ASCII byte and discards the position), and when that returns false it calls `allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list(..., latin1)` whose first step is `first_non_ascii_usize(rest)` — re-scanning the exact same ASCII prefix to recover the position that `is_all_ascii` just threw away. The all-ASCII fast path (single scan, no alloc) is fine; the waste is confined to strings that contain ≥1 high Latin-1 byte (curly quotes, accented characters, ©/®, nbsp, etc.), where the leading ASCII run is walked twice.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1640-1648: `if is_all_ascii(latin1) { return None; } Some(allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list(Vec::with_capacity(latin1.len()), 0, latin1))`; lib.rs:1614-1615: inside allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list, the very first loop iteration runs `match first_non_ascii_usize(rest)` with rest == latin1. Called from the per-string hot path at wtf.rs:63/76/97/128 and mod.rs:1902/1940 (11 call sites, all downstream of String::to_utf8*).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the `is_all_ascii` guard with `let first = first_non_ascii_usize(latin1)?;` (same SIMD primitive, _with_errors variant, same early-out on ASCII — `?` preserves the None fast-path). Then seed the output with `list.extend_from_slice(&latin1[..first])` and enter the encode loop at `&latin1[first..]` (either by slicing before calling allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list, or by adding a variant that accepts the already-known first-non-ASCII offset). Net: one scan of the ASCII prefix instead of two, zero change to the all-ASCII fast path.

findings.title: BunString__fromUTF8 re-validates UTF-8 with the validating converter after validate_utf8 already passed
findings.problem: `BunString__fromUTF8` (reached via `clone_utf8`→`BunString__fromBytes` for every non-ASCII UTF-8 Rust→JS string) does: (1) `simdutf::validate_utf8(bytes, length)` — full validating scan; (2) `simdutf::utf16_length_from_utf8` — full length scan; (3) `simdutf::convert_utf8_to_utf16` — the *validating* converter, which scans and re-validates the same bytes a third time. simdutf provides `convert_valid_utf8_to_utf16` precisely for this already-validated case; the validating variant pays for branchy per-block error checking that is provably dead here. The `BunString__fromBytes` caller also adds a preceding `validate_ascii` scan, so valid-UTF-8 non-ASCII input is walked four times end-to-end before the copy lands.
findings.evidence: BunString.cpp:438 `if (simdutf::validate_utf8(bytes, length)) {` … :445 `RELEASE_ASSERT(simdutf::convert_utf8_to_utf16(bytes, length, ptr.data()) == u16Length);`. simdutf_impl.h:6388 declares `convert_valid_utf8_to_utf16` in the same header already included via wtf/SIMDUTF.h. Caller chain: String::clone_utf8 (mod.rs:226) → BunString__fromBytes (BunString.cpp:501 validate_ascii, :505 → BunString__fromUTF8).
findings.proposed_fix: After the `validate_utf8` branch succeeds, call `simdutf::convert_valid_utf8_to_utf16(bytes, length, ptr.data())` instead of the validating `convert_utf8_to_utf16`. Keep the RELEASE_ASSERT on the returned count == u16Length (convert_valid still returns the write count). This drops one full validating pass over the input with no behavior change; input is immutable so there is no TOCTOU concern.

findings.title: encodeInto(utf16) scans the entire input to compute a length it then discards
findings.problem: `strings::copy_utf16_into_utf8` (called by `TextEncoder__encodeInto16`) computes `element_length_utf16_into_utf8(utf16)` — a full simdutf pass over the whole input — whenever `3*utf16.len() > buf.len()`. The result is used only as the gate `utf8_len <= buf.len()` for the simdutf convert; if the buffer is too small (the normal streaming `encodeInto` case where a large string is encoded into a fixed chunk), the value is discarded and the scalar fallback runs. Cost is O(input_len) per call regardless of how small the output buffer is, so `encodeInto(bigString, smallBuf)` pays to measure the entire string every time even though at most `buf.len()` input units can be consumed.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1777-1783: `let worst_case = utf16.len().saturating_mul(3); let utf8_len = if worst_case <= buf.len() { worst_case } else { element_length_utf16_into_utf8(utf16) }; copy_utf16_into_utf8_with_utf8_len(buf, utf16, utf8_len)`. In the callee (lib.rs:1796), `utf8_len` appears only in `if utf8_len > 0 && utf8_len <= buf.len()`; the scalar loop at 1813-1825 never reads it. Entry point: /workspace/bun/src/runtime/webcore/TextEncoder.rs:283 → /workspace/bun/src/jsc/bindings/webcore/JSTextEncoder.cpp:445.
findings.proposed_fix: When `buf.len() < utf16.len()` (each BMP unit needs ≥1 byte, so the buffer provably cannot hold the full encoding except for pure-surrogate-pair text), skip the full-input length pass and go straight to the partial-fill path. More generally, cap the length scan to the prefix that could possibly fit: `element_length_utf16_into_utf8(&utf16[..buf.len().min(utf16.len())])`, or use simdutf's `convert_utf16le_to_utf8_safe` (bounded-output) to do length-discovery and conversion in one pass.

findings.title: delete_tree: heap allocation per subdirectory for StackItem::name
findings.problem: When descending into a subdirectory, `stack.push(StackItem { name: entry.name.slice_u8().to_vec(), ... })` heap-allocates a fresh `Vec<u8>` for every subdirectory visited. Directory entry names are bounded by NAME_MAX (255 on Linux/macOS, 255 wchar on Windows), so this is a small short-lived heap alloc per directory. The `StackItem` already embeds an 8 KB inline `AlignedBuf` via `WrappedIterator`, so adding ~256 inline bytes for the name would be negligible relative to its existing size and would eliminate the per-directory allocator round-trip.
findings.evidence: dir.rs:130-134 `struct StackItem { name: Vec<u8>, parent_dir: Fd, iter: dir_iterator::WrappedIterator }`; dir.rs:171-175 `stack.push(StackItem { name: entry.name.slice_u8().to_vec(), ... })`. `WrappedIterator` contains `State { buf: AlignedBuf /* 8192 B inline */ , ... }` (lib.rs:258-313).
findings.proposed_fix: Change `StackItem::name` from `Vec<u8>` to `smallvec::SmallVec<[u8; 256]>` (or an inline `[u8; 256]` + len, since NAME_MAX=255). Entry names never exceed this, so the heap path is never taken. This removes one malloc/free pair per subdirectory in recursive deletion.

findings.title: RequestContext::create eagerly looks up the Range header on every request
findings.problem: `create()` unconditionally evaluates `range: RangeRequest::raw_from_request(&Self::any_request(req))`, which calls `req.header(b"range")` — an FFI hop into `uws_req_get_header` + bloom-filter probe — for every request. The stored `self.range` is only ever read on the sendfile/Bun.file path (sole usages at RequestContext.rs:1889/1891). For the dominant in-memory/sync Response case (hello-world bench) this FFI call is pure overhead with no fast-path around it.
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:1349 `range: RangeRequest::raw_from_request(&Self::any_request(req)),` inside `create()`. `rg '\.range\b' RequestContext.rs` shows only lines 1889/1891, both inside the file-response stat path. RangeRequest.rs:127-132 shows the header FFI is hit even on the None path.
findings.proposed_fix: Initialize `range: RangeRequest::Raw::None` in `create()` and move the `raw_from_request(req)` call into `to_async_without_abort_handler` (RequestContext.rs:2334, where `req` is still the live stack handle) so only requests that go async capture it. For the synchronous `new Response(Bun.file(..))` path, `self.req` is still `Some` when `do_sendfile` runs in the same frame, so the lookup can also be done lazily there. The uWS bloom filter makes the miss cheap, but it is still one FFI call + hash saved per sync request.

findings.title: Three unconditional locked atomic RMWs per tick_concurrent_with_count() call
findings.problem: tick_concurrent_with_count() is called ≥3× per tick() (lines 633/643/654/662). Each call unconditionally executes three atomic RMW swaps: (1) `concurrent_ref.swap(0, SeqCst)` at line 543 via update_counts, (2) `imminent_gc_timer.swap(null, SeqCst)` at line 472-474 via run_imminent_gc_timer, (3) `concurrent_tasks.front.swap(null, Acquire)` in pop_batch (unbounded_queue.rs:311). On x86 each is a `lock xchg` (full fence, ~20-40 cycles); none has a load-first fast path for the common zero/null case. That is ≥9 serialized locked RMWs per event-loop tick in the no-concurrent-work steady state. run_imminent_gc_timer is additionally called directly from auto_tick (jsc_hooks.rs:953).
findings.evidence: tick_concurrent_with_count body (lines 482-498) calls update_counts() → swap SeqCst, then run_imminent_gc_timer() → `self.imminent_gc_timer.swap(core::ptr::null_mut(), Ordering::SeqCst)`, then `self.concurrent_tasks.pop_batch()` → `self.front.0.swap(ptr::null_mut(), Ordering::Acquire)` (src/threading/unbounded_queue.rs:311). The only fast-path is the `if count == 0 { return 0 }` at line 500, which is AFTER all three RMWs have already executed.
findings.proposed_fix: Prefix each swap with a cheap load and early-return when already zero/null, e.g. for run_imminent_gc_timer: `if self.imminent_gc_timer.load(Relaxed).is_null() { return; }` before the swap; for pop_batch: `if self.front.0.load(Acquire).is_null() { return batch; }` before the swap. Semantics are unchanged — any value published between the load and the would-be swap is picked up on the next tick_concurrent call (same visibility window the swap already has).

findings.title: newline_in_gap_before() computes self.run(j) before the ASCII test that makes it unnecessary
findings.problem: Inside the per-property `is_single_line` tracking, `newline_in_gap_before` does `let q = self.pos_at(j); let b = self.contents[q]; let run = self.run(j); if (b >= 0x80 || b == 0x0B || b == 0x0C) && self.rest_is_ws_cold(run) { ... }`. `self.run(j)` issues another `pos_at(j+1)` (with its own bounds check) plus a two-sided slice bounds check — and `run` is only used on the rare Unicode-whitespace branch. For minified single-line JSON (npm registry manifests), `is_single_line` stays true for the whole container so `newline_before` → `newline_in_gap_before` runs ~2× per property, wasting one `at()` + one slice-index check on every call.
findings.evidence: Release IR for `newline_in_gap_before`: after loading `b`, block `at.exit17.i` computes `_5.i13 = j+1`, goes through another `fill_to` gate and `bb3.i.i` bounds-check, then `at.exit.i` does the two `slice_index_fail` checks, and ONLY THEN `run.exit` tests `_18 = icmp slt i8 %b, 0` / `%9 = add i8 %b, -11`. The slice work precedes the `b >= 0x80 || b in {0x0B,0x0C}` guard in generated code; LLVM cannot sink it because `pos_at` contains a potential `fill_to(&mut self)` side effect.
findings.proposed_fix: Move the `run` computation inside the exotic-whitespace branch: `if b >= 0x80 || b == 0x0B || b == 0x0C { let run = self.run(j); if self.rest_is_ws_cold(run) { ...; continue; } } return self.contents[q + 1..hi].iter().any(...)`. Semantics are identical (the current `&&` already short-circuits on the same condition). Also consider tagging `newline_in_gap_before` `#[cold]` since pretty-printed package.json (the thousands-per-install case) hits it only once per container.

findings.title: parse_number() calls #[cold] rest_is_ws_cold unconditionally — no empty-tail fast path
findings.problem: After `parse_number_text` consumes `used` bytes of `full_run`, the trailing-junk check is `if !self.rest_is_ws_cold(&full_run[used..])`. In the overwhelmingly common case `used == full_run.len()` (number followed immediately by a structural byte — `,`/`]`/`}` in minified JSON) this still issues an out-of-line call to a `#[cold]` function just to observe an empty slice. The sibling helper `run_rest_is_ws` (line 588) already has the `run.len() == n ||` fast path for `true`/`false`/`null`; numbers were left without it. `#[cold]` also places the callee in .text.unlikely and biases the branch predictor against the call returning true.
findings.evidence: Release IR inside `parse_scalar`→`parse_number` (bb18.i): `_26.i = sub %_16.i7.i, %_21.1.i` followed directly by `call @...rest_is_ws_cold(ptr %_30.i, i64 %_26.i)` with no `icmp eq %_26.i, 0` guard. The cold function itself (`define internal ... #6`) is out-of-line.
findings.proposed_fix: Add the same fast path used for keywords: `if used != full_run.len() && !self.rest_is_ws_cold(&full_run[used..]) { return Err(self.number_trailing_junk(start + used)); }`. Same change applies at json_stage2.rs:935 in `parse_negative_number_at`. Also note `parse_number` re-invokes `self.run(cursor)` (line 899) and `self.pos_at(cursor)` (line 900) even though `parse_scalar` already computed both at line 565 and `parse_json_value` computed `start` at line 442 — passing `run`/`start` in would remove three redundant `at()` lookups per number.

findings.title: Redundant `readableStreamHasDefaultReader(stream) &&` guard before null-safe `readableStreamGetNumReadRequests`
findings.problem: The pattern `readableStreamHasDefaultReader(stream) && readableStreamGetNumReadRequests(stream) > 0` appears in the per-chunk enqueue body (line 593) and in `readableStreamDefaultControllerShouldCallPull` (line 555), both of which run once per enqueued/read chunk. `readableStreamGetNumReadRequests` (ReadableStreamOperations.cpp:182-187) is deliberately null-safe — it re-calls `readableStreamHasDefaultReader` internally and returns 0 when there is no default reader — so the outer guard is dead. Both helpers are out-of-line (defined in ReadableStreamOperations.cpp, called from a different TU), and each invocation of `readableStreamHasDefaultReader` loads `stream->m_reader`, loads the reader's Structure, loads its classInfo, and compares against `JSReadableStreamBYOBReader::info()`. With the trailing `callPullIfNeeded → shouldCallPull`, a single `controller.enqueue()` with a waiting reader evaluates `readableStreamHasDefaultReader` up to 5 times (2× here, 1× inside `readableStreamFulfillReadRequest` at ReadableStreamOperations.cpp:222, 2× in `shouldCallPull`). The same redundant pattern exists at JSReadableByteStreamController.cpp:662/664 and JSDirectStreamController.cpp:506/673.
findings.evidence: JSReadableStreamDefaultController.cpp:593 `if (readableStreamHasDefaultReader(stream) && readableStreamGetNumReadRequests(stream) > 0)`; ReadableStreamOperations.cpp:182-187 `size_t readableStreamGetNumReadRequests(JSReadableStream* stream) { if (!readableStreamHasDefaultReader(stream)) [[unlikely]] return 0; ... }`; JSReadableStreamReaderBase.cpp:9-12 `bool isBYOB() const { return classInfo() == JSReadableStreamBYOBReader::info(); }` — a structure+classInfo chase per call, not a cached bool.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the `readableStreamHasDefaultReader(stream) &&` / `readableStreamHasBYOBReader(stream) &&` prefixes at all 6 sites (the null-safe count helper already covers them). Alternatively, move `readableStreamHasDefaultReader` / `readableStreamGetNumReadRequests` into WebStreamsInternals.h as `inline` so the compiler can CSE within a function body; or cache a `bool m_isBYOB` on `JSReadableStreamReaderBase` to make `isBYOB()` a single byte load instead of a classInfo chase.

findings.title: best_quote_char_for_string does a separate byte-by-byte pre-scan of every string literal
findings.problem: Every `EString` printed (src/js_printer/lib.rs:4028 → `print_string_literal_e_string` → `best_quote_char_for_e_string` → here) first walks up to min(len,1024) bytes with a 6-arm `match` to count quote occurrences, then the entire string is walked again by `write_pre_quoted_string_inner` to emit it. The pre-scan is a scalar `while i < n { match str[i].into() { ... } }` loop with `T: Into<u32>` generic widening on every byte — not SIMD-friendly. For the overwhelmingly common case (no `'`/`"`/`` ` ``/`$`/`\n`/`\` in the literal) all three cost counters stay 0 and the answer is trivially `"`, so the whole scan is wasted work.
findings.evidence: Loop body at :824-845; called from `best_quote_char_for_e_string` (:2596) which is reached for every `ExprData::EString` in `print_expr` (:4028) and every UTF-8 key in `print_string_literal_utf8` (:3054). No early-exit and no SIMD `index_of_any` gate, unlike e.g. `write_pre_quoted_string_inner` which uses `strings::index_of_needs_escape_for_java_script_string` to skip escape-free runs.
findings.proposed_fix: Gate the counting loop with a SIMD `index_of_any(str, b"'\"`$\n\\")` (or `first_non_ascii`-style helper) — if none found in the first min(len,1024) bytes, return `b'"'` immediately. Alternatively fuse quote-choice into the first chunk of the escaping pass so the bytes are only read once.

findings.title: Single-packet chunked path does two memcpys on the -2 (needs-more-data) branch
findings.problem: `handle_response_body_chunked_encoding_from_single_packet` is selected when `incoming_data.len() <= 16K && body_buffer.is_empty()`. When reached from `on_data` in the BodyChunk stage (line 3951), `incoming_data` is the socket recv buffer, so `response_message_buffer.owns(incoming_data)` is false and the bytes are first copied into the static `small` scratch buffer (copy #1, line 4913). `phr_decode_chunked` runs in-place on `small`. If the result is -2 (chunk not yet complete — the normal case for any chunked body larger than one TCP read), the decoded prefix is then copied again into the body buffer via `append_slice_exact` (copy #2, line 4944). The sibling `..._from_multiple_packets` path does the same work with one copy (append raw → decode in place in the body buffer). The single-packet path is only a win when the entire chunked body completes in that packet; on -2 it is strictly an extra memcpy of up to 16 KB. For non-streaming this is once per response (first body packet); for streaming consumers that drain `body_out_str` between callbacks, the body buffer is empty on every packet and this double-copy is paid per TCP read.
findings.evidence: src/http/lib.rs:4900-4915 chooses `small[..].copy_from_slice(incoming_data)` when `owns()` is false (always false for direct on_data reads). Lines 4942-4944 on pret==-2: `self.state.get_body_buffer().append_slice_exact(buffer)?;` — second copy. Compare `..._from_multiple_packets` 4818-4843: one `append_slice` then in-place decode + truncate. Dispatch at 4801 routes ≤16 KB + empty-body-buf through the single-packet path.
findings.proposed_fix: On the pret==-2 branch of the single-packet path, avoid the second copy by letting the multi-packet strategy handle continuation: e.g. restrict the single-packet fast path to the 'done' case only (append raw incoming_data to body_buf and decode there when body_buf is empty, reserving the static `small` buffer for the genuinely-complete-in-one-packet case). Alternatively, change the dispatch at line 4801 so the single-packet path is only taken when there is reason to believe the body is complete (e.g., incoming_data ends with `0\r\n\r\n`).

findings.title: `Header::value()`/`name()` pay a release-mode null-check + assert that is dead for parsed headers
findings.problem: `Header::name()` and `Header::value()` both call `bun_core::ffi::slice`, which on every invocation branches on `ptr.is_null()` and, on the null arm, executes a release-mode `assert!(len == 0, …)`. For headers returned by `phr_parse_response`/`phr_parse_headers`, `value` is always a pointer into `buf` (picohttpparser.c:349 `headers[i].value = value;`), and `name` is only NULL for obs-fold lines — which `Response::parse_parts` rejects at lib.rs:635 before the header list is ever exposed. So every per-header read in `HeaderList::get`, `get_if_other_is_absent`, `clone`, and `count` pays a provably-dead branch that also blocks LLVM from assuming non-null on the raw-pointer field. The doc comment at line 145 already concedes the check is “Defensive”.
findings.evidence: `ffi::slice` at `/workspace/bun/src/opaque/lib.rs:401-412`: `if ptr.is_null() { assert!(len == 0, …); &[] } else { from_raw_parts(ptr, len) }`. `HeaderList::get` (lib.rs:247-253) calls `header.name()`/`header.value()` once per header per lookup; several lookups run per response (e.g. S3 path does `.get(b"etag")`, `.get(b"last-modified")`, `.get(b"content-type")` sequentially over the same list).
findings.proposed_fix: Provide `#[inline(always)]` unchecked accessors (or change `value()` and post-filter `name()` to call `core::slice::from_raw_parts` directly with a `debug_assert!(!ptr.is_null())`). Keep `ffi::slice` only in `Header::ZERO`/construction paths where null is actually possible.

findings.title: consume_until_end_of_block heap-allocates a Vec on every call
findings.problem: `let mut stack: Vec<BlockType> = Vec::with_capacity(16); stack.push(block_type);` performs a heap allocation every time a block is skipped. The inline comment says "Vec is fine for the cold path", but this function is reached from `parse_until_before`'s tail loop (line 564), `parse_until_after` (line 596), `Parser::skip_whitespace` (line 4031), and `next_including_whitespace_and_comments` (line 4088). On backtracking / unknown-property fallback it fires once per block-opening token in the skipped range. `BlockType` is a 1-byte Copy enum, so the Vec holds ≤16 bytes of payload.
findings.evidence: Line 381: `Vec::with_capacity(16)`. No SmallVec / stack array; every invocation goes to the global allocator (mimalloc) and frees on return.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with `smallvec::SmallVec<[BlockType; 16]>` (already a dep across the workspace) or a plain `[BlockType; 32]` + `u8` depth counter, falling back to a Vec only on overflow. This removes the per-call malloc/free without changing behavior.

findings.title: BunString::transferToJS does a ref()+deref() round-trip instead of adopting the existing +1
findings.problem: `transferToJS` is the primary Rust→JS string hand-off (59 Rust call sites via transfer_to_js). Its `[[likely]]` WTFStringImpl branch does `auto str = this->toWTFString();` — which hits `WTF::String(this->impl.wtf)` (line 806) and increments the refcount — then immediately `this->impl.wtf->deref();`. The debug ASSERTs at lines 130/136 document this: refcount goes +1 then -1. The net operation is a *transfer* of the caller's existing +1 into a WTF::String, which `adoptRef` expresses without touching the counter at all. Two avoidable refcount ops per transferred string in the hottest branch.
findings.evidence: BunString.cpp:128-140: `auto str = this->toWTFString(); … ASSERT(newRefCount == refCount + 1); this->impl.wtf->deref(); … return jsString(vm, WTF::move(str));`. BunString.cpp:805-807 (`toWTFString()` WTF arm) returns `WTF::String(this->impl.wtf)`; WTFString.h:433 shows that constructor stores into `m_impl` (a RefPtr), which refs.
findings.proposed_fix: In the WTFStringImpl branch, build the String via adoption: `auto str = WTF::String(adoptRef(*this->impl.wtf)); *this = { .tag = BunStringTag::Dead }; return jsString(vm, WTF::move(str));`. This transfers the existing +1 into `str` with zero refcount traffic and lets the debug ASSERT block be dropped. (The non-WTF fallthrough at lines 143-145 is unaffected.)

findings.title: Buffer.from(latin1, 'utf8') encodes non-ASCII bytes scalar with per-byte reserve(2) instead of simdutf convert_latin1_to_utf8
findings.problem: `allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list` (reached from `Buffer.from(str, 'utf8')` for 8-bit JSStrings via JSBuffer.cpp:575 → `Bun__encoding__constructFromLatin1` → encoding.rs:804) locates ASCII runs with simdutf `first_non_ascii_usize`, but then encodes each non-ASCII byte one-at-a-time with `list.reserve(2); list.push(a); list.push(b)`. The `reserve(2)` executes inside the per-byte inner loop, and the initial `list.reserve(latin1.len())` is only the lower bound so the Vec still regrows mid-encode. The project already wraps `simdutf::convert_latin1_to_utf8` (/workspace/bun/src/simdutf_sys/simdutf.rs:279) and `utf8_length_from_latin1` (simdutf.rs:630), which would make this a two-pass fully-SIMD conversion with no per-byte branching or growth checks.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:1606-1636: `list.reserve(latin1.len()); … while let Some(&c) = rest.first() { if c < 0x80 { break } list.reserve(2); let [a,b] = latin1_to_codepoint_bytes_assume_not_ascii(c); list.push(a); list.push(b); rest = &rest[1..]; }`. Caller chain: /workspace/bun/src/bun_core/string/immutable/unicode.rs:199 → /workspace/bun/src/runtime/webcore/encoding.rs:804 (`construct_from_u8::<UTF8>`) → /workspace/bun/src/jsc/bindings/JSBuffer.cpp:575 (`constructFromEncoding`, utf8 branch).
findings.proposed_fix: Compute `simdutf::length::utf8::from::latin1(latin1)` once, reserve exactly that, then call `simdutf::convert::latin1::to::utf8(latin1, spare)` into the Vec's spare capacity and `set_len`. This removes the per-byte `reserve(2)`/`push` and vectorizes the whole conversion. (If exact sizing is undesirable, at minimum hoist the reserve out of the inner loop by reserving `2 * latin1.len()` up front.)

findings.title: ensure_url runs a full WHATWG URL parse (with WTFStringImpl alloc) on every async request
findings.problem: `to_async` → `to_async_without_abort_handler` → `ensure_url()` runs for every request whose handler returns a pending Promise. After assembling `protocol + host + req_url` into a stack buffer that is already well-formed, it calls `bun_url::href_from_string(&BunString::from_bytes(url))` → FFI `URL__getHref` (BunString.cpp:654) which (a) copies the stack bytes into a new WTFStringImpl via `toWTFString()`, (b) runs the full WTF::URL parser, (c) allocates/returns another WTFStringImpl. For the overwhelmingly common case (`http://<ascii-host>/<ascii-path>` with no chars needing percent-encoding), the output equals the input and the whole parse+2 allocs is redundant normalization.
findings.evidence: Request.rs:985-1014 assembles the URL in a 128-byte stack buffer from `get_protocol()` (constant), `host` (already validated by `is_valid_host_header`), and `req_url` (leading `/`). Line 1001 then feeds it to `href_from_string`. BunString.cpp:654-662 shows `URL__getHref` always constructs `WTF::URL(str)` and `toStringRef`. Additionally the `core::ptr::eq(href.byte_slice().as_ptr(), url.as_ptr())` branch at line 1003 can never be true (the returned StringImpl is heap, `url` is stack), so `clone_latin1` there is dead code.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a cheap canonical-check before calling `href_from_string`: if `host` is all lowercase-ASCII (it already passed `is_valid_host_header`) and `req_url` contains only bytes that the WHATWG path parser leaves untouched (a 256-byte LUT, same set WTF::URL uses), skip the parser entirely and go straight to `BunString::clone_latin1(url)` / `create_uninitialized_latin1`. Fall back to `href_from_string` only when normalization might actually change bytes. Also delete the dead `core::ptr::eq` branch.

findings.title: Redundant per-item ensure_unused_capacity(1) after bulk reserve in concurrent-task transfer loop
findings.problem: In tick_concurrent_with_count(), capacity for the whole batch is pre-reserved at line 506 (`self.tasks.ensure_unused_capacity(count)`), but the per-item body at line 529 calls `self.tasks.write_item(task_ref.task)`, which internally calls `ensure_unused_capacity(1)` again (linear_fifo.rs:572-575) — a `writable_length() >= 1` check (buf_len load + count load + sub + cmp + branch) per concurrent task. The comment at line 527 says 'write_item is the public path', but `write_item_assume_capacity` is also `pub` (linear_fifo.rs:578), so the redundant check can be skipped.
findings.evidence: Line 506: `let _ = self.tasks.ensure_unused_capacity(count);` then loop body line 529: `let _ = self.tasks.write_item(task_ref.task);`. linear_fifo.rs:572: `pub fn write_item(&mut self, item: T) -> Result<(), AllocError> { self.ensure_unused_capacity(1)?; self.write_item_assume_capacity(item); Ok(()) }`. linear_fifo.rs:578: `pub fn write_item_assume_capacity(&mut self, item: T)` is public.
findings.proposed_fix: Change line 529 to `self.tasks.write_item_assume_capacity(task_ref.task);` (drop the `let _ =`). Keep the line-506 bulk reserve. This saves one capacity re-check per concurrent task drained.

findings.title: parse_number_text() runs core::str::from_utf8 over bytes it just proved are ASCII
findings.problem: The float / ≥10-digit path does `core::str::from_utf8(digits).ok().and_then(|s| s.parse::<f64>().ok())`. `digits` was assembled byte-by-byte from the `digits!()` macro which only admits `b'0'..=b'9' | b'_'` plus the manually-checked `.`, `e|E`, `+|-` — every byte is < 0x80 by construction. `from_utf8` therefore performs a redundant O(n) validation scan before the actual `f64` parse. Fires for any number with a decimal point or exponent (e.g. semver-ish floats) or ≥10 integer digits (large `unpackedSize` in npm manifests).
findings.evidence: json_stage2.rs:983-1036 — the `digits!()` loop only accepts `b'0'..=b'9'` and `b'_'`; `.`/`e`/`E`/`+`/`-` are checked explicitly. No byte ≥ 0x80 can reach `text`. `from_utf8` on this slice is a pure re-scan. (`parse_radix_number` at line 1137 has the same pattern but is already `#[cold]`.)
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with `unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(digits) }.parse::<f64>()` (the invariant is locally provable), or keep it safe by writing the digits into a `&mut [u8]` of a stack `str` via `std::str::from_utf8` only in `cfg(debug_assertions)`. Either way the redundant scan disappears from the hot path.

findings.title: `drainQueueEntriesInto` takes/releases the controller cellLock once per queued chunk, plus a redundant post-loop lock+resetQueue
findings.problem: `drainQueueEntriesInto` is the per-chunk inner loop for `readMany()` and the buffered-consumer pump (`readableStreamDefaultReaderFillFromQueue`, used by `.text()/.bytes()/.json()/.blob()`). For each of N queued entries it acquires the controller's `cellLock()`, peeks `first()`, calls `removeFirst()`, and releases — N uncontended lock CAS cycles. After the loop (lines 225-231) it acquires the lock an (N+1)th time to call `resetQueue()`, which does `m_queue.clear()` on a deque that is already empty (every element was removed in the loop) plus zeroes `m_totalSize`. Only the `m_totalSize = 0` is needed, and `m_totalSize` is a plain `double` that the GC visitor never touches, so it needs no lock. For the default-controller arm (lines 216-219) nothing between `first()` and `removeFirst()` can GC-allocate, so all entry values could be drained into a stack `MarkedArgumentBuffer` under a single lock, then `putDirectIndex`'d outside it.
findings.evidence: Loop body at JSReadableStreamDefaultReader.cpp:198-223 takes `WTF::Locker locker { ...cellLock() }` on every iteration and calls `removeFirst(locker)`; lines 225-231 then take the lock again to call `resetQueue(locker)` whose body (StreamQueue.h:161-165) is `m_queue.clear(); m_totalSize = 0;` — `clear()` is a no-op on the already-emptied deque.
findings.proposed_fix: For the default-controller branch, drain all `entry.value.get()` into a local `MarkedArgumentBuffer` under one `Locker` scope and then `resetQueue` in that same scope; run the `putDirectIndex` loop afterwards without the lock. At minimum, replace the post-loop `Locker + resetQueue()` with a lock-free `controller->m_queue.setTotalSize(0)` (StreamQueue.h:189) since the deque is already empty.

findings.title: `HTTPServerWritable::write_latin1` scans the chunk for ASCII twice on the backpressure path
findings.problem: In the per-chunk `write_latin1` hot path, the `buffer.len()==0 && len>=HWM` arm first runs `strings::is_all_ascii(bytes)` (full SIMD scan). If the chunk IS all-ASCII but `self.send(bytes)` returns false (socket backpressure / no-res), it falls through to `self.buffer.write_latin1(bytes)`, which calls `allocate_latin1_into_utf8_with_list` (bun_core/lib.rs:1606) and invokes `first_non_ascii_usize(rest)` — a second full SIMD scan of the same bytes that we already proved are all-ASCII. The second scan is pure waste; a plain `self.buffer.write(bytes)` (memcpy, no scan) would be equivalent for the known-ASCII case.
findings.evidence: streams.rs:1708-1720: `if strings::is_all_ascii(bytes) { if self.send(bytes) { return ... } do_send = false; }  if self.buffer.write_latin1(bytes).is_err() { ... }` — when `is_all_ascii` was true but `send` failed, `write_latin1` re-scans via `first_non_ascii_usize` (vec_ext.rs:612-617 → bun_core/lib.rs:1614-1618).
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the `is_all_ascii` result and branch on it for the buffer write: when true, call `self.buffer.write(bytes)` (no transcoding scan); when false, call `self.buffer.write_latin1(bytes)`. This keeps the send fast-path and removes the redundant scan on backpressure.

findings.title: Dead store `self.token = T::TEndOfFile` at the top of every token-loop iteration
findings.problem: Every iteration of the `next()` loop (and `next_inside_jsx_element` at line 2913, `next_jsx_element_child` at line 3196) begins with `self.token = T::TEndOfFile;`. Every match arm then either overwrites `self.token` before returning, or `continue`s (whitespace/newlines/comments) back to the same store. The `-1` arm redundantly writes `T::TEndOfFile` again. LLVM can DSE the store on the whitespace/punctuator arms, but it CANNOT prove the store is dead before the `#[inline(never)]` calls (`parse_string_literal_inner`, `scan_single_line_comment`, `scan_multi_line_comment_body`, `scan_identifier_with_escapes`) because it cannot see that those do not read `self.token`. So one extra `mov` to memory survives per string literal, per comment, and per escaped identifier.
findings.evidence: Line 1538 `self.token = T::TEndOfFile;`; line 1542 `-1 => { self.token = T::TEndOfFile; }` (duplicate). Audit of all arms in `next()` (lines 1540-2229): every arm either assigns `self.token` before reaching `return Ok(())` / `break`, or takes `continue`. No arm relies on the line-1538 default.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete line 1538 (and 2913, 3196). The `-1` arm already sets it explicitly, and every other arm that returns sets it. Alternatively, if kept for defensive reasons, move it inside only the `-1` arm so it is not re-executed on every whitespace-skip iteration.

findings.title: next_byte_unchecked / byte_at do checked indexing despite every caller proving the bound
findings.problem: `next_byte_unchecked` is `self.src[self.position]` (panicking index) and `byte_at(n)` is `self.src[self.position + n]`. Every per-byte inner loop — `consume_name` (4980/4984), `consume_whitespace` (4938/4940), `skip_whitespace` (4540/4542), `consume_quoted_string` (5044/5048), `consume_comment` (5371/5372), `consume_numeric` digit loops (4836, 4854, 4884) — guards the call with `!self.is_eof()` or `has_at_least(n)` immediately beforehand, so the slice bounds check is provably redundant on the hot per-byte path. If LLVM fails to prove the invariant (the two checks load `self.position` and `self.src.len()` through `&mut self` with an intervening store to `self.position`), every byte of CSS input pays an extra compare+branch and carries a cold panic edge that inhibits further optimization.
findings.evidence: Lines 5524-5526: `pub fn next_byte_unchecked(&self) -> u8 { self.src[self.position] }` — the `_unchecked` name suggests the author intended `get_unchecked`. Lines 5502-5504: `byte_at` likewise. Upstream rust-cssparser relies on the same pattern and LLVM usually elides it, so this may already optimize out; flagged because it is the single operation executed most often (once per input byte) and the "unchecked" naming implies intent that isn't implemented.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) leave as-is if release asm confirms the check is elided, or (b) change to `*self.src.get_unchecked(self.position)` inside a debug-asserted unsafe block, matching the function name. Same for `byte_at`. The caller invariant (`is_eof()` / `has_at_least()` precedes every call in the hot loops) makes the unsafe locally provable.

findings.title: Empty-regular-file fast path on POSIX uses stale `file_store.mode` and never fires for fresh Bun.file()
findings.problem: After `resolve_size_and_last_modified` has fstat'd the fd and set `self.could_block = !is_regular_file(stat.st_mode)`, the empty-file short-circuit tests `bun_sys::is_regular_file(self.file_store.mode)`. `self.file_store` is a clone taken in `create_with_ctx` from the Blob's store *before* any stat, and `File::init` defaults `mode = 0`. `is_regular_file(0)` → `kind_from_mode(0) == Unknown` → false, so for `Bun.file(path).text()` on a 0-byte file the fast path is never taken: we fall through, allocate a 16-byte buffer, zero the 64 KB stack buffer, and issue a read() that returns 0. The Windows equivalent (line 1262) correctly uses `this.is_regular_file` which was set from the fresh fstat at line 1240.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:738 `if self.size == 0 && bun_sys::is_regular_file(self.file_store.mode)` — but `file_store` is populated at read_file.rs:346 `let file_store = store.data.as_file().clone();` and `resolve_size_and_last_modified` (lines 672-722) never writes `self.file_store.mode`; it only sets `self.could_block` from `stat.st_mode`. `File::default()` at src/jsc/webcore_types.rs:783 sets `mode: 0`. Compare ReadFileUV at line 1262: `if this.size == 0 && this.is_regular_file` (correct — `is_regular_file` set at line 1240 from fresh fstat).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the condition with `if self.size == 0 && !self.could_block` (which is exactly `is_regular_file(fresh_stat.st_mode)`), mirroring the Windows branch. This restores the intended short-circuit: skip buffer alloc and read() for 0-byte regular files.
findings.user_visible_impact: `await Bun.file(emptyFile).text()` / `.bytes()` on Linux/macOS does an unnecessary 16-byte heap allocation, a 64 KB memset, and one extra read() syscall per call. In build-tool / glob workloads that touch many empty placeholder files, each such read pays full overhead instead of returning immediately after fstat.

findings.title: Fast-path guard for stat'd Blob destinations is inverted (checks ==RegularFile instead of !=)
findings.problem: `fast_path_ok` intends (per the original Zig comment: 'Is this a file that is known to be a pipe? Let's avoid blocking the main thread on it.') to SKIP the main-thread sync fast path when the destination is a known pipe/TTY. The code instead skips it when the destination is a known REGULAR FILE: `f.mode != 0 && bun_core::kind_from_mode(f.mode) == bun_core::FileKind::File`. The condition is inverted. Result: a Blob whose mode has been populated (Bun.stdout/Bun.stderr, or any Bun.file() after `.size`/`.stat()`) and is a regular file is forced onto the async threadpool even for <256 KiB writes, while a Blob known to be a pipe/TTY takes the synchronous path the comment was trying to avoid. Bug originates in Zig PR #7470 and was ported faithfully.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:5102-5107 vs. the Zig source (`git show b1c8ae97ff -- src/bun.js/webcore/blob.zig`) which carries the comment 'known to be a pipe' immediately above `bun.isRegularFile(...)`. RareData::stdout()/stderr() (src/jsc/rare_data.rs:907-934) fstat and set `mode`, so Bun.stdout/Bun.stderr always hit the guarded branch.
findings.proposed_fix: Flip the comparison to `!= bun_core::FileKind::File` (equivalently `!bun_sys::is_regular_file(f.mode)`), so the sync fast path is skipped only when the destination is known NOT to be a regular file.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.write(Bun.stdout, shortBuf)` when stdout is redirected to a file (e.g. `bun run script.js > out`) pays threadpool-dispatch + promise-microtask latency (≈10–20 µs) instead of a ~1 µs synchronous write; any `const f=Bun.file(p); await f.size; await Bun.write(f, small)` pattern likewise loses the fast path.

findings.title: EAGAIN retry in WriteFile::do_write never re-issues the write() — infinite busy-loop
findings.problem: `do_write` computes `let result = sys::write(fd, ...)` at line 347 *outside* the `loop { match &result { ... } }` at line 350. On `Err` with errno == RETRY (EAGAIN) and `!self.could_block`, it executes `continue` (line 361), which re-matches the SAME stale `result` forever — the syscall is never retried. `could_block` is computed from `file.pathlike.is_fd()` (write_file.rs:462) so any path-opened destination has `could_block=false`; if that path resolves to a FIFO/pipe/char-device opened O_NONBLOCK and the kernel returns EAGAIN, the threadpool worker spins at 100% CPU. The identical bug exists in the pre-port Zig (`const result = bun.sys.write(...)` outside `while (true)`), so it is latent but real.
findings.evidence: write_file.rs:347-373: `let result = sys::write(...); loop { match &result { ... Err(err) => { if err.get_errno()==io::RETRY { if !self.could_block { continue; } ... } } } break; }`. `result` is never reassigned inside the loop body.
findings.proposed_fix: Move the `sys::write(fd, ...)` call inside the loop (making `result` mutable), or replace `continue` with `result = sys::write(fd, &self.bytes_blob.shared_view()[off..off+len]); continue;` so EAGAIN actually retries the syscall.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.write(Bun.file('/tmp/my_fifo'), bigBuf)` (or any path that resolves to a non-regular file) hangs a threadpool worker at 100% CPU once the pipe buffer fills, instead of waiting for writability; user sees the promise never resolve and one core pegged.

findings.title: response.text() skips the to_blob_if_possible() fast path that json()/arrayBuffer()/bytes() take for already-buffered streams
findings.problem: In BodyMixin::get_text, the `Value::Locked` + readable-stream branch goes straight to `locked.set_promise(.., Action::GetText, Some(readable))`, which dispatches into C++ `readableStreamToText` (JS property `get`, `JSC::call`, promise-then plumbing in BunStreamConsumers.cpp:875-911). Every sibling — get_json (line 1896), get_array_buffer (line 1946), get_bytes (line 2001), get_blob (line 2154) — first calls `value.to_blob_if_possible()`, which for a Locked body with a native ByteStream/ByteBlobLoader that already holds the full payload (ReadableStream.rs:194-203) converts to `Value::InternalBlob` and falls through to the synchronous `use_as_any_blob_* → to_*` path. `get_text` is the only consumer missing this call, so `.text()` on a Response/Request whose body is a completed native stream (e.g. `new Response(stream)` constructed from a Blob- or Bytes-backed ReadableStream, or after `.body` was touched) always takes the slow C++ stream-consumer path while `.json()` on the same object resolves synchronously in Rust.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `value.to_blob_if_possible();` before the `if let Value::Locked(locked) = value` check in `get_text` (both the with-readable and no-readable Locked arms), mirroring get_json/get_array_buffer/get_bytes. Then the already-buffered case falls through to `use_as_any_blob_allow_non_utf8_string() → AnyBlob::to_string(Transfer)`.
findings.user_visible_impact: `.text()` on a Response/Request backed by a native ReadableStream whose data is already buffered (e.g. `new Response(blobStream).text()` or `.text()` after touching `.body`) takes the C++ readableStreamToText path (handle->get + JSC::call + promise chaining) instead of the zero-JS-call native path. A microbenchmark of `new Response(someCompletedByteStream)` would show `.text()` measurably slower than `.json()`/`.arrayBuffer()` for the same payload, even though `.text()` should be the cheapest of the three.

findings.title: Per-write version bump defeats the cached row Structure for every statement on the DB
findings.problem: After every sqlite3_step(), Bun does `if (!sqlite3_stmt_readonly(stmt)) castedThis->version_db->version++;` and then checks `need_update()` (== `version_db->version != this->version`). Any INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE therefore increments the shared DB version, which makes `need_update()` return true for *every* prepared statement on that DB on its next execution, forcing `initializeColumnNames()` to run again. That function heap-allocates a fresh `PropertyNameArrayBuilder`, clears `_structure`, calls `sqlite3_column_name` / `String::fromUTF8ReplacingInvalidSequences` / `Identifier::fromString` per column, and rebuilds the JSC Structure via `addPropertyTransition`. The intent (commit bcc4580 "invalidate column name caches when the schema of table may change") was to catch ALTER TABLE, but plain DML does not change column names — so the expensive fast-path cache (`_structure` used by `putDirectOffset` in `constructResultObject`) is thrown away on every write. Worse, because the increment happens *before* the `need_update()` check, the write statement itself rebuilds its own (usually empty) column set on every `.run()`.
findings.proposed_fix: Only bump `version_db->version` for schema-changing statements. A cheap accurate check is available via SQLite: cache `sqlite3_column_count(stmt)` on the statement and compare after step (SQLite auto-reprepares on SQLITE_SCHEMA so the count/name changes become visible), or gate the bump on `sqlite3_stmt_readonly(stmt)==0 && sqlite3_stmt_isexplain(stmt)==0 && sqlite3_column_count(stmt)==0 && <statement touches sqlite_schema>`. Simplest correct fix: drop the unconditional `version++` on every write; instead, in `initializeColumnNames`, skip the rebuild when `sqlite3_column_count` and the first column name still match the cache. At minimum, move `update_version()` before the increment so a write statement doesn't invalidate *itself* every call.
findings.user_visible_impact: In the canonical ORM pattern `for each row { insert.run(row); }` followed by or interleaved with `select.all()`, every `select.all()` pays the full cost of re-deriving column Identifiers and re-creating the JSC Structure (one heap alloc + N UTF-8 decodes + N Structure transitions) instead of reusing the cache, and every `insert.run()` pays an extra heap alloc for a throwaway PropertyNameArrayBuilder. This adds measurable overhead proportional to column count on every query in any workload that mixes reads and writes on the same Database.

findings.title: Statement#run() with no args allocates the {changes, lastInsertRowid} object twice
findings.problem: For statements with `paramsCount > 0`, `this.run` is wired to `#run`. When the user calls `.run()` with no arguments (re-executing with previously bound params), `#run` calls `this.#runNoArgs()` — which already builds and returns a `{changes, lastInsertRowid}` object — then discards that return value and calls `createChangesObject()` a second time. Two objects are allocated where one suffices.
findings.proposed_fix: Change the zero-arg branch of `#run` to `return this.#runNoArgs();`.
findings.user_visible_impact: One extra `{changes, lastInsertRowid}` allocation (and two extra internal-field reads) per `.run()` call on parameterized statements invoked without arguments.

findings.title: onwrite `needTick` tests kObjectMode bit instead of kDestroyed (operator-precedence bug preserved with Number())
findings.problem: The expression `state[kState] & Number(kDestroyed !== 0)` evaluates to `state[kState] & 1`, which is the kObjectMode bit (1<<0), not kDestroyed (1<<4). The original Node.js line `(state[kState] & kDestroyed !== 0)` has the same precedence bug; Bun wrapped it in `Number()` (likely to silence a lint/TS warning) instead of fixing the parenthesization. Result: for objectMode Writables with a synchronous `_write` and no user callback (e.g. every objectMode Transform/PassThrough), `needTick` is always truthy, so the fast-path `state.pendingcb--` is never taken on the first write of each tick and a `process.nextTick(afterWrite, ...)` is scheduled instead. Conversely, for destroyed non-objectMode streams the needed afterWrite tick is skipped.
findings.evidence: kObjectMode = 1<<0 and kDestroyed = 1<<4 (src/js/internal/streams/utils.ts:23,27). `Number(kDestroyed !== 0)` === 1. Instrumented run: `new Writable({objectMode:true, write(c,e,cb){cb()}})` + one `.write(obj)` per setImmediate turn → 5000 writes schedule 5000 `process.nextTick(afterWrite)` calls; with objectMode:false it schedules 0. Benchmark of 50k such writes in Bun: objectMode=true 102ms vs objectMode=false 80ms (≈27% overhead attributable solely to the extra nextTick+afterWrite per chunk). Node upstream still has the same bug (`(state[kState] & kDestroyed !== 0)`), so fixing it would make Bun faster than Node here.
findings.proposed_fix: Change line 618 to `const needTick = needDrain || (state[kState] & kDestroyed) !== 0 || cb !== nop;`.
findings.user_visible_impact: objectMode Transform/PassThrough streams (and any objectMode Writable with a sync _write) pay one extra process.nextTick + afterWrite invocation per chunk when chunks arrive one-per-tick (the normal pipe() pattern). Measured ≈27% extra wall-clock per chunk on a sync objectMode Writable; fixing it eliminates ~N redundant microtask schedulings for an N-chunk objectMode pipeline.

findings.title: 64 KB stack buffer zeroed on every POSIX Bun.file() read
findings.problem: `do_read_loop` declares `let mut stack_buffer = [0u8; 64 * 1024];`, which memsets 64 KB on every file read regardless of file size. The original Zig used `var stack_buffer: [64 * 1024]u8 = undefined;` (no memset). The in-code comment claims the memset is "negligible next to the per-iteration syscall", but for a small file the total syscall cost (open+fstat+read+read+close) is ~5-10 µs while a 64 KB memset is ~0.5-1 µs — a fixed 5-15% tax on every `Bun.file(path).text()/.bytes()/.arrayBuffer()`.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:809 `let mut stack_buffer = [0u8; 64 * 1024];` vs. the pre-port Zig at `git show d4514457e8^:src/runtime/webcore/blob/read_file.zig` line 430: `var stack_buffer: [64 * 1024]u8 = undefined;`. `do_read` (line 510) already takes a raw `(*mut u8, usize)` pair, so the buffer could be `[MaybeUninit<u8>; 64*1024]` and passed as `as_mut_ptr().cast()` without ever materialising a `&[u8]` over uninit bytes (the stated UB concern); `bun_sys::read` would then need a `read_uninit` variant, which `node_fs.rs` already side-steps via `expand_to_capacity` on a fresh Vec.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `let mut stack_buffer = [MaybeUninit::<u8>::uninit(); 64 * 1024];` and pass `stack_buffer.as_mut_ptr().cast::<u8>()` to `remaining_buffer`/`do_read` (both already operate on raw `(*mut u8, usize)`). After `do_read` returns `read_amount`, only `stack_buffer[..read_amount]` is observed, which is initialized. Alternatively, skip the stack buffer entirely for `!self.could_block` (regular files) — see the companion finding on `remaining_buffer`.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `await Bun.file(path).text()` / `.bytes()` / `.arrayBuffer()` on Linux/macOS pays a fixed ~0.5-1 µs 64 KB-zeroing cost. For small-file-heavy workloads (reading 10k config/source files), this is ~5-10 ms of pure memset that Node.js and the pre-Rust Bun did not pay.

findings.title: Files smaller than ~64 KB are read into the stack buffer then memcpy'd to the already-allocated heap buffer
findings.problem: `remaining_buffer` chooses the 64 KB stack buffer whenever `spare.len() < stack_buffer.len()`, i.e. whenever the heap buffer's spare capacity is < 64 KB. But for a regular file of size N, `run_async_with_fd` has just called `try_reserve_exact(N+16)` so spare is exactly N+16 — for any N < 65520, `spare < 64 KB` and the read goes into the stack buffer. `do_read_loop` then `extend_from_slice`'s the bytes into the heap buffer that was sitting ready. The pre-allocation at line 749-763 is defeated for the common case.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:495 `if spare.len() < stack_buffer.len() { (stack_buffer.as_mut_ptr(), stack_buffer.len()) } else { (spare...) }` combined with read_file.rs:749-752 `try_reserve_exact(self.size + 16)` and read_file.rs:825-838 `if buf_ptr == stack_ptr { ... self.buffer.extend_from_slice(read); }`. For a 50 KB file: spare=51216 < 65536 → read 50 KB into stack → memcpy 50 KB into the 51 216-byte heap buffer that was allocated specifically to receive it.
findings.proposed_fix: Change the selection to prefer spare capacity whenever it is non-empty (or whenever `!self.could_block`): `if spare.is_empty() { stack } else { spare }`. For regular files, fstat gave us the size and we allocated exactly for it; reading directly into spare is correct and yields the same 2-syscall pattern (read N, read 0/EOF) without the N-byte copy. The stack buffer remains useful only for pipes/sockets (`could_block`) and the rare grew-after-fstat case.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.file(path).bytes()` / `.arrayBuffer()` / `.text()` on any file under ~64 KB does one redundant memcpy of the entire file contents on Linux/macOS. For a 50 KB file that's ~50 KB of extra memory traffic per read; reading many source/config files is measurably slower than it needs to be (and slower than `fs.readFile`, which reads directly into its target buffer).

findings.title: Large ArrayBuffer input is fully memcpy'd into a new allocation before being written
findings.problem: When Bun.write(dest, data) receives an ArrayBuffer/TypedArray ≥ 256 KiB, the sync fast path is skipped and `Blob::get::<false,false>` (Blob.rs:5331) is invoked to turn `data` into a source Blob. For TypedArrays this hits `Blob::try_create(top_value.as_array_buffer(..).byte_slice(), ..)` (Blob.rs:3351) which lands on `bytes_.to_vec()` at Blob.rs:2544 — a full heap allocation + memcpy of the entire payload (or a memfd-backed copy above 8 MiB on Linux). The copied buffer is then passed to the WriteFile threadpool task, written to disk, and freed. The original JS-owned buffer is never written from directly.
findings.evidence: Call chain: write_file_internal (Blob.rs:5331 `break 'brk Blob::get::<false,false>(global_this, data)?`) → from_js_without_defer_gc (Blob.rs:3351 `Blob::try_create(top_value.as_array_buffer(global).unwrap().byte_slice(), global, false)`) → try_create (Blob.rs:2544 `Self::create_with_bytes_and_allocator(bytes_.to_vec(), global_this, was_string)`). Node's fs.promises.writeFile keeps a reference to the user's Buffer and writes it in place without copying.
findings.proposed_fix: For the Bun.write code path, construct a source blob that borrows the ArrayBuffer backing store instead of cloning it: hold a `jsc::Strong` on the ArrayBuffer value inside the WriteFile/WriteFileWindows task and point the bytes slice at `buffer_view.byte_slice()` (store::Bytes can already wrap an external allocator/owner). This removes one O(n) allocation + memcpy per large write.
findings.user_visible_impact: `await Bun.write(path, uint8array)` on a 1 GB Uint8Array spends ~1 GB extra RSS and a full 1 GB memcpy before the first write() syscall; throughput for large-buffer writes is roughly halved versus writing the buffer directly (Node does not copy).

findings.title: Inherited envp rebuilt from scratch on every Bun.spawn()
