findings.title: fs.copyFile read/write fallback zeroes 64KB stack buffer per call
findings.problem: `copy_file_using_read_write_loop()` begins with `let mut stack_buf = [0u8; 64 * 1024];` and then hands it straight to `Syscall::read()` which overwrites it from the kernel. The 64KB memset is pure waste and is #[inline(never)] so it always runs. The heap path right below it (lines 4936-4946) was already fixed with a comment explaining exactly this ("The slab must stay uninitialised: Vec::resize here was a debug-build hot path… the slab is write-only, Syscall::read fills it from the kernel") — but the stack buffer at line 4927 was not given the same treatment. This function is the fallback for `fs.copyFile`/`fs.cp`/`Bun.write(file, file)` when reflink/`copy_file_range`/`sendfile` are unavailable (cross-device, procfs, macOS non-APFS, etc.), called from 8 sites across node_fs.rs and blob/copy_file.rs.
findings.evidence: Line 4927: `let mut stack_buf = [0u8; 64 * 1024];`. Lines 4936-4946 show the heap path already uses `try_reserve_exact` + `expand_to_capacity()` to avoid zero-fill, with an explicit comment about why. Callers: blob/copy_file.rs:346,421,478,560,902 and node_fs.rs:5055,5158,5285,8746,9078.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `let mut stack_buf = MaybeUninit::<[u8; 64 * 1024]>::uninit();` and pass `unsafe { &mut *stack_buf.as_mut_ptr() }` (or the existing `bun_core::ffi::zeroed_unchecked`-style helper) to `Syscall::read`, which only writes to it. Only the first `read_len` bytes (returned by the kernel) are ever read back.

findings.title: Postgres bytea/typed-array column decode zero-fills output then overwrites every byte
findings.problem: `parse_bytea()` (text-format `bytea` columns) does `let mut buf = vec![0u8; len].into_boxed_slice(); decode_hex_to_bytes(&mut buf, hex)?;` — zero-fills then hex-decodes into every byte. `bytea` columns are frequently large (binary blobs, images, file contents), so this doubles the memory-bandwidth cost per bytea cell per row. The same pattern appears at line 678 in `parse_binary_array<Elem>()` for int4[]/float4[] columns: `let mut out = vec![0u8; out_bytes].into_boxed_slice();` followed by a loop that writes every element. Both run once per matching column per row in a Postgres result set — a tight per-item loop for any query returning these types.
findings.evidence: Line 22-29: `fn parse_bytea(hex:&[u8]) { let len = hex.len()/2; let mut buf = vec![0u8; len].into_boxed_slice(); let written = bun_core::decode_hex_to_bytes(&mut buf, hex)?; ... }`. Called from line 892 (per-bytea-column in the main `to_data_cell` switch) and line 199 (per-element in bytea arrays). Line 678: `let mut out: Box<[u8]> = vec![0u8; out_bytes].into_boxed_slice(); for i in 0..array_len { ... val.write_unaligned_ne_bytes(&mut out[i*elem_size..]) }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `bun_core::vec::reserve_spare_bytes(&mut v, len)` + decode into the spare + `commit_spare(written)` + `into_boxed_slice()`. For line 678, build the Box via `Box::new_uninit_slice(out_bytes)` (or the existing `bun_core::heap` helpers) and write each element into `MaybeUninit`, then `assume_init()`.

findings.title: Full PackageManifest deep-clone (7× Box<[T]>) on every manifest cache save
findings.problem: save_async() builds a SaveTask with `manifest: this.clone(), scope: scope.clone()`. PackageManifest is `#[derive(Clone)]` over 7 Box<[T]> fields (string_buf, versions, external_strings, external_strings_for_versions, package_versions, extern_strings_bin_entries, bundled_deps_buf) — for large packages this is hundreds of KB to several MB memcpy'd on the main install thread before the threadpool write even starts. Scope.clone() adds 4 more Box<[u8]> + OwnedURL allocations. This fires once per unique package on cold install (npm.rs:576) and once per 304-Not-Modified on warm install (runTasks.rs:568).
findings.evidence: struct PackageManifest { pkg: NpmPackage, string_buf: Box<[u8]>, versions: Box<[Semver::Version]>, external_strings: Box<[ExternalString]>, external_strings_for_versions: Box<[ExternalString]>, package_versions: Box<[PackageVersion]>, extern_strings_bin_entries: Box<[ExternalString]>, bundled_deps_buf: Box<[PackageNameHash]> } with #[derive(Default, Clone)] at npm.rs:892. The clone exists only so the threadpool task owns its input; Serializer::save() reads it once to write bytes to disk.
findings.proposed_fix: Store PackageManifest behind Arc<PackageManifest> in manager.manifests (entries are inserted once, never mutated in place afterward — the 304 path mutates *before* insert), and have SaveTask hold an Arc clone (pointer bump) instead of a deep copy. Alternatively, serialize into a Vec<u8> on the caller's thread and hand THAT Vec to the threadpool — still one allocation, but sized exactly to the output instead of duplicating every parsed buffer.

findings.title: Per-iteration Dependency.clone() in peer-dedup inner loop when only Copy fields are read
findings.problem: Inside the nested O(peers × non-peers) loop, `let mut dep = lockfile.buffers.dependencies[dep_i].clone()` (and again at line 125) deep-clones the whole Dependency — for npm-tagged deps that walks and Box-allocates the entire semver::query::Group/List/Query linked list — yet the loop body only reads `dep.behavior` (a bitflags u8) and `dep.name_hash` (u64), both Copy. The clone is purely a borrowck workaround around the indexed write at line 118-119.
findings.evidence: Dependency::clone() → DependencyVersion::clone() → for Tag::Npm, NpmInfo::clone() → semver::query::Group::clone() which recursively clones a Box<List>/Box<Query> chain (SemverQuery.rs:381-398, 195-209). Loop body reads only `dep.behavior.is_peer()`, `dep.behavior.is_dev()`, `dep.name_hash`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the clone with direct field reads: `let (behavior, name_hash) = { let d = &lockfile.buffers.dependencies[dep_i]; (d.behavior, d.name_hash) };` and re-read on each dep_i decrement. No allocation; identical semantics.

findings.title: Per-row, per-column WTF::String + AtomString lookup for wide SQL result sets
findings.problem: In the Objects result path, when `names` is provided (column count > JSFinalObject::maxInlineCapacity so no pre-built Structure exists), every row re-runs `Identifier::fromString(vm, name.name.toWTFString())` for every column. The `name` array is the same cached ExternColumnIdentifier[] for the whole statement, so for a 100-column × 1M-row result this performs 100M toWTFString() + 100M AtomStringTable lookups on identical inputs. Same pattern repeats at line 374.
findings.evidence: Hot path: `toJS(...)` is invoked per row from `JSC__constructObjectFromDataCell`, which /workspace/bun/src/sql_jsc/shared/SQLDataCell.rs documents as "used by the per-row `to_js` paths (postgres `Putter`, mysql `Row`)". `names` is populated (and the `putDirectOffset` fast path bypassed) exactly when CachedStructure::build_from_columns stores `self.fields = Some(...)` — i.e. `non_duplicated_count > JSObject::max_inline_capacity()` (see /workspace/bun/src/sql_jsc/shared/CachedStructure.rs:106-112). Inside the per-row loop at SQLClient.cpp:314-328 and 346-382, `Identifier::fromString(vm, name.name.toWTFString())` is re-evaluated each iteration with no hoisting.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the identifier creation out of the per-row loop: before the row loop, build a `Vector<Identifier>` (or reuse a cache hung off the same ExternColumnIdentifier[] lifetime) once from `namesPtr`, then index into it inside the loop. Alternatively, have CachedStructure store `Identifier`s (or a JSC::Structure for the >maxInlineCapacity case as well) instead of BunStrings so per-row conversion is a direct `putDirect(vm, cachedIdent[i], value)`.

findings.title: Fresh WTF::String allocated per unknown HTTP/2 header name, then immediately re-atomized
findings.problem: For header names that miss `findHTTPHeaderName` (any custom `x-*` header), `nameStr = jsString(vm, nameView.toString())` allocates a brand-new StringImpl copying the name bytes. A few lines later (129-131) the same name is pulled back out with `nameStr->getString()` and fed to `Identifier::fromString(vm, nameString)`, which looks up/inserts an AtomString — so if the atom already exists (same header seen on a prior request), the freshly allocated StringImpl from `toString()` is pure waste. This runs inside the `for (i < fieldCount)` loop, once per header, once per HTTP/2 HEADERS frame.
findings.evidence: Bun__h2__materializeHeaders is the per-request header materializer (file header comment: "Replaces per-field JSArray::push round trips ... with one call per block"). Loop at lines 86-185 processes every header field. Known names take the interned zero-alloc branch (102-104); unknown names hit line 106 `nameView.toString()`. Line 131 `Identifier::fromString(vm, nameString)` then does an AtomStringTable lookup on the same bytes. `Identifier::fromString(VM&, std::span<const Latin1Character>)` exists (/workspace/bun/vendor/WebKit/Source/JavaScriptCore/runtime/Identifier.h:116) and would atomize directly from the wire bytes with no intermediate StringImpl.
findings.proposed_fix: For the miss branch, build the Identifier first from the raw span (`auto ident = Identifier::fromString(vm, std::span<const Latin1Character>{nameBytes, nameLen})`), then derive the JSString from that atom (`nameStr = jsString(vm, ident.string())`). This allocates at most once (only when the atom is new) and lets repeated custom header names across requests share the same StringImpl, matching what the known-header branch already does via `httpHeaderNameStringImpl`.

findings.title: Triple hash lookup in find_hoisted_context_declarations::visit (per-operand, per-instruction)
findings.problem: The inner visit() closure is called once per instruction operand and once per terminal operand across every block of every React function (lines 1204-1211). It does hoisted.contains_key(&decl_id) && hoisted.get(&decl_id).unwrap().is_none() and then hoisted.insert(decl_id, Some(...)) — three hash/probe operations on the same DeclarationId key.
findings.evidence: Line 1183-1185: `if hoisted.contains_key(&decl_id) && hoisted.get(&decl_id).unwrap().is_none() { hoisted.insert(decl_id, Some(place.clone())); }`. Driven by the `for (_block_id, block) in &func.body.blocks { for instr_id in &block.instructions { ... each_instruction_value_operand } }` loop at 1188-1212.
findings.proposed_fix: Collapse to one lookup via get_mut: `if let Some(slot @ None) = hoisted.get_mut(&decl_id) { *slot = Some(place.clone()); }` — one probe instead of three, and avoids the clone when the slot is already filled.

findings.title: Triple lookup on function_signature_cache in per-effect apply loop
findings.problem: Inside apply_effect (reached from the per-instruction × fixed-point-iteration infer_block loop), a call to a known local function does !context.function_signature_cache.contains_key(&func_id), then .insert(func_id, sig), then .get(&func_id).unwrap().clone() — three hash probes on the same FunctionId key. build_signature_from_function_expression only borrows env (not context), so there is no borrowck obstacle to using entry().
findings.evidence: Lines 2059-2067: `if !context.function_signature_cache.contains_key(&func_id) { let sig = build_signature_from_function_expression(env, func_id); context.function_signature_cache.insert(func_id, sig); } let sig = context.function_signature_cache.get(&func_id).unwrap().clone();`
findings.proposed_fix: `let sig = context.function_signature_cache.entry(func_id).or_insert_with(|| build_signature_from_function_expression(env, func_id)).clone();` — one probe.

findings.title: contains_key + insert (plus unconditional clone) in DependencyCollectionContext::declare, called per-instruction
findings.problem: declare() runs at least once per HIR instruction via handle_instruction (line 2049) and again for each lvalue in StoreLocal/Destructure/etc. It does `if !self.declarations.contains_key(decl_id) { self.declarations.insert(decl_id, decl.clone()); }` — two probes on the same IdMap key, and decl.clone() allocates a Vec<ScopeId> even when the key is already present (the clone result is discarded).
findings.evidence: Lines 1808-1811: `if !self.declarations.contains_key(decl_id) { self.declarations.insert(decl_id, decl.clone()); } self.reassignments.insert(identifier_id, decl);`. IdMap exposes entry() (collections.rs:470). Call sites at propagate_scope_dependencies_hir.rs:2049/2079/2092/2112/2129/2164/2174/2229 are all inside the per-block/per-instruction walk.
findings.proposed_fix: `self.declarations.entry(decl_id).or_insert_with(|| decl.clone());` — single probe, and the Decl (with its Vec<ScopeId>) is only cloned on first sight of a declaration id.

findings.title: HTMLRewriter setter/attribute ops re-validate UTF-8 from JSValue::to_slice()
findings.problem: `setter_utf8_arg` (used for TextChunk.text setter, Comment.text setter, Element.tagName setter, lines 1706/1803/2216) and `get_attribute_`/`has_attribute_`/`set_attribute_`/`remove_attribute_` (lines 2045/2057/2079-2080/2104) all run `core::str::from_utf8` on the output of `JSValue::to_slice()` or `ZigString::to_slice()`. Both of those functions produce valid UTF-8 by construction (they transcode WTF Latin-1/UTF-16 to UTF-8 — see JSValue.rs:889-896 and mod.rs:1892-1908). These are per-attribute/per-setter calls inside element callbacks, i.e. inner-loop of HTML rewriting.
findings.evidence: Line 1466-1469: `let slice = value.to_slice(global)?; Ok(utf8_or_throw(global, slice.slice())?.to_owned())`. `JSValue::to_slice` → `to_bun_string()?.to_utf8()` which routes WTFStringImpl through `to_utf8()` (Latin-1/UTF-16 → UTF-8 transcode). Line 2045: `let slice = name.to_slice(); let Ok(name) = core::str::from_utf8(slice.slice()) else { return Ok(JSValue::NULL); };` — same redundant scan on attribute names per getAttribute call.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `from_utf8_unchecked` (guarded by `debug_assert!`) on the `ZigStringSlice::slice()` result, or introduce a single `ZigStringSlice::as_str()` that encapsulates the unchecked cast for the whole file. The `Err` branches at 1460/2045 are unreachable for JSC-sourced strings.

findings.title: Inline regex literal in per-property loop of getOwnNonIndexProperties (duplicate of existing module-level numberRegExp)
findings.problem: getOwnNonIndexProperties() iterates every own property descriptor of an Array/TypedArray being inspected and, for each key, evaluates the inline literal /^(0|[1-9][0-9]*)$/ through the uncurried RegExpPrototypeTest. Because the regex is passed through a generic Function.prototype.call.bind thunk rather than invoked as str.test()/str.match(), JSC cannot fold it and allocates a fresh RegExp wrapper per iteration. For util.inspect/util.format on an N-element array this is N+1 RegExp allocations per call (e.g. 1001 for a 1000-element array). An identical, already-hoisted module-level constant `numberRegExp` exists at line 465 and is used at line 2080 for the same purpose — line 2689 just forgot to use it.
findings.evidence: Line 2685-2693: `for (const [k, v] of ObjectEntries(desc)) { if (!RegExpPrototypeTest(/^(0|[1-9][0-9]*)$/, k) || ...) { ... } }`. Line 465 already defines `const numberRegExp = /^(0|[1-9][0-9]*)$/;`. Callers at lines 1284 and 1310 (formatRaw) invoke this for every $isJSArray and isTypedArray value. Micro-benchmark reproducing the exact uncurried-call + for..of shape shows the hoisted form is ~12-30% faster across repeated runs (120-131 ms hoisted vs 135-198 ms inline for 2M iterations). End-to-end, util.inspect on a 100-element array (364 ms / 5000 calls) is 2.5x slower than on a 100-key object (144 ms) and this loop is the main array-only code path.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the inline literal with the existing module-level constant: `if (!RegExpPrototypeTest(numberRegExp, k) || NumberParseInt(k, 10) >= 2 ** 32 - 1) {`. No behavior change; pure reuse of an already-defined constant.

findings.title: H2FrameParser::request() re-creates JSPropertyIterator each pass of the 2-pass header loop
findings.problem: Every outbound `node:http2` request (object-form headers path) runs `for ignore_pseudo_headers in 0..2 { let mut iter = JSPropertyIterator::init(...) ... }`. Each `init` is an FFI crossing to `Bun__JSPropertyIterator__create`, which heap-allocates a `JSPropertyIterator`, builds a `PropertyNameArray` (walks every own property), and may reify static props — then `Drop` makes another FFI crossing to `Bun__JSPropertyIterator__deinit`. The code comment at 8872 says `JSPropertyIterator lacks .reset()`, but `reset()` exists (JSPropertyIterator.rs:171). Same pattern at line 8320 (`push_promise`).
findings.evidence: h2_frame_parser.rs:8867-8882: `for ignore_pseudo_headers in 0..2usize { // Note: lacks .reset(); re-initialize per pass …  let mut iter = bun_jsc::JSPropertyIterator::init(...) }`. JSPropertyIterator.rs:171 `pub fn reset(&mut self) { self.iter_i = 0; self.i = 0; }`. C++ create (JSPropertyIterator.cpp:39) heap-allocs + walks property names.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist `JSPropertyIterator::init` above the `for 0..2` loop and call `iter.reset()` between passes (semantics preserved: `include_value:true` still re-observes getters via `getNameAndValue`). Removes 1 heap alloc + 2 FFI crossings + a full own-property-name walk per request. Apply the same fix at line 8320.

findings.title: create_array_from_iter/from_slice use per-element put_index FFI instead of the existing batched JSArray__constructArray
findings.problem: `JSValue::create_array_from_iter` (and `create_array_from_slice`, which delegates to it) builds a JS array by calling `put_index` once per element. Each `put_index` is two FFI crossings (`JSC__JSValue__putIndex` + `Bun__RETURN_IF_EXCEPTION` via `call_check_slow`). For N elements that is 2N+2 crossings. A batched one-crossing path already exists: `JSArray::create` → `JSArray__constructArray(global, *const JSValue, len)` (JSArray.rs:20). Hot callers include Valkey RESP array/set/push decoding (`protocol_jsc.rs:112/141/158` — every multi-bulk Redis reply such as MGET/LRANGE/KEYS), glob scan results (`glob.rs:288`), and the many DNS `array.put_index(...)` loops in `dns_jsc/cares_jsc.rs`.
findings.evidence: JSValue.rs:572-576: `let array = create_empty_array(len)?; for (i,item) in iter.enumerate() { array.put_index(global, i as u32, f(item)?)?; }`. JSValue.rs:1523 `put_index → call_check_slow(|| JSC__JSValue__putIndex(...))`. JSArray.rs:20-26 `JSArray::create → JSArray__constructArray(global, items.as_ptr(), items.len())` — single crossing.
findings.proposed_fix: Have `create_array_from_slice` call `JSArray::create(global, items)` directly. For `create_array_from_iter`, collect the mapped `JSValue`s into a small stack-backed buffer (`SmallVec<[JSValue; 32]>`) and then call `JSArray__constructArray` once. This turns 2N+2 crossings into ~2 (construct + exception check) for the common case.

findings.title: Recursive tree walk clones entire path Vec at every node, uses it only as &[_]
findings.problem: `collect_minimal_deps_in_subtree` does `let mut new_path = path.to_vec(); new_path.push(..);` for every child and passes `&new_path` to the recursive call. The owned Vec is never used for ownership — only borrowed (`&new_path`) and, at leaves, re-copied into the arena via `AstAlloc::vec_from_slice(path)`. This is O(nodes × depth) heap allocations of `DependencyPathEntry` (a non-trivial struct with `PropertyLiteral` + `Option<SourceLocation>`).
findings.evidence: Lines 1714-1728: `for (child_name, child_entry) in &node.properties { let mut new_path = path.to_vec(); new_path.push(DependencyPathEntry{..}); collect_minimal_deps_in_subtree(&child_entry.node, reactive, root_id, &new_path, results); }`. Called from `derive_minimal_dependencies` (line 1694) once per dependency-root per reactive scope, and recursively for every property node — i.e. per-property-access in every compiled React component.
findings.proposed_fix: Change the signature to take `path: &mut Vec<DependencyPathEntry>` and push/pop around the recursive call (the standard DFS path-stack pattern). One Vec reused for the whole tree; zero per-node allocs. The arena copy at line 1711 is then the only copy and is required.

findings.title: InferenceState::values_for() allocates a Vec per lookup; all callers only iterate it
findings.problem: `values_for` does `match self.variables.get(place_id) { Some(values) => values.as_slice().to_vec(), None => Vec::new() }`. Every caller (`freeze` line 761, `freeze_function_captures_transitive` line 1670, `apply_effect` lines 1713/2052/3239) immediately does `for vid in &value_ids { .. }` or `value_ids[0]` and drops the Vec. The mutated field inside those loops is `self.values` (via `freeze_value` → `value_slot`), which is disjoint from `self.variables`, so the copy exists purely to defeat borrowck.
findings.evidence: `fn values_for(&self, place_id) -> Vec<ValueId> { match self.variables.get(place_id) { Some(v) => v.as_slice().to_vec(), None => Vec::new() } }` and `fn freeze_value(&mut self, ..) { *self.value_slot(..) = .. }` with `fn value_slot(..) -> &mut Option<..> { &mut self.values[i] }`. `self.variables` and `self.values` are separate fields (struct at line 632). `apply_effect` is invoked per-effect per-instruction inside the fixed-point inference loop — the hottest part of the React Compiler backend.
findings.proposed_fix: Add an inherent helper that split-borrows `(&self.variables, &mut self.values)` so callers can iterate `variables.get(id).as_slice()` while mutating `values`. Alternatively return a `SmallVec<[ValueId; 4]>` since `ValueIdSet` already has a 3-word inline representation — eliminates the heap alloc for the common small case without API churn.

findings.title: `size_of_url()` does FFI + linear header scan just for GC size hint on every Request
findings.problem: `Request::to_js` (called once per request at server_body.rs:3167) invokes `calculate_estimated_byte_size()` → `size_of_url()`. For server-created Requests `url` is empty (lazy), so `size_of_url()` falls through to `req.url()` (FFI) and `req.header(b"host")` (FFI + linear `strncasecmp` walk over all request headers in uWS `getHeader`). The Host header is present on essentially every HTTP/1.1 request so the bloom filter does not save it. This work produces only a heuristic byte count fed into `reported_estimated_size`, used solely for GC accounting.
findings.evidence: Request.rs:535-536 `pub fn to_js(...) { self.calculate_estimated_byte_size(); ... }`. Request.rs:512-518 → `self.size_of_url()`. Request.rs:874-888 `req.url()` + `req.header(b"host").filter(|h| Self::is_valid_host_header(h))`. packages/bun-uws/src/HttpParser.h:262-276 `for (Header *h = headers; (++h)->key.length();) if (... strncasecmp ...)`.
findings.proposed_fix: In `calculate_estimated_byte_size`, when `self.url` is empty and `request_context` is live, use a cheap constant estimate (e.g., `size_of::<Request>()` + typical URL length) or just `req.url().len()` without the Host-header lookup. Alternatively drop `size_of_url()` from the estimate entirely — it is an approximation anyway and the URL bytes are owned by uWS, not the Request.

findings.title: Second redundant `drain_microtasks()` in `on_response` before render
findings.problem: For a synchronous `fetch` handler, `on_response()` calls `ctx.drain_microtasks()` at line 2690, then immediately routes to `render()` → `do_render()` → `do_render_with_body()`, which calls `this.drain_microtasks()` again at line 3124 (already reported). Both go through `is_async()` (false, since `defer_deinit_until_callback_completes` is Some) and reach `VirtualMachine::drain_microtasks()` — an FFI hop into JSC. The sync handler just returned from `JSValue::call`; no microtasks were enqueued between 2690 and 3124, making one of the two calls pure overhead on every request.
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:2687-2690 `let ctx = self; ...; ctx.drain_microtasks();` followed at 2752 by `unsafe { ctx.render(response) };` → do_render() (3376-3397) → do_render_with_body() line 3124 `this.drain_microtasks();`. drain_microtasks() at 627-640: `if self.is_async() { return; } ... (*vm).drain_microtasks();` — is_async()==false in sync path because defer_deinit flag is Some (set at server_body.rs:2904).
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the `ctx.drain_microtasks()` at line 2690; the call at 3124 (or its eventual fix) covers the same ground. Alternatively, hoist a single drain to `handle_request_for` after `on_response` returns and delete both inner calls.

findings.title: `to_blob_if_possible()` runs twice per response body (double ASCII scan)
findings.problem: `on_response()` calls `body_value.to_blob_if_possible()` at line 2736 (and 2814 on the fulfilled-promise arm), then `render()` → `do_render()` → `do_render_with_body()` calls `value.to_blob_if_possible()` again on the same `&mut Body::Value` at line 3128. For the plaintext/JSON benchmark the body is `WTFStringImpl`, so each call executes `strings::is_all_ascii(latin1_slice)` over the entire response payload. The first call leaves the value as `WTFStringImpl` (ASCII → no conversion needed), so the second scan re-walks the same bytes for nothing. Cost scales linearly with response body size on every request.
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:2735-2736 `let body_value = unsafe { (*response).get_body_value() }; body_value.to_blob_if_possible();` then 2752 `ctx.render(response)` → 3906 `self.do_render()` → 3392-3396 `do_render_with_body(self, (*response).get_body_value(), ...)` → 3128 `value.to_blob_if_possible();`. Body.rs:723-734 + wtf.rs:126-133 → `strings::to_utf8_from_latin1` → `is_all_ascii(latin1)` full-length scan; returns None so variant stays `WTFStringImpl` and the second call repeats the scan.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the `to_blob_if_possible()` calls at 2736/2814 — the only consumer of the post-conversion tag in that block is the `match body_value { Blob/Locked }` for the `protect()` decision, which only cares about `Blob` (needs_to_read_file) and `Locked`; a `WTFStringImpl` → `InternalBlob` conversion does not change that decision. `do_render_with_body()` already normalizes at 3128 before the real dispatch.

findings.title: `echo` builtin clones output buffer twice after just storing it
findings.problem: Same store-then-clone antipattern as seq: line 90 moves `output` into `state.output`, then line 94 (fd path) and line 100 (no-io path) both `.clone()` it back out to obtain a `&[u8]`. IOWriter::enqueue copies the slice internally and on_io_writer_chunk (line 106-118) never reads state.output, so both the store and the clone are wasted allocations per `echo` invocation. echo is the single most-invoked shell builtin.
findings.evidence: Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).output = output; … let buf = Self::state_mut(interp, cmd).output.clone(); … .stdout.enqueue(child, &buf, safeguard) — and at line 100 a second .clone() for the write_no_io path.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the assignment at line 90; keep `output` as a local and pass `&output` to both `enqueue()` and `write_no_io()`. The `output` field of EchoState appears unused after this function and can likely be removed entirely.

findings.title: Triple Box<[u8]> hostname clone per dns.lookup() in libc backend path
findings.problem: Resolver::lookup() allocates the hostname three times: (1) `PendingCacheKey::init(query_init)` at line 325 clones `query.name` (dns.rs:1165); (2) `let query = query_init.clone()` at line 337 clones `name: Box<[u8]>` (dns/lib.rs:70); (3) `query.clone()` at line 342 clones it again into the Backend::Libc enum. `query` itself is only used by-ref afterward (line 345) and then dropped. dns.lookup is per-connection in proxy/gateway workloads.
findings.evidence: GetAddrInfo { name: Box<[u8]>, port: u16, options } with `fn clone(&self) -> GetAddrInfo { name: Box::<[u8]>::from(&*self.name), … }`. At dns.rs:337-346 the second clone exists only because `GetAddrInfoRequest::init` takes both an owned Backend (consuming one copy) and `&query` in the same call.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the intermediate `let query = query_init.clone()` at line 337 and pass `query_init` by reference everywhere; construct the single owned copy for `Backend::Libc(LibcBackend::Query(query_init.clone()))` inline. Reduces 3 hostname allocations to 2 (cache key + backend payload). Further: have PendingCacheKey borrow the name or share it via Arc<[u8]> if the request/cache-entry lifetimes permit.

findings.title: url.protocol getter allocates via makeString() on every access
findings.problem: `URLDecomposition::protocol()` returns `makeString(fullURL.protocol(), ':')`, heap-allocating a fresh StringImpl on every `new URL(...).protocol` read. The function already special-cases `javascript:` to return a static literal; the overwhelmingly common protocols (`http:`, `https:`, `ws:`, `wss:`, `file:`, `blob:`, `data:`) take the allocating path. In request-routing / URL-inspection code this getter is called per-request and the result is typically compared to a literal and discarded.
findings.evidence: `jsDOMURL_protocolGetter` at /workspace/bun/src/jsc/bindings/webcore/JSDOMURL.cpp:326-332 forwards directly to `impl.protocol()` with no caching; `toJS<IDLUSVString>` then wraps the freshly-allocated String in a new JSString. `WTF::URL::protocol()` returns a `StringView` into the existing href, so the only allocation is the `makeString(...)` here.
findings.proposed_fix: Extend the existing `protocolIsJavaScript()` special-case to the handful of ubiquitous schemes: `if (fullURL.protocolIs("http"_s)) return "http:"_s;` etc. (or a small switch over `protocol().length()`). Falls back to `makeString` only for exotic schemes. This turns a per-access allocation into a static-string return for ~100% of web/server workloads.

findings.title: Per-response-header toWTFString() in node:http writeHead fast path when only a StringView is consumed
findings.problem: In `NodeHTTPServer__writeHead`'s flat-array branch (the primary node:http ServerResponse path), the per-header loop calls `nameValue.toWTFString(globalObject)` and `headerValue.toWTFString(globalObject)` (lines 976/978, and 1031/1051 in the object branches). The only consumers are `findHTTPHeaderName(StringView(name))` and `writeResponseHeader<isSSL>(response, name, value)`, whose signature (line 730) is `(..., const WTF::StringView& name, const WTF::StringView& value)` — it reads raw bytes and never retains the String. `toWTFString()` on a rope JSString allocates the resolved buffer; on a flat JSString it still incurs an atomic ref/deref per header per response.
findings.evidence: Loop at NodeHTTP.cpp:970-1009 iterates every outgoing header of every node:http response (this is `NodeHTTPServer__writeHead`, called from `writeHead_http`/`writeHead_https` at 1065-1083). `writeResponseHeader` at 730-756 only inspects `.is8Bit()`/`.span8()`/`.utf8()` on the StringView — no ownership taken. `jsHTTPGetHeader` at line 1326 already uses the `nameString->view(globalObject)` pattern to avoid this for the getter.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `JSValue::toWTFString(globalObject)` with `JSString* s = val.toString(globalObject); auto view = s->view(globalObject);` (a `GCOwnedDataScope<StringView>`), and pass the StringView directly to `findHTTPHeaderName` / `writeResponseHeader`. The sentinel check at 985 (`name.length() == 1 && name[0] == 0`) works unchanged on a StringView. This removes the per-header WTF::String ref/deref and avoids materializing a standalone String when the JS side built the header value as a rope (e.g. template-literal `${k}: ${v}`).

findings.title: Per-DATA-frame triple lookup on streams map in HTTP/2 connection
findings.problem: begin_streamed_data (line 1287) and handle_data (line 1423) run once per inbound HTTP/2 DATA frame — the hottest per-chunk path for h2 body streaming. Each does self.streams.contains_key(&hdr.stream_id), then self.streams.insert(hdr.stream_id, s) on miss, then self.streams.get_mut(&hdr.stream_id) a few lines later (1298 / 1433) — up to three hash probes on the same u32 stream id per frame. handle_headers (line 909) has the same shape: contains_key → entry().or_insert_with → get_mut.
findings.evidence: Lines 1287-1298: `if !self.streams.contains_key(&hdr.stream_id) && sink.is_local_stream(hdr.stream_id) { ... self.streams.insert(hdr.stream_id, s); } ... match self.streams.get_mut(&hdr.stream_id) { ... }`. Identical pattern at 1423-1433. streams is HashMap<u32, Stream> (line 221).
findings.proposed_fix: Route through a single entry/get_mut: `let st = if sink.is_local_stream(hdr.stream_id) { Some(self.streams.entry(hdr.stream_id).or_insert_with(|| { let mut s = Stream::new(send_init, recv_init); s.state = State::Open; s })) } else { self.streams.get_mut(&hdr.stream_id) }; match st { None => ..., Some(st) => ... }`. One probe per DATA frame instead of 2-3.

findings.title: contains_key + insert in RequestedModules::insert_if_absent, called per import record during printing
findings.problem: RequestedModules::insert_if_absent is invoked via request_module()/request_module_with_phase() for every import record emitted by the JS printer (lib.rs:5569/5806/6366) — i.e. once per import/export-from statement per printed module. It does self.index.contains_key(&(key, phase)) then self.index.insert((key, phase), ...), hashing the (StringID, ModulePhase) tuple twice on every miss. The surrounding PERF comment (line 444) already flags this struct as allocation-heavy.
findings.evidence: Lines 469-476: `if self.index.contains_key(&(key, phase)) { return true; } self.index.insert((key, phase), self.keys.len()); self.keys.push(key); ...`
findings.proposed_fix: Use entry: `match self.index.entry((key, phase)) { Entry::Occupied(_) => return true, Entry::Vacant(v) => { v.insert(self.keys.len()); } } self.keys.push(key); ...` — single hash+probe per import record.

findings.title: Uncached getenv("BUN_DISABLE_STORE_AST_HEAP") on every AST side-arena enter(), once per bundler output chunk
findings.problem: store_ast_alloc_heap::enter() begins with an uncached bun_core::getenv_z("BUN_DISABLE_STORE_AST_HEAP") call, placed *before* the `if !STATE_ID.get().is_null() { return }` fast-path. In the bundler's chunk pipeline, post_process_js_chunk() (src/bundler/linker_context/postProcessJSChunk.rs:63) constructs a StoreAstAllocHeap guard whose new() calls enter() and whose Drop calls exit() (which nulls STATE_ID). That guard is created once per JS output chunk on each worker thread, so every chunk pays a fresh libc getenv linear scan for an env var that is immutable for the process lifetime.
findings.proposed_fix: Cache the flag once, e.g. `static DISABLED: OnceLock<bool> = OnceLock::new(); if *DISABLED.get_or_init(|| bun_core::getenv_z(bun_core::zstr!("BUN_DISABLE_STORE_AST_HEAP")).is_some()) { return; }` — or register it in bun_core::env_var and call the cached `::get()`. Same pattern already used nearby (threading/ThreadPool.rs:42, sys/lib.rs:5749 RWF_STATE).

findings.title: React compiler ref_name() validates UTF-8 on every identifier resolution
findings.problem: `HirBuilder::ref_name()` runs `core::str::from_utf8` + `.to_owned()` on every `Ref` it sees; it is called from `resolve_binding_with_loc` (line 938) which executes once per identifier reference while lowering every expression/statement in a React component. The bytes come from `host.ref_name()` → `P::load_name_from_ref()` (src/js_parser/p.rs:877-892), which returns either a `Symbol.original_name` slice, a slice of the source buffer at an identifier span, or an allocated name — all of which are JS identifier names that the JS lexer has already accepted as Unicode ID_Start/ID_Continue sequences (valid UTF-8 by spec). The `from_utf8` scan is redundant and the `cold_todo("non-utf8 identifier")` path is unreachable.
findings.evidence: Line 918-922: `core::str::from_utf8(self.host.ref_name(ref_)).map(str::to_owned).map_err(|_| cold_todo("non-utf8 identifier", None))`. Called at line 938 inside `resolve_binding_with_loc`, which is invoked for every identifier via `resolve_binding` (line 928) and `lower_identifier_reference` (line 1121) during HIR construction — i.e. per-token in the React compile pipeline.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(self.host.ref_name(ref_)) }.to_owned()` with `debug_assert!(str::from_utf8(..).is_ok())`. Better: change downstream consumers to accept `&[u8]` so the `String` allocation also disappears (the only eq check at line 940 is `name == "fbt"` which works on bytes).

findings.title: React compiler get_property_type validates UTF-8 on every property lookup
findings.problem: `Environment::get_property_type` (line 666) and `get_property_type_from_shapes` (line 634) convert the `&[u8]` property name to `&str` via `core::str::from_utf8` solely to key into `shape.properties: HashMap<&'static str, Type>` (object_shape.rs:124). These run per property-access expression during type inference of every compiled component. Property names originate from JS AST `StoreStr` identifier/string slices produced by the JS lexer and are valid UTF-8; moreover every key in the shape map is a `'static` ASCII literal, so a non-UTF-8 input would simply miss regardless.
findings.evidence: Line 666-668: `if let Some(ty) = core::str::from_utf8(property).ok().and_then(|k| shape.properties.get(k))` and the identical pattern at line 634. Registry keys are `&'static str` (object_shape.rs:124). Same pattern in `get_global_declaration` at lines 484/502/551 for `GlobalRegistry::get(&str)` (globals.rs:135), hit once per LoadGlobal during `pre_resolve_globals` (infer_types.rs:384-401).
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) change `ShapeRegistry`/`GlobalRegistry::get` to accept `&[u8]` and hash/compare bytes (the internal `BASE_GLOBAL_INDEX.contains_key` already takes `key.as_bytes()` at globals.rs:151), or (b) use `from_utf8_unchecked` with a `debug_assert!`. Option (a) removes the `&str` requirement entirely and avoids 5+ redundant validations per compiled function.

findings.title: React type inference re-validates property-literal bytes in unifier fixpoint
findings.problem: Inside the type-unifier's property-type resolution (called for every `Type::Property` encountered during the `unify` fixpoint over all instructions), `PropertyLiteral::String(s)` is converted with `core::str::from_utf8(s.slice())` to look up `shape.properties.get(k)`. The unifier runs iteratively until fixpoint over every block's instructions, so each `obj.prop` in the source triggers this validation multiple times. `s` is a `StoreStr` from the JS AST (a parsed identifier or string literal) and is valid UTF-8; the comment on line 230-232 already acknowledges a non-UTF-8 name 'cannot match any registry key' — i.e. the validation exists only to satisfy the `&str` map key type, not for correctness.
findings.evidence: Line 228-246: `PropertyLiteral::String(s) => core::str::from_utf8(s.slice()).ok().and_then(|k| shape.properties.get(k)).or_else(|| shape.properties.get("*"))...`. This helper is reached from the per-instruction `unifier.unify(.., Type::Property { .. }, shapes)` at infer_types.rs:722-732 for every `PropertyLoad` inside the block iteration loop at line 406.
findings.proposed_fix: Same as the environment.rs finding: make the shape-properties map lookups accept `&[u8]`, or use `from_utf8_unchecked` + `debug_assert!`. Since all `HashMap<&'static str, Type>` keys are ASCII literals, a byte-keyed lookup is trivially equivalent and eliminates the O(n) scan from the unifier inner loop.

findings.title: JSArrayIterator::next() makes an FFI revalidation call on every element even on the fast path
findings.problem: The fast path holds a raw butterfly pointer but still calls `Bun__JSArray__contiguousVectorIsStillValid` (extern "C") on every `.next()`. That is one FFI crossing per element for all ~79 call sites, including per-packet UDP `sendMany` (udp_socket.rs:1346), per-header h2 array-form headers (h2_frame_parser.rs:8723/8941), per-arg spawn argv (js_bun_spawn_bindings.rs:285), and per-arg Valkey commands. The C++ body is cheap (pointer + indexingType compare), but the crossing itself dominates for short elements.
findings.evidence: JSArrayIterator.rs:41-51: `if let Some(elements) = self.fast { if Bun__JSArray__contiguousVectorIsStillValid(self.array, elements, self.len) { … } }`. bindings.cpp:6534 shows the C++ impl only reads `indexingType()`, `canDoFastIndexedAccess()`, `publicLength()` and compares the butterfly data pointer — all fields that could be read once and cached Rust-side, or revalidated only after the caller ran side-effecting JS.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) add a `next_assume_stable()` that skips revalidation for callers that do not run user JS between iterations, or (b) change the iterator to cache the `{indexingType, canDoFastIndexedAccess, publicLength, data}` tuple via one FFI struct-return at `init`, then revalidate via a single FFI only when the caller explicitly signals a side-effect (`iter.note_possible_invalidation()`) instead of on every element.

findings.title: Bun.serve do_write_headers issues 2–6 sequential fast_remove FFI calls per response
findings.problem: On every HTTP response that carries a `Headers` object, `do_write_headers` calls `headers.fast_remove(...)` once per header to strip (Content-Length, Transfer-Encoding, and for HTTP/3 also Connection/Keep-Alive/Proxy-Connection/Upgrade). Each is a separate `WebCore__FetchHeaders__fastRemove_` FFI crossing. This is on the per-request hot path of `Bun.serve`, so 2 crossings/resp (6 for H3) multiply across high-RPS workloads.
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:3817-3824: `headers.fast_remove(ContentLength); headers.fast_remove(TransferEncoding); if HTTP3 { fast_remove(Connection); fast_remove(KeepAlive); fast_remove(ProxyConnection); fast_remove(Upgrade); }`. FetchHeaders.rs:295-297: `fast_remove_ → WebCore__FetchHeaders__fastRemove_(self, header)` extern call.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `WebCore__FetchHeaders__fastRemoveMask(&FetchHeaders, u64)` that removes all headers whose `HTTPHeaderName` bit is set, and call it once with the precomputed const mask (2-header or 6-header variant). Collapses 2–6 crossings into 1 per response.

findings.title: ModuleInfo::str interns every new string by to_vec()'ing bytes already stored in strings_buf
findings.problem: `str()` appends `value` to `self.strings_buf` (line 653) and then does `self.strings_map.insert(value.to_vec(), idx)` (line 656). The map key is an owned `Vec<u8>` whose bytes are an exact duplicate of what was just written into `strings_buf`. The Vec is never mutated — it's only ever hashed/compared on subsequent `get(value)` lookups. This is one heap alloc+memcpy per unique identifier/specifier printed.
findings.evidence: Called from ~30 sites in the per-statement printer loop (`mi.str(local_name)`, `mi.str(item.alias.slice())`, `mi.str(irp)` at js_printer/lib.rs:2110/5068/5186/5224/5340/5384/5434/5568/5740/5741/5805/5812/5813/6326 and postProcessJSChunk.rs:239/329/339/360/367/383/412/893). Runs once per top-level decl + import item + export item in every module when ModuleInfo is enabled (bytecode-cache/compile path). The file already carries a `// PERF: owned-key dupe; revisit with a raw-entry API.` note.
findings.proposed_fix: Key `strings_map` by the `(start_offset, len)` range into `strings_buf` with a custom hasher/Borrow that hashes the bytes at that range, or switch to hashbrown's `HashTable<u32>` keyed by `wyhash(value)` with equality against `strings_buf[off..off+len]`. Removes the per-unique-string Vec allocation entirely.

findings.title: raw_template_contents copies template bytes to a throwaway Vec, then copies again into the arena
findings.problem: On the `\r` branch the code does `let mut bytes: Vec<u8> = text.to_vec();` (heap alloc+copy), rewrites CRLF→LF in place, then `self.arena.alloc_slice_copy(&bytes)` (second copy into the arena). The Vec is immediately dropped; it exists only to provide a mutable `&[u8]`. The arena copy alone suffices.
findings.evidence: Lines 3528-3551: `let mut bytes: Vec<u8> = text.to_vec(); … bytes.truncate(end); self.arena.alloc_slice_copy(&bytes)`. Called per tagged-template head/middle/tail via `parse_suffix.rs:281,312` and `parse/mod.rs:303` — i.e. per template-literal part in the lexer hot path whenever the source contains CR (Windows line endings, or minified bundles with embedded \r).
findings.proposed_fix: Allocate once directly in the arena: `let bytes = self.arena.alloc_slice_copy(text);` then run the same in-place CRLF compaction over that `&mut [u8]` and return `&bytes[..end]`. Drops the heap Vec alloc+copy entirely.

findings.title: Legacy transpiler copies the entire printed output via to_vec() even though the writer is about to drop
findings.problem: `output_file.value = Value::Buffer { bytes: writer.ctx.written().to_vec().into_boxed_slice() }`. `writer` (a local `BufferPrinter`) is last used here and drops immediately after the match arm, so its `MutableString` buffer (often hundreds of KB of printed JS) is freed right after being fully duplicated. Ownership could be moved out instead of cloned.
findings.evidence: `BufferWriter::written()` returns `&self.buffer.list[..self.written_len]` (js_printer/lib.rs:7611). `MutableString::to_owned_slice()` / `take_slice()` (bun_core/string/MutableString.rs:362-368) already exist to move the Vec out without copying. `build_with_resolve_result_eager` runs once per file in `process_resolve_queue` (transpiler.rs:2915-2929), the per-entry loop of the non-bundle-v2 `Transpiler::build` path.
findings.proposed_fix: Truncate then move: `writer.ctx.buffer.list.truncate(writer.ctx.written_len); bytes: core::mem::take(&mut writer.ctx.buffer.list).into_boxed_slice()` (or add a `BufferWriter::into_written_box()` helper). Eliminates an O(output_size) memcpy + alloc per transpiled file.

findings.title: `AnyResponse` 3-way runtime dispatch on every uWS write despite SSL/HTTP3 being const-generic
findings.problem: `RequestContext<ThisServer, SSL_ENABLED, DEBUG_MODE, HTTP3>` stores `pub resp: Option<uws::AnyResponse>`, a runtime 3-variant enum (TCP/SSL/H3). Every method call on `resp` in the hot path (`write_status`, `write_header`, `try_end`, `should_close_connection`, `timeout`, `state`, `has_responded` — 7-10 calls per plaintext response across render_metadata/render_bytes) expands via `any_dispatch!` into a 3-arm `match` + indirect call. The concrete variant is fully determined by the `SSL_ENABLED`/`HTTP3` const generics already on the struct, so all of this branching is dead weight the optimizer cannot eliminate (the discriminant is stored in memory and re-loaded per call).
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:138 `pub resp: Option<uws::AnyResponse>`. uws_sys/Response.rs:621-637 `macro_rules! any_dispatch { match $self { SSL(..)|TCP(..)|H3(..) } }`. RequestContext.rs:3805 `resp.write_status(...)`, 3726 `resp.write_header(...)`, 3841 `resp.try_end(...)`, 3461-3463 `resp.should_close_connection()` — all `AnyResponse` methods, all per-request.
findings.proposed_fix: Change `resp` field to the monomorphic type chosen by the const generics (e.g., `Option<NonNull<uws_sys::NewAppResponse<SSL_ENABLED>>>` for `!HTTP3`, `Option<NonNull<uws_sys::h3::Response>>` for `HTTP3`) via an associated type on a helper trait, or add `#[inline(always)]` unchecked accessors on `AnyResponse` that assume the variant (e.g., `resp.as_tcp_unchecked()`) and dispatch through those inside `RequestContext`.

findings.title: detect_light_background() scans environ on every Bun.markdown.ansi() call
findings.problem: md::detect_light_background() calls bun_core::getenv_z("COLORFGBG") on every invocation with no caching. It is invoked unconditionally from the JS-visible host function Bun.markdown.ansi() (src/runtime/api/MarkdownObject.rs:160) before user options are even consulted, so a script that renders many markdown snippets pays a libc getenv linear scan per call. COLORFGBG is a startup-time terminal property and does not change during the process.
findings.proposed_fix: Memoize the result in a process-global OnceLock<bool> (the value cannot change at runtime), or move COLORFGBG into bun_core::env_var so the existing cached `::get()` accessor applies. Same treatment is warranted for detect_kitty_graphics() (lines 2478-2505) which does up to 5 getenv scans, though its only current callers are one-shot CLI paths.

findings.title: Per-call closure + inline regex literals in tls checkServerIdentity pattern-matching loop
findings.problem: checkServerIdentity() calls check() once per DNS SAN / CN entry (via ArrayPrototypeSome over dnsNames). Inside check(), line 460 allocates a fresh `isBad = s => RegExpPrototypeExec.$call(/[^\u0021-\u007F]/u, s) !== null` closure on every call, and that closure is then invoked once per host segment by ArrayPrototypeSome on patternParts. Likewise splitHost()/unfqdn() (lines 433, 442) pass inline /[.]$/ and /[A-Z]/g literals through RegExpPrototypeSymbolReplace.$call, which defeats JSC's literal-at-callsite optimization (measured ~14% overhead vs a hoisted regex for the /[A-Z]/g replace). For an HTTPS client handshaking against a cert with many SAN entries this allocates dozens of RegExp wrappers + closures per TLS connection.
findings.evidence: check() is invoked from the ArrayPrototypeSome at line 570 over every DNS SAN entry; inside it splitHost(pattern) (line 449) re-evaluates two inline regexes via primordial .$call, and isBad (line 460) is a fresh closure each call that runs on every element of patternParts (line 461). Micro-benchmark of RegExpPrototypeSymbolReplace.$call(/[A-Z]/g, host, cb) shows 1140 ms hoisted vs 1326 ms inline over 500k iters (~14%). This is a per-item loop (per-SAN × per-host-segment), but sits behind TLS crypto so absolute impact is small.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist the three regexes to module scope alongside the other tls constants (e.g. `const trailingDotRe = /[.]$/; const upperCaseRe = /[A-Z]/g; const badHostCharRe = /[^\u0021-\u007F]/u;`) and move `isBad` out of check() to a module-level `const isBad = s => RegExpPrototypeExec.$call(badHostCharRe, s) !== null;`. All three are stateless / safe to share (the /g regex is only used via Symbol.replace which resets lastIndex internally).

findings.title: stmts_to_list allocates+copies every block/try/switch body where Zig wrapped in-place
findings.problem: `stmts_to_list` does `bun_alloc::vec_from_iter_in(ptr.iter().copied(), arena)` — a fresh arena allocation plus O(N) memcpy of every `Stmt` — at 6 call sites that run once per `{}` block (1598), try body (2041), catch body (2057), finally body (2072), every switch case body (2105), and every TS namespace body (2371). The Zig original used `ListManaged(Stmt).fromOwnedSlice(p.allocator, data.stmts)` which is a zero-alloc, zero-copy pointer wrap because Zig's `BabyList` stores `ptr+len+cap` and can round-trip to/from a growable list. Rust's `StoreSlice` only stores `ptr+len` (12 bytes), so capacity is lost and the port defensively copies. The in-file comment admits this: "bumpalo Vec cannot adopt the existing backing storage, so we copy." This is on the visit-pass hot path and fires for every nested statement list in the file.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) restore a `cap: u32` on `StoreSlice` so an `unsafe fn into_arena_vec(self, &'a Arena) -> ArenaVec<'a, T>` can reconstruct the growable vec (the arena is `MimallocArena`, which supports real realloc/free, so this is sound), or (b) add an `ArenaVec::from_raw_parts_in(ptr, len, cap=len, arena)` helper and call it here — growth inside `visit_stmts` will realloc (`mi_heap_realloc`) instead of copying twice. Option (a) trades +4 bytes per StoreSlice for eliminating one O(stmts) arena alloc+memcpy per block/function/case body.

findings.title: e_arrow copies the body stmts twice per arrow function (Zig copied once)
findings.problem: For every arrow function visited, line 2463 does `p.arena.alloc_slice_copy(e_.body.stmts.slice())` (matches the Zig `p.allocator.dupe(...)`), but then line 2485 does a second O(N) alloc+copy: `bun_alloc::vec_from_iter_in(dupe.iter().copied(), p.arena)`. The Zig original wrapped the first dupe with `ListManaged(Stmt).fromOwnedSlice(p.allocator, dupe)` — zero extra alloc/copy. The in-code PERF comment says "bumpalo Vec cannot adopt an existing slice, so this copies. PERF: profile if it shows up on a hot path (react-refresh only)" — but the second copy is NOT react-refresh-gated; it runs unconditionally for every arrow, which is extremely common in modern JS/TS (every callback, every `.map(x => ...)`).
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the separate `dupe` and build `stmts_list` directly from `e_.body.stmts.slice()` (one copy instead of two), or — once the `StoreSlice`→`ArenaVec` adoption helper from finding #1 exists — construct `stmts_list` by adopting `dupe` in-place. Either way eliminates one O(body_len) arena alloc+memcpy per arrow function.

findings.title: Redundant operand collection inside fixpoint loop (computed twice + extra collect)
findings.problem: Inside the `loop { for block … for instr … }` fixpoint, each instruction calls `visitors::each_instruction_value_operand(value, env)` at line 148 (heap-allocating a Vec<Place>), immediately re-collects it into a second `Vec<IdentifierId>` at line 151, and then calls `each_instruction_value_operand(value, env)` AGAIN at line 196 for the same `value`. Together with `each_instruction_lvalue` at line 182 and its `.collect()` at 185, that is 4–5 fresh heap allocations and a duplicated operand walk per instruction per fixpoint iteration.
findings.evidence: Lines 100–235: `loop { for block_id in &block_ids { … for instr_id in &block.instructions { … let operands: Vec<IdentifierId> = visitors::each_instruction_value_operand(value, env).into_iter().map(|p| p.identifier).collect(); … let operand_places = visitors::each_instruction_value_operand(value, env); … } } if !reactive_map.snapshot() { break; } }`. `each_instruction_value_operand` (visitors.rs:176) builds a fresh `Vec<Place>` every call.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the operand `Vec<Place>` once per instruction and reuse it for both the `has_reactive_input` check and the effect-based mutation loop (it carries `identifier`, `effect`, `loc` — everything both sites need). Drop the intermediate `Vec<IdentifierId>` entirely and iterate the single Vec directly. Optionally hoist a reusable `Vec<Place>` buffer outside the block/instruction loops and `clear()` it per instruction to avoid the per-iteration allocation altogether.

findings.title: Deep clone of entire inner-function instruction array per nested closure
findings.problem: `visit_inner_function_blocks` deep-clones `env.functions[func_id].instructions` (a `HirVec<Instruction>` — each `Instruction` owns an `InstructionValue` and an `Option<HirVec<AliasingEffect>>`) plus every block's `instructions` list and `terminal` (lines 1996–1998), solely to avoid a borrow conflict while later code mutates `env.scopes`/`env.identifiers`. This runs once per `FunctionExpression`/`ObjectMethod` encountered during dependency collection, so components with many inline callbacks clone their entire HIR bodies.
findings.evidence: `let inner_instrs: HirVec<Instruction> = env.functions[func_id.0 as usize].instructions.clone();` followed by `let inner_blocks: Vec<(BlockId, HirVec<InstructionId>, Vec<(BlockId, IdentifierId)>, Terminal)> = env.functions[...].body.blocks.iter().map(|(bid, blk)| { … blk.instructions.clone(), …, blk.terminal.clone() }).collect();`. The clones are only read (line 2011 `let inner_instr = &inner_instrs[iid.0 as usize];`).
findings.proposed_fix: Split the Environment borrow instead of cloning: the loop body only needs `&env.functions[...]` for reading and `&mut env.scopes` / `&mut env.identifiers` for writing (see `visit_dependency`/`visit_reassignment`). Either (a) take disjoint field borrows via a helper that returns `(&[HirFunction], &mut [ReactiveScope], &mut [Identifier])`, or (b) reborrow inside the loop (`let inner_instr = &env.functions[func_id.0 as usize].instructions[iid.0 as usize];`) after each mutation, eliminating the deep clones entirely.

findings.title: getOwnNonIndexProperties allocates a descriptor object per index for every Array/TypedArray
findings.problem: `getOwnNonIndexProperties` is called from `formatRaw` for every Array (line 1284) and every TypedArray (line 1310) encountered during recursion. Its implementation calls `ObjectGetOwnPropertyDescriptors(a)` — which materialises a fresh `{value,writable,enumerable,configurable}` object for *every* own property, including every numeric index — then `ObjectEntries(desc)` builds a second array of `[k, v]` pairs, then runs a regex + `parseInt` on every key just to discard all the indices. For `util.inspect(new Uint8Array(100_000))` or a dense 100k-element array, this allocates ~200k garbage objects and runs 100k regex tests purely to conclude there are no extra properties.
findings.proposed_fix: Expose a native helper (like Node's `internalBinding('util').getOwnNonIndexProperties`) that walks JSC's property table and skips indexed storage without allocating descriptors. Short of that, iterate `ObjectGetOwnPropertyNames(a)` (names only, no descriptor objects), compare the leading run against `value.length` (index names are spec-guaranteed to come first, ascending) and only call `ObjectGetOwnPropertyDescriptor` for the handful of survivors that need the enumerable check. Either variant turns O(length) allocations into O(extra_props).

findings.title: visit_func and append_part copy the input stmt slice into a fresh ArenaVec where Zig used fromOwnedSlice
findings.problem: `visit_func` at mod.rs:139-140 does `BumpVec::with_capacity_in(body_stmts.len(), self.arena); stmts.extend_from_slice(body_stmts)` — one arena alloc + O(body_len) memcpy per function declaration/expression. `append_part` at p.rs:5303 does the same via `vec_from_iter_in(stmts.iter().copied(), arena)` once per top-level part. The Zig originals are `ListManaged(Stmt).fromOwnedSlice(p.allocator, body.stmts)` and `ListManaged(Stmt).fromOwnedSlice(allocator, stmts)` — both zero-copy. Same root cause as finding #1 (StoreSlice lacks cap), but these two sites are independently hot: `visit_func` runs for every `function` in the source, and `append_part` runs for every top-level statement group during the visit pass.
findings.proposed_fix: Same as finding #1 — give `StoreSlice` (or the callers) a zero-copy adopt-as-ArenaVec path. Alternatively, since `visit_stmts` immediately copies its input into a separate `visited` buffer and then `clear()`s+re-extends `stmts`, `visit_stmts` could be restructured to take `&[Stmt]` input + `&mut ArenaVec` output, eliminating the need for the caller to materialize a growable vec at all.

findings.title: Linear key scan over abort_tracker where hashed get_index() is available
findings.problem: `drain_queued_shutdowns` looks up each aborted request in `abort_tracker()` (an `ArrayHashMap<u32, uws::AnySocket>`) via `tracker.keys().iter().position(|&k| k == http.async_http_id)` — a linear scan over every in-flight request's key. `ArrayHashMap` already provides hashed `get_index(&key)` (array_hash_map.rs:1034) and every other tracker lookup in this file (lines 717, 776, 822) correctly uses the hashed `get()`. With N concurrent fetches and S queued aborts this is O(S·N) instead of O(S). Hot under mass-abort workloads (e.g. AbortController cancelling many parallel requests, or page navigation with hundreds of pending fetches).
findings.evidence: HTTPThread.rs:643-647: `for http in &queued_shutdowns { let tracker = abort_tracker(); let found_idx = tracker.keys().iter().position(|&k| k == http.async_http_id); if let Some(idx) = found_idx { let (_k, socket_ptr) = tracker.swap_remove_at(idx); ... }`. Compare HTTPThread.rs:717 `abort_tracker().get(&write.async_http_id)` — same map, hashed lookup.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the linear scan with `let found_idx = tracker.get_index(&http.async_http_id);` (hashed O(1) lookup returning the dense index), then keep the existing `swap_remove_at(idx)`.

findings.title: Fresh Vec per on_data in replenish_window (borrowck-reshaped two-pass)
findings.problem: `replenish_window()` runs after every socket `on_data` delivery (line 757) and after every JS-side `resume` (line 579). It collects `(stream_id, unacked)` pairs into a freshly-constructed `Vec<(u32,u32)>` so that `self.write_window_update()` (needs `&mut self`) can be called after the `self.streams` iteration ends — a classic 'reshaped for borrowck' pattern. The comment at line 656 already concedes `// PERF: could iterate and write in one pass; profile if extra Vec matters.` Under a streaming download this path fires on most DATA-frame batches and allocates each time at least one stream crosses the window threshold.
findings.evidence: ClientSession.rs:639-656: `let mut updates: Vec<(u32, u32)> = Vec::new(); for &s in self.streams.values() { ... updates.push((s.id, s.unacked_bytes)); s.unacked_bytes = 0; } for (id, unacked) in updates { self.write_window_update(id, unacked); }` called from on_data at line 757.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep a `window_update_scratch: Vec<(u32,u32)>` on `ClientSession` (next to the existing `encode_scratch` reuse buffer at line 258) and `clear()` it at entry instead of `Vec::new()`. Alternatively, since `write_window_update` only touches `self.write_buffer` via `write_frame`, split the borrow: iterate `self.streams` while holding `&mut self.write_buffer` separately (disjoint-field borrow) and emit WINDOW_UPDATE frames inline, eliminating the Vec entirely.

findings.title: Per-chunk heap allocation of ConcurrentTask in async zlib/brotli/zstd write path
findings.problem: Every async `write()` (once per stream chunk) heap-allocates a fresh `ConcurrentTask` via `ConcurrentTask::create(Task::init(this))` to post the completion back to the JS thread. `ConcurrentTask::create` is `Box::new(...)` (see src/event_loop/ConcurrentTask.rs:304-325) with `auto_delete: true`, so the event loop frees it after dispatch. For a streaming compress/decompress of a large payload (e.g. 1 GB in 16 KB `chunkSize` = ~65 k chunks) this is ~65 k small Box alloc/free pairs that exist purely for plumbing. The threadpool-side dispatch already avoids this — `WorkPoolTask` is stored intrusively in `Native{Zlib,Brotli,Zstd}` (field `task: JsCell<WorkPoolTask>`) and reused across writes — but the JS-thread completion task is not.
findings.evidence: node_zlib_binding.rs:492-494: `unsafe { (*vm.event_loop()).enqueue_task_concurrent(ConcurrentTask::create(Task::init(this))); }`. `ConcurrentTask::create` (src/event_loop/ConcurrentTask.rs:318) calls `ConcurrentTask::new` (line 304) which is `bun_core::heap::into_raw(Box::new(init))`. Other per-chunk hot paths in Bun already store `ConcurrentTask` inline and reuse it with `.from(ptr, AutoDeinit::ManualDeinit)` instead of allocating: `src/runtime/webcore/fetch/FetchTasklet.rs:2495`, `src/runtime/webcore/s3/download_stream.rs:345`, `src/runtime/api/Archive.rs:752`, `src/jsc/WorkTask.rs:136`, `src/jsc/ConcurrentPromiseTask.rs:115`, `src/runtime/node/node_fs_watcher.rs:143`.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `concurrent_task: JsCell<ConcurrentTask>` field to `NativeZlib`/`NativeBrotli`/`NativeZstd` (all three share the same field set, stamped by `__impl_compression_stream!`) and in `async_job_run` replace `ConcurrentTask::create(Task::init(this))` with the intrusive in-place form: `this_ref.concurrent_task().with_mut(|ct| ct.from(this, AutoDeinit::ManualDeinit))`, then pass `NonNull::from(...)` to `enqueue_task_concurrent`. This matches the established pattern used by FetchTasklet/S3/Archive/WorkTask and eliminates one Box alloc + free per async chunk.

findings.title: Double heap allocation for every SSLConfig string field on each SecureContext::intern() call
findings.problem: `zbox_into_raw(z)` is implemented as `bun_core::dupe_z(z.as_bytes())`, and every string-bearing option (passphrase, server_name, ALPN protos, ciphers, and each key/cert/ca PEM string) is converted via `zbox_into_raw(&X.to_owned_slice_z())`. `to_owned_slice_z()` heap-allocates a `ZBox`, then `dupe_z` heap-allocates again, and the intermediate `ZBox` is immediately dropped. `SecureContext::intern()` (/workspace/bun/src/runtime/api/bun/SecureContext.rs:244) always calls `SSLConfig::from_js` → `from_generated` before it can compute the digest to check the WeakGCMap cache, so even on a cache hit every option string is materialised twice and freed once per `tls.connect()` / `new TLSSocket()` that doesn't pass an explicit `secureContext`. For a client passing a CA bundle (PEM certs), this duplicates KBs of data per handshake just to look up a cache key.
findings.evidence: SSLConfig.rs:65: `fn zbox_into_raw(z: &bun_core::ZBox) -> *const c_char { bun_core::dupe_z(z.as_bytes()) }`. Used at lines 160, 168, 213, 227, 296, 397 as `zbox_into_raw(&X.to_owned_slice_z())`. `handle_path` at line 284 additionally allocates a third time on the error branch: `unsafe { bun_core::free_sensitive(zbox_into_raw(&name)) }` allocates a fresh copy and immediately zero-frees it (the sensitive original `name: ZBox` is dropped without zeroing).
findings.proposed_fix: Add a helper that goes directly from `bun_core::String` (or its UTF-8 byte view) to a `dupe_z` buffer without the intermediate `ZBox`, e.g. `fn string_to_cstr(s: &bun_core::String) -> *const c_char { bun_core::dupe_z(s.to_utf8().slice()) }` using the borrowing `to_utf8()` (falls back to a single temporary only for UTF-16 impls). In `handle_path`, drop the error-path `zbox_into_raw`+`free_sensitive` round-trip — the only allocation to scrub is `name` itself.

findings.title: Unconditional heap allocations (makeUnique) on every slow-path postMessage serialization
findings.problem: Every time `SerializedScriptValue::create` takes the slow path (any nested object, any transfer list, Map/Set/Date, etc.), it unconditionally performs three heap allocations that are empty in the overwhelmingly common case: (a) `sharedBuffers = makeUnique<ArrayBufferContentsArray>()` at line 6473, (b) `makeUnique<WasmModuleArray>(wasmModules)` at line 6589, (c) `makeUnique<WasmMemoryHandleArray>(wasmMemoryHandles)` at line 6589 when context is WorkerPostMessage. (b) and (c) additionally copy-construct a Vector from an lvalue. For the 99.9% of postMessage calls that contain no SharedArrayBuffer and no WebAssembly module/memory, all three are immediately-discarded empty Vectors on the heap. The constructor (line 5796) and all readers (`if (m_wasmMemoryHandlesArray)` at 5899, `if (m_sharedBufferContentsArray)` at 5875) already tolerate nullptr.
findings.evidence: Line 6473: `std::unique_ptr<ArrayBufferContentsArray> sharedBuffers = makeUnique<ArrayBufferContentsArray>();`. Line 6589: `makeUnique<WasmModuleArray>(wasmModules), context == SerializationContext::WorkerPostMessage ? makeUnique<WasmMemoryHandleArray>(wasmMemoryHandles) : nullptr`. `ENABLE_WEBASSEMBLY` is defined to 1 (vendor/WebKit/Source/WTF/wtf/PlatformEnable.h:733). This runs on every `worker.postMessage` / `port.postMessage` that misses the fast path (fast path only covers flat strings, Int32/Double arrays, and one-level-deep plain objects — any `{type, data:{...}}` RPC shape falls through).
findings.proposed_fix: For the Wasm arrays: `wasmModules.isEmpty() ? nullptr : makeUnique<WasmModuleArray>(WTF::move(wasmModules))` and likewise for `wasmMemoryHandles`. For `sharedBuffers`: declare a stack `ArrayBufferContentsArray sharedBuffers;`, pass `sharedBuffers` by reference to `CloneSerializer::serialize`, then at the constructor call pass `sharedBuffers.isEmpty() ? nullptr : makeUnique<ArrayBufferContentsArray>(WTF::move(sharedBuffers))`. Saves 2–3 heap alloc/free pairs per slow-path postMessage.

findings.title: serialize_and_send allocates a temp StreamBuffer then memcpy's it into msg.data on every IPC send
findings.problem: Every `process.send()` / `subprocess.send()` call allocates a fresh `StreamBuffer` (`payload`), serializes the message into it (heap alloc #1), then calls `start_message()` which in the common case pushes a brand-new `SendHandle { data: StreamBuffer::default() }`, and finally does `msg.data.write(&payload.list)` — a second heap allocation plus a full memcpy of the serialized bytes. `payload` is then dropped (free #1). Net: 2 allocs + 1 full-payload memcpy + 1 free per message where 1 alloc would suffice.

findings.title: Windows IPC _write clones the entire outgoing payload into a fresh Box<[u8]> for every uv_write
findings.problem: On Windows, `_write()` builds `write_req_slice: Box<[u8]> = Box::from(&data[..write_len])`, i.e. a fresh heap allocation + memcpy of the full remaining payload, and then hands that to libuv via `uv_buf_t`. The source bytes already live in `self.queue[0].data.list`, whose heap buffer is stable for the entire duration of the write: `write_in_progress` is set, `continue_send` early-returns while it's true, and `start_message` never mutates `queue[0]` while `len==1 && write_in_progress`. libuv's `uv_write` only needs the buffer to remain valid until the write callback fires, which is exactly when `queue[0]` is popped. So the Box copy is redundant per-message work (alloc + memcpy + free of the full message) on every Windows IPC send.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop `write_slice: Box<[u8]>` from `WindowsWrite` and point `uv_buf_t` directly at `queue[0].data.list[cursor..cursor+write_len]` (raw ptr + len). The Vec<u8>'s heap buffer does not move when `Vec<SendHandle>` reallocates (only the {ptr,len,cap} triple moves), and it is neither grown nor dropped until `_on_write_complete` pops `queue[0]` — which happens strictly after the libuv write callback returns. Store only `write_len` on `WindowsWrite` for the completion path.

findings.title: Includes glob loop does not break after first match
findings.problem: In `iterate_included_project_tree`, the inner `for include in includes` loop (lines 585-610) sets `included = true` on a glob match but never breaks. The match arm only ever sets `included = true` (negated results are `unreachable!()`), so once a match is found the remaining iterations are pure waste — yet every remaining include pattern is still run through `glob::r#match`. This is executed for every file/directory entry encountered during the tree walk, so with a `"files"` array of K patterns and a tree of N entries it does Θ(N·K) glob matches instead of early-exit. The adjacent `for exclude in excludes` loop at lines 616-637 has the identical issue (only ever sets `included = false`).
findings.proposed_fix: Change the `Match` arm to `{ included = true; break; }` (and likewise `{ included = false; break; }` in the exclude loop at line 634). Semantics are unchanged because no later iteration can flip the flag back.

findings.title: Per-file heap clone of `item.path` when building pack_list
findings.problem: Inside the hot `while let Some(item) = pack_queue.remove_or_null()` archive-write loop, `pack_list.push(PackListEntry { subpath: ZBox::from_bytes(item.path.as_bytes()), .. })` allocates a brand-new ZBox copy of the path even though `item.path` is already an owned ZBox that is dropped unused at the end of the iteration. The only subsequent use of `item.path` is a borrow passed to `add_archive_entry(..., &item.path, ...)`. One redundant heap allocation + memcpy per packed file.
findings.proposed_fix: Capture `let size = stat.st_size as usize;`, call `add_archive_entry` first (it only borrows `&item.path`), then `pack_list.push(PackListEntry { subpath: item.path, size })`, moving the existing ZBox instead of re-allocating.

findings.title: SHA-512/256 integrity hashing performed while holding mutex contended by the single HTTP thread
findings.problem: take_pending() acquires `mutex` (line 446) and then calls `(*this).hasher.update(&(*this).pending)` before swapping/appending into `reading` and unlocking. The same `mutex` is taken by `on_chunk()` (line 280) on the single HTTP thread to append incoming body bytes. During `bun install` hundreds of tarballs stream concurrently through that one HTTP thread, so every drain-side SHA-512 update of a 16–64 KB chunk (~tens of µs) stalls the HTTP event loop for all downloads. The comment on line 442 already documents `hasher` as drain-side-only state; only `pending`/`closed` actually need the lock.
findings.proposed_fix: Move the hash update out of the critical section: under the lock, record `let hash_from = (*this).reading.len();` (or 0 in the swap branch), perform the swap/extend and clear `pending`, unlock, then call `(*this).hasher.update(&(*this).reading[hash_from..])`. `reading` is drain-local so no synchronization is needed for the hash. The same applies to line 391 (`hasher.update(&pending)` in the terminal-phase branch) — take `pending` out with `core::mem::take` under the lock and hash it after unlock.

findings.title: `ctx.scope_stack.clone()` per instruction in collect_dependencies
findings.problem: `handle_instruction` runs for every instruction in every block and unconditionally does `let scope_stack_copy = ctx.scope_stack.clone();` (a `Vec<ScopeId>` heap clone) to store in a `Decl`. The same clone is repeated at lines 2016, 2078, 2091, 2111, 2128, 2228. Because the scope stack only changes at `enter_scope`/`exit_scope`, thousands of consecutive instructions clone identical Vec contents.
findings.evidence: Definition at 1741 `scope_stack: Vec<ScopeId>`; usage `let scope_stack_copy = ctx.scope_stack.clone(); ctx.declare(instr.lvalue.identifier, Decl { id, scope_stack: scope_stack_copy }, env);` in the per-instruction hot path. Grep shows 8 `ctx.scope_stack.clone()` sites in this file, all inside the per-instruction/handle path.
findings.proposed_fix: Share the snapshot instead of cloning it: either wrap the current snapshot in `Rc<[ScopeId]>` and regenerate only on `enter_scope`/`exit_scope` (each `Decl` stores the Rc — clone is a refcount bump), or intern stack states to a small integer id. The later read sites (lines 1878–1887) only need membership testing + `.last()`, both of which an `Rc<[ScopeId]>` satisfies without per-instruction heap allocation.

findings.title: O(n²) phi re-lookup with dead per-phi Vec allocation
findings.problem: Part 2 builds `phi_data: Vec<_>` by mapping over `block.phis` (lines 780–805), including `phi.operands.values().map(|o| o.identifier).collect::<Vec<_>>()` per phi — a heap allocation whose result is bound as `_operand_ids` and never used. Then for each entry it fetches the block mutably and linear-scans `block.phis` again (`for phi in &mut block.phis { if phi.place.identifier == *phi_id { … break; } }`) to find the same phi it just mapped from — O(P²) per block. `first_instr_id` is also recomputed per phi though it is block-constant.
findings.evidence: Line 797–800: `phi.operands.values().map(|o| o.identifier).collect::<Vec<_>>()` stored in tuple position 1; line 807: `for (phi_id, _operand_ids, is_mutated_after_creation, first_instr_id) in &phi_data` (underscore = unused). Lines 810–823: `let block = func.body.blocks.get_mut(&block_id).unwrap(); for phi in &mut block.phis { if phi.place.identifier == *phi_id { … } }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute `first_instr_id` once before the loop, then iterate `block.phis` mutably a single time: for each phi compute `is_mutated_after_creation` inline, set `phi.place.effect`/operand effects directly, and update `env.identifiers`. This removes the `phi_data` Vec, the dead `_operand_ids` Vecs, the O(P²) inner search, and the per-phi `get_mut` hash lookup.

findings.title: Array fast-path allocates and re-iterates a second Vector when no objects found
findings.problem: The `ArrayWithContiguous` fast path (the common path for `postMessage(['a','b',...])` and other dense arrays of primitives/strings) first builds a `Vector<DenseArrayElement> elements` of N entries. If `hasObjects` ends up false — the most common case — it then allocates a second `Vector<SimpleCloneableValue> simpleElements`, loops all N elements again, and std::visits each to move it into the new vector. That is an extra O(N) heap allocation + O(N) traversal purely to down-convert `variant<JSValue,String,SimpleCloneableObject>` to `variant<JSValue,String>`, even though the data is already fully materialized.
findings.evidence: Lines 6228-6300 build `elements` once per element. Lines 6306-6318: `WTF::Vector<SimpleCloneableValue> simpleElements; simpleElements.reserveInitialCapacity(length); for (auto& elem : elements) { std::visit(...); }` — second allocation and N-element loop. The `DenseArray` deserialize path at lines 6813-6903 already handles the no-object case identically to `SimpleArray` (same MarkedArgumentBuffer + initializeIndex pattern), so dispatching to `createDenseArrayFastPath` unconditionally would change nothing observable on deserialize.
findings.proposed_fix: Drop the `hasObjects` special case and always `return createDenseArrayFastPath(FixedVector<DenseArrayElement>(WTF::move(elements)))` when `ok`. If keeping the narrower SimpleArray storage type is desired (it is smaller per element), instead do a cheap first scan of the butterfly for any object cell before allocating, and build the correctly-typed vector exactly once.

findings.title: DenseArray serialize: property-name isolatedCopy() allocated per-object instead of deduped
findings.problem: In the arrays-of-simple-objects fast path, `entry.key()->isolatedCopy()` is called for every property of every object (lines 6279 and 6282). `isolatedCopy()` heap-allocates a fresh StringImpl and memcpys the characters. For the dominant payload shape this path was built for — an array of N records with the same K-property Structure — that is N*K string allocations where the keys are identical across all N objects. The slow-path CloneSerializer dedupes property names via `m_constantPool` (line 2318) and the DenseArray *deserialize* side has a Structure/identifier cache (line 6824), but serialize has no equivalent, so it does O(N*K) allocations where O(K) would suffice.
findings.evidence: `objStructure->forEachProperty(vm, [&](const PropertyTableEntry& entry) { ... properties.append({ entry.key()->isolatedCopy(), ... }); })` runs inside `for (unsigned i = 0; i < length; i++)` over array elements (line 6234). No cache keyed on `entry.key()` or on `objStructure` exists in this scope.
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a small `HashMap<UniquedStringImpl*, String>` (or `HashMap<Structure*, FixedVector<String>>`) outside the element loop and reuse the isolated-copied key String across objects. Since same-Structure objects share the exact `UniquedStringImpl*` atom pointer for each key, a pointer-keyed map gives O(1) lookup and reduces allocations from N*K to K.

findings.title: isBelowBreakLength allocates a colour-stripped copy of every output element
findings.problem: `isBelowBreakLength` is called from `reduceToSingleString` for every container (object, array, Set, Map, …) in the recursion. When `ctx.colors` is true (the default for a TTY), it loops over every formatted property string and does `StringPrototypeReplaceAll(output[i], /\u001B\[\d\d?m/g, "").length` — i.e. it allocates a brand-new colour-stripped string per element solely to read its `.length`. For an object with 1000 properties this is 1000 throwaway string allocations per level, multiplied across the whole tree.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute visible length without allocating: subtract the summed length of the ANSI matches (walk the regex with `exec` and add `match[0].length`), or call the already-imported native `internalGetStringWidth` / `Bun.stripANSI` only when the early-exit hasn't already triggered. Since the loop already breaks as soon as `totalLength > breakLength`, a count-only scan avoids one allocation per rendered property.
