findings.title: Dead-part branch deep-clones declared_symbols and symbol_uses just to satisfy borrowck
findings.problem: When a top-level part visits to zero stmts, the Rust port builds a temporary `js_ast::Part` by deep-cloning two heap collections: `self.declared_symbols.clone()?` (DeclaredSymbolList — a Vec-backed MultiArrayList) and `self.symbol_uses.clone()?` (an ArrayHashMap). The only consumer, `clear_symbol_usages_from_dead_part`, merely iterates them read-only (p.rs:1582-1597). Both are `clear_retaining_capacity()`'d immediately after, so the clones are pure waste. The Zig original built the temporary `Part` struct with the existing handles (`p.declared_symbols`, `p.symbol_uses`) — no deep copy. This path is hit whenever a top-level statement is dead-code-eliminated (e.g. type-only exports in TS, dead `if (false)` blocks), so it can fire many times per file.
findings.proposed_fix: Refactor `clear_symbol_usages_from_dead_part` to take `(&DeclaredSymbolList, &SymbolUseMap)` directly (it never reads any other `Part` field) and split-borrow `self.symbols` against `self.declared_symbols`/`self.symbol_uses`; or `core::mem::take` both fields into locals, pass `&` to them, then drop/clear — since they're cleared right after anyway, `take` costs nothing extra.

findings.title: Redundant alloc+copy when combining paused body buffer with new chunk
findings.problem: `get_bytes()` handles the 'data arrived while paused, now a new chunk came in' case by taking ownership of the paused `Vec<u8>` (line 1534-1536), then allocating a brand-new `combined: Vec<u8>` and copying both `paused` and `chunk` into it, then passing `&combined` to `ArrayBuffer::create_buffer` (which copies again into JS memory). The already-owned `paused` Vec is dropped unused. This is one extra allocation + one extra memcpy of the entire paused body (potentially large uploads).
findings.evidence: NodeHTTPResponse.rs:1534-1549: `let paused = self.buffered_request_body_data_during_pause.replace(Vec::new()); let paused_len = paused.len(); let mut combined: Vec<u8> = Vec::with_capacity(paused_len + chunk.len()); combined.extend_from_slice(&paused); combined.extend_from_slice(chunk); drop(paused); break 'brk match jsc::ArrayBuffer::create_buffer(global_this, &combined) { ... }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with `let mut paused = self.buffered_request_body_data_during_pause.replace(Vec::new()); paused.extend_from_slice(chunk); break 'brk match jsc::ArrayBuffer::create_buffer(global_this, &paused) { ... }` — reuses the existing allocation and copies only `chunk`.

findings.title: newNativeSecureContext spreads the options object up to three separate times per TLS connection
findings.problem: `newNativeSecureContext` runs on every `new InternalSecureContext(options)` (i.e. every `tls.connect()`/`new TLSSocket()` that doesn't reuse a `secureContext`). It rebuilds `options` with `{ ...options, ... }` up to three times in sequence: once if `ALPNProtocols` is an array (line 619), once if any of key/cert/ca is falsy (lines 624-629 — true for almost every client connection), and once unconditionally for the minVersion/maxVersion translation (line 649). Each spread re-enumerates and copies every own-enumerable property of the user options object. The `if (options)` guards at 621 and 632 are also dead (options is already normalised to an object at line 607).
findings.evidence: tls.ts:619: `options = { ...options, ALPNProtocols: normalized.ALPNProtocols };`; 624-629: `options = { ...options, key: key || null, cert: cert || null, ca: ca || null };`; 649: `options = { ...options, minVersion, maxVersion };` (unconditional). `InternalSecureContext`'s constructor (line 702) calls this per-socket via `this[ksecureContext] = options.secureContext || new InternalSecureContext(options)` in `TLSSocket` (line 824).
findings.proposed_fix: Build the normalised object once: spread `options` a single time into a local (e.g. `const o = { ...options }`) and then mutate `o.ALPNProtocols`, `o.key/cert/ca`, `o.minVersion/maxVersion` in place before passing to `NativeSecureContext.intern/createPrivate`. Remove the redundant `if (options)` guards.

findings.title: Redundant HashSet dedup in transferArrayBuffers (caller already deduplicated)
findings.problem: `transferArrayBuffers` builds a `HashSet<JSC::ArrayBuffer*> visited` and does a `contains()` + `add()` (two hash lookups) for every ArrayBuffer in the transfer list. But its sole caller (line 6532, verified by grep) already rejects duplicate transferables at lines 6388–6401 via `uniqueTransferables.add(transferable.get()).isNewEntry`, so `arrayBuffers` can never contain duplicates when it reaches this function. The entire HashSet is dead weight: one HashSet allocation + 2N hash ops per postMessage-with-ArrayBuffer-transfer.
findings.evidence: Lines 5982–5986: `HashSet<JSC::ArrayBuffer*> visited; for (...) { if (visited.contains(arrayBuffers[i].get())) continue; visited.add(arrayBuffers[i].get()); ... }`. Sole caller at line 6532 passes `arrayBuffers` that was populated at lines 6403–6411 inside a loop guarded by `if (!uniqueTransferables.add(transferable.get()).isNewEntry) return Exception{DataCloneError}` at line 6393.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the `visited` HashSet and the `contains`/`add` lines; the loop body reduces to the `transferTo` call. (If defensiveness is desired, keep an `ASSERT` that sizes match.)

findings.title: Per-message JSArray allocation in node:worker_threads postMessage wrappers (rest args + $apply)
findings.problem: Both `Worker.prototype.postMessage` (lines 1182–1184) and the fake `parentPort.postMessage` (lines 777–781) are written as `function(...args) { return target.$apply(thisArg, args); }`. The rest parameter materialises a fresh JSArray on every call, and `$apply` then iterates it — purely to forward at most two arguments. These are the per-message hot paths for node:worker_threads users (every `worker.postMessage(msg)` on the parent and every `parentPort.postMessage(msg)` in the worker goes through them). The underlying native `jsFunctionPostMessage` / `jsWorkerPrototypeFunction_postMessage` read only `callFrame->argument(0)` and `argument(1)`, so fixed-arity forwarding is behaviourally identical.
findings.evidence: Line 1182–1184: `postMessage(...args: [any, any]) { return this.#worker.postMessage.$apply(this.#worker, args); }`. Line 777–781: `Object.defineProperty(fake, "postMessage", { value(...args: [any, any]) { return postMessage.$apply(null, args); } });`. Native side (Worker.cpp jsFunctionPostMessage line 915–916) reads `callFrame->argument(0)` / `argument(1)` and ignores `this`.
findings.proposed_fix: Rewrite as fixed-arity forwarding: `postMessage(message, options) { return this.#worker.postMessage(message, options); }` and for parentPort `value(message, options) { return postMessage.$call(null, message, options); }` (or simply `value: postMessage` since the native fn ignores `this`). Eliminates one JSArray allocation + GC pressure per message.

findings.title: Directory subpath is allocated twice per visited directory
findings.problem: Every tree-walk site that pushes a subdirectory does `dirs.push(DirInfo(subdir, entry_subpath_.as_bytes().into(), dir_depth + 1))`. `entry_subpath_` is a freshly-allocated `ZBox` (built by `entry_subpath()` at line 878) and `.as_bytes().into()` allocates a second `Box<[u8]>` copy; the original `ZBox` is then dropped. This double-allocation happens at lines 654, 674, 845, 996/1026, 1294 and 1421 — i.e. once per directory traversed during `bun pm pack`.
findings.proposed_fix: Change `DirInfo.1` to hold a `ZBox` instead of `Box<[u8]>` and move `entry_subpath_` into it directly (`dirs.push(DirInfo(subdir, entry_subpath_, dir_depth + 1))`). `ZBox` derefs to `&[u8]` so existing `&dir_subpath` borrows keep working, and `entry_subpath()` already returns `ZBox`.

findings.title: O(N·M) linear scan of bundled_deps for every node_modules entry
findings.problem: `iterate_bundled_deps` iterates every top-level `node_modules/*` (and `@scope/*`) entry and for each does `ctx.bundled_deps.iter().position(|dep| strings::eql_long(entry_name, &dep.name, true))` (lines 976 and 1006). With N top-level node_modules entries and M bundled deps this is Θ(N·M) case-insensitive string compares. When `bundledDependencies: true` (M = all `dependencies`), a large project can hit hundreds × dozens of compares where a single pre-built lowercase-name hash set would make it Θ(N + M).
findings.proposed_fix: Before the `while let Some(entry) = iter.next()` loop, build a `StringHashMap<usize>` keyed by the ASCII-lowercased dep name → index into `ctx.bundled_deps`. Replace the `.iter().position()` scans with a single hash lookup on the lowercased entry name.

findings.title: Bun.password.hash: encoded hash is allocated then copied twice through a 4KB stack buffer
findings.problem: `PasswordObject::hash` zero-inits `let mut outbuf = [0u8; 4096]` on the stack, passes it to `pwhash::{argon2,bcrypt}::str_hash`, which internally already obtains a heap-owned `String` from the vendor crate (`vendor::hash_encoded` / `parts.format_for_version`) and `copy_from_slice`s it into `outbuf`. `PasswordObject::hash` then immediately does `Box::<[u8]>::from(out_bytes)`, allocating and copying a second time. Net per-call waste: one 4KB stack memset + one ~100-byte memcpy + one ~100-byte heap alloc+memcpy, all avoidable by having `str_hash` return the vendor's `String` bytes directly (`encoded.into_bytes().into_boxed_slice()`). The `&mut outbuf[SHA512::DIGEST..]` offset in the bcrypt branch is vestigial — the digest now lives in its own separate `digest` buffer, so the slice offset serves no purpose.
findings.evidence: PasswordObject.rs:300-317: `let mut outbuf = [0u8; 4096]; ... let out_bytes = pwhash::argon2::str_hash(password, hash_options, &mut outbuf)?; Ok(Box::<[u8]>::from(out_bytes))`  —  pwhash.rs:163-171: `let encoded = vendor::hash_encoded(password, &salt, &config)...; let bytes = encoded.as_bytes(); ... out[..bytes.len()].copy_from_slice(bytes); Ok(&out[..bytes.len()])`  —  pwhash.rs:340-345 (bcrypt): `let encoded = parts.format_for_version(vendor::Version::TwoB); let bytes = encoded.as_bytes(); ... out[..bytes.len()].copy_from_slice(bytes);`
findings.proposed_fix: Change `pwhash::argon2::str_hash` and `pwhash::bcrypt::str_hash` to return `Result<Box<[u8]>, Error>` directly via `Ok(encoded.into_bytes().into_boxed_slice())`, drop the `out: &'a mut [u8]` parameter, and remove `outbuf` (and the dead `outbuf_slice = &mut outbuf[SHA512::DIGEST..]` offset) from `PasswordObject::hash`. This eliminates one 4KB zeroing, one copy, and one allocation per `Bun.password.hash()` call.

findings.title: Recursive directory walk (open + readdir) executed while holding the process-wide PathWatcherManager mutex
findings.problem: Inside the Linux inotify reader thread, `manager.mutex` is locked at line 895 for the whole event batch. When a new directory appears under a `{recursive:true}` watch, lines 1096–1114 call `Linux::add_one` + `walk_subtree::<false>(...)`, which does `sys::open()` and iterates `sys::dir_iterator` recursively (getdents syscalls) for the entire new subtree — all while still holding `manager.mutex`. Every other thread that calls `fs.watch()`/`PathWatcher::detach()` (JS thread, Worker threads — lines 358, 465, 539) blocks for the full duration of the subtree crawl. A moderately sized directory move (e.g. thousands of entries) turns into a multi-millisecond global stall of the watcher subsystem.
findings.proposed_fix: Collect the (child_abs, rel_owned, owner_watcher) triples that need a subtree crawl into a small Vec while processing the batch, `manager.mutex.unlock()`, perform the `walk_subtree` readdir passes lock-free into a results buffer, then re-lock to call `Linux::add_one` / `watcher.emit` for each discovered entry. `add_one` itself needs the lock (it mutates `wd_map`), but the expensive open/readdir recursion does not — it only reads the filesystem, not manager state.

findings.title: Per-batch ArrayHashMap allocation inside inotify/kqueue hot loop (under manager.mutex)
findings.problem: `let mut touched: ArrayHashMap<*mut PathWatcher, ()> = ArrayHashMap::default();` is created fresh every iteration of the `while running.load(...)` reader loop — once per inotify read() batch — and dropped at the end of the loop body. The first `get_or_put` allocates the keys/values/hashes Vecs; those are then freed at scope end. The identical pattern exists in the FreeBSD kqueue path at line 1504. For a busy dev-server/HMR scenario generating many FS-event batches per second, this is a needless alloc/free pair per batch, performed while `manager.mutex` is held.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist `let mut touched = ArrayHashMap::default();` above the `while running` loop and call `touched.clear_retaining_capacity()` after the `flush()` loop. Capacity is bounded by the number of distinct PathWatchers (tiny). Apply the same change at line 1504 (kqueue backend).

findings.title: Per-dependency linear dedup over `scope.declarations` (quadratic growth)
findings.problem: `visit_dependency` is invoked for every operand of every instruction (via `handle_instruction` → `visit_operand`). For each, it iterates `orig_scope_stack` and for every scope does `scope.declarations.iter().any(|(_, d)| env.identifiers[d.identifier…].declaration_id == decl_id)` — a linear scan over a `HirVec<(IdentifierId, ReactiveScopeDeclaration)>` (hir/mod.rs:1533) that grows as the pass pushes to it at line 1892–1894. This is O(operands × scope_depth × declarations), i.e. quadratic in the number of declarations produced for a scope. `visit_reassignment` at line 1930 has the same pattern over `scope.reassignments`.
findings.evidence: `let already_declared = scope.declarations.iter().any(|(_, d)| { env.identifiers[d.identifier.0 as usize].declaration_id == decl_id });` with `pub declarations: HirVec<(IdentifierId, ReactiveScopeDeclaration)>`. The surrounding call chain is `collect_dependencies` → per-block → per-instruction → `handle_instruction` → `ctx.visit_operand` → `visit_dependency` (also line 1880 does `self.scope_stack.contains(&scope_id)` — a Vec linear scan — in the same nest).
findings.proposed_fix: Maintain a side `FxHashSet<(ScopeId, DeclarationId)>` (or per-scope `FxHashSet<DeclarationId>`) in `DependencyCollectionContext` that mirrors insertions at line 1892/1947; dedup becomes O(1). Likewise keep a `FxHashSet<ScopeId>` alongside `scope_stack` for the `contains` check at line 1880. No change to the stored `HirVec` output shape is required.

findings.title: startObjectInternal() hashes every object twice (find then add)
findings.problem: `startObjectInternal()` is invoked once per object in the serialization graph (arrays, plain objects, maps, sets, Date, RegExp, Error, ArrayBuffer, CryptoKey, etc. — 14 call sites). It calls `checkForDuplicate()` which does `m_objectPool.find(object)`, and when the object is new (the common case) falls through to `recordObject()` which does `m_objectPool.add(object, m_objectPool.size())`. That is two full hash-table lookups per new object where one suffices. `HashMap::add()` already returns `AddResult::isNewEntry` plus an iterator to the existing entry on collision.
findings.evidence: Lines 1130-1158: `ObjectPool::const_iterator found = m_objectPool.find(object); if (found != m_objectPool.end()) { ... return true; } return false;` then `m_objectPool.add(object, m_objectPool.size());`. `writeObjectIndex`'s byte-width decision reads `m_objectPool.size()`, which is unchanged when `add` hits a duplicate, so merging is semantically identical.
findings.proposed_fix: Collapse `startObjectInternal` to a single `auto result = m_objectPool.add(object, m_objectPool.size()); if (!result.isNewEntry) { write(ObjectReferenceTag); writeObjectIndex(result.iterator->value); return false; } m_gcBuffer.appendWithCrashOnOverflow(object); return true;`. Leave the two split-call sites (JSArrayBufferView at 1891 and X509 at 2085) alone since they interleave pool-mutating work between check and record.

findings.title: DenseArray deserialize recomputes Identifier::fromString for cached identifiers
findings.problem: When the structure cache misses (first object in a DenseArray), the code loops properties once computing `JSC::Identifier id = JSC::Identifier::fromString(vm, prop.propertyName)` and calling `putDirect` (line 6865). Then, to seed `cachedIdentifiers`, it loops the same properties again and calls `JSC::Identifier::fromString(vm, prop.propertyName)` a second time (line 6878). Each call is an AtomStringTable hash lookup. The `id` values from the first loop are discarded instead of being appended directly.
findings.evidence: Lines 6863-6871 compute `id` per property; lines 6874-6879: `if (!cachedStructure) { ... for (const auto& prop : obj.properties) cachedIdentifiers.append(JSC::Identifier::fromString(vm, prop.propertyName)); }` — same atom lookup repeated.
findings.proposed_fix: In the cache-miss branch, `reserveInitialCapacity` `cachedIdentifiers` before the property loop when `!cachedStructure`, and append `id` inside the existing loop right after computing it. Eliminates the second loop and K redundant atom-table lookups for the first object.

findings.title: print_jsx computes Tag::get(children) twice for JSX children
findings.problem: `print_jsx` computes `let tag = Tag::get(children, self.global_this)?` at line 5345 to decide how to print children, then inside the `Tag::JSX` arm calls `Tag::get(children, self.global_this)?` again at line 5388 to pass to `self.format`. `Tag::get` is not cheap: for any object it performs `value.fast_get(.., InspectCustom)` and `value.get_own_truthy(.., "$$typeof")` plus `symbol_for` comparisons. For a JSX tree where every element's child is itself JSX (the common `` case), this doubles the per-node tag work.
findings.proposed_fix: Reuse the `tag` computed at line 5345: `self.format::<C>(tag, writer_, children, self.global_this)?;`. `tag` is a `TagResult` (Copy-sized) already in scope and unchanged between the two sites.

findings.title: Redundant JSC__JSValue__asArrayBuffer FFI calls per async write() chunk
findings.problem: `CompressionStream::write()` resolves each of the `in`/`out` JS buffers twice per call: first via `as_array_buffer()` (lines 355, 382) solely to read `byte_len` for bounds validation, then again via `as_pinned_arraybuffer()` (lines 414, 420) — which internally pins and then calls `as_array_buffer()` a second time — to obtain the slice actually handed to the compressor. That is 4 `JSC__JSValue__asArrayBuffer` FFI round-trips per chunk where 2 suffice. `write_sync()` (lines 619-682) already does this with a single lookup per buffer, so the async path is strictly doing extra work. This runs once per stream chunk.
findings.evidence: Lines 355/382: `arguments[1].as_array_buffer(global_this)` / `arguments[4].as_array_buffer(global_this)` used only for `in_buf.byte_len` / `out_buf.byte_len`. Lines 414/420: `arguments[1].as_pinned_arraybuffer(global_this)` / `arguments[4].as_pinned_arraybuffer(global_this)`. `as_pinned_arraybuffer` (src/jsc/JSValue.rs:961-966) is `JSC__JSValue__pinArrayBuffer` followed by `self.as_array_buffer(global)`.
findings.proposed_fix: Pin first, then bounds-check on the pinned `ArrayBuffer` (whose `byte_len` is identical; only the data pointer may change when a FastTypedArray materializes). Call `throw_unless_idle` before pinning so no state is mutated on failure, and on a bounds/out-buf error unpin whatever was pinned (the code already has this cleanup pattern at lines 421-423 for the OOM case). Net: 2 fewer C++ FFI calls per async chunk. Low absolute win relative to the deflate/inflate work itself, so only worth folding in alongside a larger change.

findings.title: PackageManifest::parse does ~22 O(n) linear property scans per version instead of one pass
findings.problem: Both the count pass (loop at :2079) and the build pass (loop at :2342) call `version_obj.get(b"…")` for each key they need — dist, bin, directories, bundleDependencies, bundledDependencies, cpu, os, libc, hasInstallScript, dependencies/optionalDependencies/peerDependencies, peerDependenciesMeta — ≈9 calls in the count pass + ≈13 in the build pass. `ObjectJSON::get()` (src/ast/e.rs:1166) is `properties().iter().find(|p| p.key == key)`, i.e. a linear scan over the ~15 keys an abbreviated version object carries, and most of these queried keys (cpu/os/libc/directories/bundledDependencies) are usually *absent*, so each such scan walks all 15 entries.
findings.evidence: src/ast/e.rs:1166 `pub fn get(&self, key:&[u8])->Option<&JsonValue>{ self.properties().iter().find(|p| p.key.slice()==key)... }`. npm.rs count-pass lookups: :2112 dist, :2123 bin, :2152 directories, :2165+2166 bundle(d)Dependencies, :2186 pair.prop×3, :2209 peerDependenciesMeta. build-pass lookups: :2371/2373, :2393 cpu, :2397 os, :2401 libc, :2406 hasInstallScript, :2414 bin, :2552 directories, :2572 dist, :2622 pair.prop×3, :2628 peerDependenciesMeta, :2942 time.
findings.proposed_fix: In each of the two per-version loops, iterate `version_obj.properties()` once and dispatch via `match p.key.slice()` (byte-slice match, same key set is statically known) into local `Option<&JsonValue>` slots, then run the existing logic against those slots. Do the same for the `dist` sub-object (tarball/fileCount/unpackedSize/integrity/shasum — currently 5 separate `get()`s). No API changes; all work stays inside `parse()`.
findings.workload_impact: ~22 scans × ~12 avg compares × ~100 versions/manifest × ~1000 unique manifests ≈ 26M short byte-slice compares reduced to 2 passes × 15 props × 100 × 1000 ≈ 3M (with one compare each). Order-of-magnitude: removes ~100-200ms of worker-thread CPU on a 1500-dep cold install; scales linearly with total published-versions-across-deps.

findings.title: NetworkTask::for_manifest does a C++ WHATWG URL join + two separate URL::parse() on the same bytes per manifest
findings.problem: For every manifest enqueue (main-thread, on the latency-critical HTTP fan-out path): (1) `bun_url::join()` at :448 calls the WebKit `URL__getHrefJoin` FFI, which allocates a WTFStringImpl and runs the full WHATWG parser just to concatenate the known-good registry href with a package name; (2) `tmp.to_owned_slice().into_boxed_slice()` at :502 copies the result; (3) `URL::parse(&url_bytes)` at :505 parses it with the Rust-side parser for a same-origin check; (4) `URL::parse(...)` at :630 parses the *identical* bytes again for `AsyncHTTP::init`. That is 1 C++ WHATWG parse + 2 Rust URL parses + 1 WTFString alloc/deref + 1 Box alloc per unique package name.
findings.evidence: NetworkTask.rs:448 `bun_url::join(&String::borrow_utf8(scope.url.href()), &String::borrow_utf8(encoded_name))` → src/url/lib.rs:97 `URL__getHrefJoin` (C++ FFI). NetworkTask.rs:505 `let joined = URL::parse(&url_bytes);` used only for the origin check, then dropped. NetworkTask.rs:630 `let url = URL::parse(unsafe { detach_lifetime(&self.url_buf) });` — same bytes reparsed.
findings.proposed_fix: Reuse the `joined` `URL` from the origin check at :505 as the `url` passed to `AsyncHTTP::init` (move its construction after `self.url_buf = url_bytes` and detach its lifetime the same way the second parse does), eliminating the duplicate `URL::parse` at :630. Additionally, for the overwhelmingly common case where `scope.url.href()` equals the default registry and `name` contains no characters needing WHATWG normalization, build `url_buf` by direct concatenation (registry href trimmed of trailing '/' + '/' + encoded_name) and skip the C++ `bun_url::join` round-trip; fall back to `bun_url::join` only when the registry is non-default.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~1 WTFStringImpl alloc/deref + ~1 redundant bun_url::URL::parse per unique manifest × ~1000 manifests ≈ ~1000 C++ allocations and ~1000 URL parses on the single main thread during request fan-out — ~2-10ms of main-thread time plus allocator pressure in the phase where faster enqueue directly widens HTTP concurrency.

findings.title: verify() does a full recursive JSON parse (~10 heap allocs) per package just to read name/version
findings.problem: `verify_package_json_name_and_version` (hit once per hoisted dependency on the warm path) calls `PackageJSONVersionChecker::parse()` → `parse_to_rows()` (src/parsers/json.rs:690), which builds a `StructuralIndex` (heap `win` Vec ≈8KB + `dirty` Vec), a heap `Box<JsonTape>`, and grows `scratch_props`/`tape.props`/`tape.items` Vecs while recursively parsing *every* nested object in the package.json (`dependencies`, `devDependencies`, `scripts`, `keywords`, …). After all that, the caller only reads the two top-level string values and breaks.
findings.evidence: src/parsers/json_stage2.rs:100 `Box::leak(Box::new(E::JsonTape::empty()))`; src/parsers/json_index.rs:68-69 `idx.win = Vec::with_capacity(win_cap); idx.dirty = vec![0; dirty_words];`; src/parsers/json_stage2.rs:813 `parse_json_value()` recurses into every nested object/array; src/parsers/json.rs:694-712 iterates top-level rows and stops once name+version found. All invoked from PackageInstall.rs:977-980 with a fresh `Log` and fresh parser per package.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the `parse_to_rows` call in `PackageJSONVersionChecker::parse` (or add a dedicated fast path for `verify_package_json_name_and_version`) with a zero-alloc depth-0 byte scanner: walk bytes tracking brace/bracket depth and string state, capture the first `"name":"…"` and `"version":"…"` values at depth 1, and bail as soon as both are found. `name`/`version` are the first two keys in >99% of published packages, so this typically touches <150 bytes and never allocates. The existing `skip_ws_and_comments`/`skip_string_token` helpers in json.rs already provide the primitives.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ≈10 heap allocations × N hoisted deps (≈1500 in a typical Next.js-size project) = ~15,000 allocs per warm install, and skips full structural-index + stage-2 parse of ~3-6 MB of JSON. This is the single largest CPU cost of warm install after the unavoidable openat/read/close syscalls.

findings.title: filter()→hoist_dependency does an O(D×H) linear scan over the root tree's dep list on every warm install
findings.problem: `install_hoisted_packages` unconditionally calls `lockfile.filter()` (src/install/hoisted_install.rs:100) to rebuild the hoisted tree. For each of D dependency edges, `hoist_dependency` recurses up to the root and linearly scans the current tree's placed-dependency list (`for i in 0..this_deps_len { if dep.name_hash != target_name_hash { continue } … }`). The root tree accumulates H≈unique-package-count entries, so most edges pay an O(H) scan, yielding O(D·H) total comparisons — each of which also does a random-access load into `deps[dep_id]` (cache-unfriendly).
findings.evidence: Tree.rs:1004-1013 linear loop comparing `dep.name_hash != target_name_hash`; Tree.rs:1108-1125 recurses to parent and re-scans; hoisted_install.rs:100 `(*lockfile_ptr).filter(...)` runs unconditionally before the install loop; Tree.rs:937 `builder.list.items_dependencies_mut()[dest.id].push(dep_id)` grows the very list that subsequent edges scan.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a `name_hash → index` side-table (e.g. `bun_collections::HashMap<u64,u32>`) to `BuilderEntry` (or at minimum keep one for tree id 0, which dominates). `hoist_dependency` then becomes a single hash lookup per ancestor instead of a linear scan; placement in `process_subtree` inserts into the map alongside the existing `push(dep_id)`.
findings.workload_impact: Turns O(D·H) into O(D). For a 1500-package project (~15K dep edges, root ~1200 entries) this removes ~15-20 million u64 comparisons + indirect loads from every warm install — roughly 10-30 ms of pure CPU before the link loop even starts.

findings.title: join_string_buf_t zero-fills a 4KB stack temp on every path join in the per-package verify path
findings.problem: `NodeModulesFolder::open_file_without_opening_directories` (src/install/PackageInstaller.rs:169) calls `join_z_buf` → `join_string_buf_t`, which begins with `let mut temp_buf_: [T; 4096] = [T::from_u8(0); 4096];` — a 4 KB memset — even though the function immediately computes `count` (the exact concatenated length, typically 60-120 bytes for `<cwd>/node_modules/<pkg>/package.json`) and writes only `count` bytes before reading. Combined with `PackageJSONVersionChecker::init` (src/parsers/json.rs:663-664) zeroing two more 1 KB buffers, that's ~6 KB of dead memset per package.
findings.evidence: resolve_path.rs:1609 `[T::from_u8(0); 4096]`; resolve_path.rs:1613-1619 computes `count` before any temp-buf read; json.rs:663-664 `found_version_buf: [0; 1024], found_name_buf: [0; 1024]` with only `len.min(1024)` bytes ever copied in (json.rs:699-706).
findings.proposed_fix: In `join_string_buf_t`, move the `count` computation above the temp-buf declaration and either (a) use `MaybeUninit::<[T; 4096]>::uninit()` (mirroring `PathBuffer::uninit()` / `depth_buf_uninit()` in Tree.rs:83) since every byte is written before read, or (b) zero only `temp_buf_[..count+1]`. In `PackageJSONVersionChecker`, leave `found_*_buf` uninitialised and rely on `found_*_len` for the valid range.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~6 KB memset × N packages ≈ 9 MB of memset per 1500-package warm install. Small next to findings 1-2, but the `join_string_buf_t` fix also benefits every other path join in Bun (resolver, bundler, fs APIs).

findings.title: NumberRenamer allocates & drops a fresh hashbrown HashMap for every AST scope
findings.problem: In `assign_names_recursive_with_number_scope`, each scope with symbols calls `number_scope_pool.get_init(NumberScope { name_counts: NameCountMap::with_capacity_and_hasher(symbol_count, ..) })` (line 697-717), and the walk-back at line 753 calls `put(s)` which `drop_in_place`s the `name_counts` HashMap (hive_array.rs:433). The HiveArrayFallback pool recycles the 2-field struct slot but NOT the HashMap's heap buffer, so every non-empty scope does one `hashbrown` table alloc + one free. `rename_symbols_in_chunk` is invoked per chunk and iterates every file in `files_in_order`, recursing into every scope — and it is serialized (one worker per chunk; typical non-code-split builds have 1 JS chunk).
findings.evidence: renamer.rs:697-718 constructs `NameCountMap::with_capacity_and_hasher(symbol_count, ..)` inside `get_init`; renamer.rs:743-755 `number_scope_pool.put(s)` per intermediate scope; hive_array.rs:419-437 `put` does `drop_in_place(value)` which frees `name_counts`; renameSymbolsInChunk.rs:403-413 resets `hive.used` per part but never reuses map capacity. This is the default (non-`--minify-identifiers`) path, taken by a plain `bun build`.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep a small `Vec<NameCountMap>` stack on `NumberRenamer` (or change `HiveArrayFallback::put` callers to instead `name_counts.clear()` and leave the slot claimed): on pop, `clear()` the map (retains buckets) and push it to the free-list; on push, pop a map, `clear()` + `reserve(symbol_count)`. This turns ~N alloc/free pairs into O(max scope depth) allocations total.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~1 HashMap alloc + 1 free per non-empty scope. A 1000-module React app has ~30–50 scopes/module ⇒ ~30,000–50,000 hashbrown table alloc/free pairs eliminated on the serial per-chunk rename thread (the renameSymbolsInChunk step is a known TODO-parallelize hotspot per the file's own comment at line 16).

findings.title: StringJoiner watcher memmem-scans the entire bundle output for unique_key, but `estimated_count` is never read
findings.problem: `push_node` runs `strings::index_of(data_slice, self.watcher.input)` (SIMD memmem) over every pushed slice to increment `watcher.estimated_count` whenever `watcher.input` is non-empty. `post_process_js_chunk` (postProcessJSChunk.rs:456) sets `watcher.input = chunk.unique_key` (25 bytes), so every `j.push(compile_result.code())` for each of the ~1000+ part_ranges scans that slice. But the only reads of `estimated_count` in the whole repo are StringJoiner's own unit tests (StringJoiner.rs:284,304). `break_output_into_pieces` (LinkerContext.rs:4079) then calls `j.contains(&self.unique_key_prefix)`, which re-scans every node's bytes a second time for effectively the same information.
findings.evidence: `rg estimated_count` returns only the StringJoiner definition/increment/tests — no bundler code reads it. postProcessJSChunk.rs:456-459 sets `watcher.input = chunk.unique_key`; LinkerContext.rs:4079 independently scans via `j.contains()`. postProcessCSSChunk.rs:20 and postProcessHTMLChunk.rs:18 do the same.
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) set `watcher.input` to `c.unique_key_prefix` instead of `chunk.unique_key` and replace `j.contains(&self.unique_key_prefix)` in `break_output_into_pieces` with `j.watcher.estimated_count > 0` (and use the count to size `pieces`), eliminating the second full scan; or (b) simply stop setting `watcher.input` in post_process_* so the `input.len() > 0` gate short-circuits the dead scan.
findings.workload_impact: Eliminates one full memmem pass over the concatenated JS output per chunk. For a 1000-module app producing ~5–10 MB of printed JS, that is ~5–10 MB of redundant SIMD scanning removed from the single-threaded post-process step (and the same for CSS/HTML chunks).

findings.title: compute_reserved_names_for_scope heap-boxes every unbound global's name, once per module, via owning StringHashMap::put
findings.problem: `rename_symbols_in_chunk` loops `for &source_index in files_in_order { compute_reserved_names_for_scope(...) }` (renameSymbolsInChunk.rs:129-136). Inside, for every `Unbound`/`must_not_be_renamed` symbol in each module scope it calls `names.put(symbol.original_name.slice(), 1)` (renamer.rs:1170-1172, 1180-1182). `StringHashMap::put` unconditionally allocates a `Box<[u8]>` key copy via `owned_key` (array_hash_map.rs:1883-1886), even when the key is already present (hashbrown's `insert` then drops the old boxed key). Every module references roughly the same ~15-30 unbound globals (`console`, `window`, `Array`, `Promise`, `Object`, `Math`, …), so after the first module almost every call is a duplicate insert that allocates-then-drops a Box.
findings.evidence: array_hash_map.rs:1883 `pub fn put(&mut self, key: &[u8], ..) { self.inner.insert(owned_key::<A>(key), value); }`; `owned_key` (array_hash_map.rs:1820) builds a `StringHashMapKey::owned(Box<[u8],A>)`. `symbol.original_name` is already documented in this file as 'an AST-arena slice that outlives the renamer' (renamer.rs:800, 1169) and the map itself is consumed inside `NumberRenamer::init` before the renamer returns, so no owned copy is needed. The zero-copy `put_borrowed`/`put_static_key` path already exists (array_hash_map.rs:1895, 2078).
findings.proposed_fix: Use the existing borrowed-key API: change lines 1170-1172 / 1180-1182 to the `unsafe put_borrowed`/`put_static_key` path (the AST-arena lifetime guarantee is already relied on throughout this file). Optionally gate on `!names.contains_key(name)` to skip the reinsert entirely.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~1 Box<[u8]> alloc+free per unbound global per module on the serial per-chunk rename thread: ~1000 modules × ~15-30 unbound globals ≈ 15,000–30,000 heap alloc/free pairs eliminated (each ~8-16 B), plus the redundant hash+insert on duplicates.

findings.title: AbortSignal is heap-allocated for every request even when request.signal is never read
findings.problem: prepare_js_request_context_for() unconditionally does `let signal = AbortSignal::new(global); ctx.set_signal(signal); signal.pending_activity_ref(); AbortSignalRef::adopt(signal.ref_())` and stores it on both the RequestContext and the Request. WebCore__AbortSignal__new() does `new AbortSignal(context)` — a TZone heap allocation of a large object (RefCounted + EventTargetWithInlineData + 3 Vectors incl. inline-2 callback vector + 2 WeakListHashSets + Lock + atomic, ~200-300B) and registers it in ScriptExecutionContext::m_destructionObservers (UncheckedKeyHashSet insert). finalize_without_deinit() then does pending_activity_unref()+unref() (2 FFI), and the Request finalizer later does the last unref() which triggers HashSet.remove + delete. A hello-world handler never touches request.signal, so the entire lifecycle is dead weight. Request::get_signal() at Request.rs:782-794 already has lazy-create logic for when self.signal is None.
findings.evidence: server_body.rs:3061-3069 (eager create + pending_activity_ref + ref_); RequestContext.rs:1561-1570 (signal_release on every finalize); bindings.cpp:5534-5540 (`adoptRef(*new AbortSignal(context))`); AbortSignal.h:63,194-211 (object layout: Vectors/WeakListHashSets/Lock); ContextDestructionObserver.cpp:51-63 + ScriptExecutionContext.cpp:156-170 (HashSet add/remove on every AbortSignal ctor/dtor); Request.rs:782-794 (lazy getter already exists).
findings.proposed_fix: Stop creating the signal eagerly in prepare_js_request_context_for. Initialize `ctx.signal = None` and pass `signal: None` to Request::init. When JS reads `request.signal`, Request::get_signal()'s existing lazy branch creates it and should additionally hand the pointer back to the RequestContext via AnyRequestContext (so on_abort/finalize can still fire it). finalize_without_deinit/on_abort already guard on `self.signal.take()` being Some, so they no-op when it was never materialized.
findings.workload_impact: Per request: removes 1 C++ heap alloc/free (~200-300B), 1 UncheckedKeyHashSet insert + 1 remove, and 5 FFI calls (new, pending_activity_ref, ref_, pending_activity_unref, unref). At ~200k req/s hello-world, that is ~200k C++ allocations/sec and ~1M FFI round-trips/sec eliminated from the hot path.

findings.title: drain_microtasks() (and to_blob_if_possible) run twice per synchronous response
findings.problem: For a handler that returns a Response synchronously, on_response() calls ctx.drain_microtasks() (line 2690) and body_value.to_blob_if_possible() (line 2736), then calls render()→do_render()→do_render_with_body() which immediately calls this.drain_microtasks() (line 3124) and value.to_blob_if_possible() (line 3128) again. No user JS executes between the two drains on this path. drain_microtasks() (is_async()==false on the sync path, so it does not early-return) performs jsc_vm.release_weak_refs() FFI + JSC__JSGlobalObject__drainMicrotasks() FFI + deferred_tasks.run() + uws_loop.drain_quic_if_necessary(). The duplicate exists only to serve the do_render_with_body_locked callback entry point.
findings.evidence: RequestContext.rs:2690 (drain #1) and 2736 (to_blob #1) in on_response; RequestContext.rs:3124 (drain #2) and 3128 (to_blob #2) in do_render_with_body; RequestContext.rs:627-640 (drain_microtasks gated on !is_async, i.e. it DOES run on sync path); event_loop.rs:311-352 (each drain = release_weak_refs FFI + drainMicrotasks FFI + deferred_tasks.run + drain_quic_if_necessary).
findings.proposed_fix: Remove the drain_microtasks()/to_blob_if_possible() prologue from do_render_with_body() and move it into do_render_with_body_locked (the only caller that has not already drained). The on_response/handle_resolve → render → do_render path then drains exactly once.
findings.workload_impact: Per synchronous request: removes 1 redundant drain = ~2-3 FFI calls (release_weak_refs, JSC__JSGlobalObject__drainMicrotasks, drain_quic_if_necessary) plus a redundant to_blob_if_possible() branch. At ~200k req/s, ~400-600k FFI round-trips/sec eliminated.

findings.title: Per-callback memcpy of body bytes into scheduled_response_buffer (then source is discarded)
findings.problem: On every HTTP-thread progress callback, `scheduled_response_buffer.write(response_buffer.list.as_slice())` copies the entire chunk, then `response_buffer.reset()` clears the source two lines later. `scheduled_response_buffer` is also never pre-reserved to Content-Length, so Vec doubling adds another ~1× payload of realloc-memcpy. All of this runs under `task_ref.mutex`.
findings.evidence: Lines 2428-2441: `handle_oom(task_ref.scheduled_response_buffer.write(task_ref.response_buffer.list.as_slice())); ... task_ref.response_buffer.reset();`. `MutableString::write` is `Vec::extend_from_slice` (src/bun_core/string/MutableString.rs:107). No `reserve` of `body_size`/Content-Length anywhere in `callback`. uSockets recv buffer is 512KB (packages/bun-usockets/src/libusockets.h:95 LIBUS_RECV_BUFFER_LENGTH=524288) so 100MB ≈ 200 callbacks.
findings.proposed_fix: When `scheduled_response_buffer.list.is_empty()` do `core::mem::swap(&mut task_ref.scheduled_response_buffer.list, &mut task_ref.response_buffer.list)` instead of write+reset; only fall back to `extend_from_slice` when coalescing onto non-empty bytes. Additionally `scheduled_response_buffer.list.reserve(content_length)` once from `result.body_size` so the coalescing path doesn't realloc. response_buffer re-grows to one chunk (≤512KB) on the next recv, which is cheap.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ≈100MB direct memcpy + ≈100MB Vec-growth realloc-memcpy per 100MB fetched (≈200 callbacks × 512KB, all under the tasklet mutex on the HTTP thread). Cuts body-byte copy amplification from 3× payload to ~1× for `await res.arrayBuffer()`.

findings.title: is_streaming tests body_receive_mode.is_some() instead of the mode value, forcing per-chunk callbacks in BufferAll
findings.problem: `let is_streaming = self.signals.get(Field::ResponseBodyStreaming) || self.signals.body_receive_mode.is_some();` — fetch() always wires body_receive_mode via `Store::to_with_backpressure()` (Signals.rs:120-124, FetchTasklet.rs:1920), so `.is_some()` is always true and every recv chunk triggers progress_update → FetchTasklet::callback even after the consumer called `.arrayBuffer()`/`.text()` and set `BodyReceiveMode::BufferAll` (i.e. 'just buffer, I don't need chunks').
findings.evidence: lib.rs:4770-4772 gates per-chunk progress on `.is_some()`. Signals.rs:103 defaults the atomic to AutoPause; FetchTasklet.rs:1680 sets BufferAll on `.arrayBuffer()`. No code path reads the enum value here. Same pattern in the chunked path at lib.rs:4859-4860.
findings.proposed_fix: Load the atomic and treat only AutoPause/Paused as streaming: `let is_streaming = signals.get(Field::ResponseBodyStreaming) || matches!(signals.body_receive_mode_value(), Some(BodyReceiveMode::AutoPause | BodyReceiveMode::Paused));`. When BufferAll/Ignore, accumulate in body_out_str and fire a single progress_update at `is_done` (plus pre-reserve body_out_str to `content_length.min(PREALLOCATE_MAX)` at line 4752 so the accumulation doesn't realloc).
findings.workload_impact: For `await res.arrayBuffer()` on 100MB: eliminates ≈200 progress_update/FetchTasklet::callback round-trips per download (≈200 mutex lock+unlock, ≈200 sync_progress_from, ≈200 has_schedule_callback CAS) and, combined with the swap above, collapses the HTTP-thread handoff to one move of a 100MB Vec instead of 200×512KB appends.

findings.title: on_body_received hands scheduled_response_buffer to ByteStream as Temporary then resets it — forces an extra full copy in the streaming path
findings.problem: For `res.body` streaming, `on_body_received` does `let chunk = self.scheduled_response_buffer.list.as_slice(); bytes.on_data(Self::temporary_chunk(chunk, ..))` and then the scopeguard (line 650-655) resets scheduled_response_buffer. `StreamResult::Temporary` tells ByteStream it must copy (ByteStream.rs:370-374, 387-389 `extend_from_slice`), so every streamed byte is memcpy'd a third time even though FetchTasklet owns the buffer and is about to throw it away.
findings.evidence: FetchTasklet.rs:714/722/740/748 all pass `temporary_chunk(chunk, ..)`; the `defer` at 650-655 unconditionally `(*scheduled_buf).reset()` on return. ByteStream.rs append() for Temporary variants does `Vec::with_capacity + extend_from_slice` (line 370-374) or `b.extend_from_slice` (387-389); for Owned it does `move_to_list_managed()` (365-367) — zero-copy.
findings.proposed_fix: Pass ownership: `let owned = core::mem::take(&mut self.scheduled_response_buffer.list); bytes.on_data(StreamResult::Owned(owned.into()) / OwnedAndDone(..))?;` and set `buffer_reset = false`. ByteStream::append's Owned arm adopts the Vec without copying.
findings.workload_impact: For `for await (chunk of res.body)` / Readable.fromWeb on 100MB: removes one full-payload memcpy (≈100MB) on the JS thread plus one heap alloc per delivered chunk (~200 Vec allocations). Brings streaming copy amplification from 4× payload to 3× (and to 2× when combined with finding #1).

findings.title: Expect::call() heap-boxes a RefData per expect() whose refcount is never shared
findings.problem: Every `expect()` invocation runs `bun_test::BunTest::ref_(&buntest_strong, state)` (bun_test.rs:734-743), which does `IntrusiveRc::new(RefData{...})` → `Box::new(RefData)` (ptr/ref_count.rs:842-845). The resulting `RefDataPtr` is stored as `Expect::parent` and consumed only by `increment_expect_call_counter()` (reads `buntest_weak`+`phase`) and `get_snapshot_name()` (same), then `finalize()` calls `parent.deref()` → `RefData::destroy` → `heap::take` (Box free). The intrusive refcount stays at 1 for its entire lifetime in the expect() path — it is never bumped (sharing is only needed for done-callback / promise-then paths in `run_test_callback`, bun_test.rs:1206-1246, which create their own RefData). So every assertion pays 1 Box alloc + 1 Box free for a struct that could be stored inline in the already-boxed `Expect` payload.
findings.evidence: expect.rs:638-654 builds `parent: Some(BunTest::ref_(...))`; bun_test.rs:738 `bun_ptr::IntrusiveRc::new(RefData { buntest_weak: Rc::downgrade(this_strong), phase, ref_count })`; ref_count.rs:844 `Box::new(init_data)`. The only reads are expect.rs:195-212 (`increment_expect_call_counter`) and expect.rs:549-606 (`get_snapshot_name`), neither of which clone/ref the pointer. expect.rs:616-618 is the paired free.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `Expect::parent: Option<RefDataPtr>` with inline fields `Option<(BunTestPtrWeak, RefDataValue)>` (or `Option<InlineRefData>`), since `Expect` is already `Box`ed by `to_js()` (generated.rs:948). `increment_expect_call_counter`/`get_snapshot_name` read the two fields directly; `finalize` drops the `Weak`. No behavior change — the IntrusiveRc count is provably 1 throughout.
findings.workload_impact: Removes 1 Box::new(~56 B) + 1 Box::drop per `expect()` × 10k assertions = ~10,000 mimalloc alloc/free pairs. Also shaves the `RefCount::init()` store and the indirection through `*mut RefData` on every counter increment.

findings.title: Per-assertion Rc clone→downgrade→upgrade round-trip for a BunTest that is guaranteed live
findings.problem: Each assertion performs ~6 `Rc`/`Weak` refcount ops on the same `BunTestCell` that `BunTestRoot::active_file` already keeps strongly alive for the test's entire duration: `Expect::call` does `clone_active_strong()` (Rc clone, bun_test.rs:55-58/568-570), then `Rc::downgrade` inside `ref_()` (bun_test.rs:739), then drops the cloned strong at end of `call`. Later `increment_expect_call_counter()` calls `parent.bun_test()` → `Weak::upgrade()` (bun_test.rs:1517-1519) and drops the upgraded Rc, and `finalize` drops the Weak. All of this exists solely to check 'is the test file still running', which is always true while the matcher executes synchronously inside that file.
findings.evidence: expect.rs:638 `clone_active_strong()`; bun_test.rs:739 `Rc::downgrade(this_strong)`; expect.rs:196 `parent.bun_test()` → bun_test.rs:1518 `self.buntest_weak.upgrade()`; the strong owner is `BunTestRoot::active_file` (bun_test.rs:568-570) which is set for the entire file run.
findings.proposed_fix: If Finding #2 is taken, store a strong `Rc<BunTestCell>` (not `Weak`) directly in `Expect` — it is dropped in `finalize` before the file's `active_file` is cleared, so no cycle. Then `increment_expect_call_counter` becomes a direct `.get()` with no upgrade. Net: 6 refcount ops → 2 (one clone in `call`, one drop in `finalize`). Alternatively store a raw `NonNull<BunTestCell>` validated once in `call` and eliminate all refcount ops.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~4 non-atomic Rc/Weak refcount ops per assertion × 10k = ~40,000 refcount writes + the `Weak::upgrade` strong==0 branch check. Small per-op (~1-2 ns) but sits on the inner loop of every matcher and touches shared cachelines; ~5-10% of the non-FFI per-assertion native overhead.

findings.title: bunfig.toml probed 2-3× per startup when absent
findings.problem: `load_bunfig` only sets `ctx.debug.loaded_bunfig = true` AFTER `bun_ast::to_source(config_path)` succeeds. When bunfig.toml doesn't exist (the common case), the ENOENT is swallowed by `auto_loaded` and `loaded_bunfig` stays false. Downstream, `exec_with_cfg` (run_command.rs:2394) and `boot()` (run_command.rs:930) both gate on `!ctx.debug.loaded_bunfig` and each re-issue `load_config_path("bunfig.toml")`, which calls `to_source` → another failed `open()`. For `bun script.ts` (AutoCommand) the probe happens 3×: once via `arguments::parse` → `load_config` (cwd/bunfig.toml), once in `exec_with_cfg`, once in `boot`.
findings.evidence: Code path: arguments.rs:44-58 (to_source err → `return Ok(())` before line 79), run_command.rs:2394-2403 (`if !ctx.debug.loaded_bunfig { load_config_path(..., zstr!("bunfig.toml"), ctx) }`), run_command.rs:930-937 (identical guard inside `boot`). `read_global_config()` is false for Auto/RunCommand (command_tag.rs:89-104), so these calls go straight to `load_bunfig` → `to_source` → `bun_sys::open`.
findings.proposed_fix: Move `ctx.debug.loaded_bunfig = true;` to the top of `load_bunfig` (before `to_source`), or rename the flag to `attempted_bunfig_load`. The two later call sites only exist to ensure bunfig is considered once; whether the file exists does not change that contract. Alternatively, drop the `exec_with_cfg`/`boot` re-probes entirely for the Auto/RunCommand path since `arguments::parse` already covered it.
findings.workload_impact: Removes 2 failed `openat()` syscalls per `bun script.ts` cold start (1 per `bun run script.ts`). On Linux each ENOENT open is ~1-3 µs of kernel round-trip; on a ~10-20 ms cold start that is measurable in the syscall count and avoids 2 dcache lookups.

findings.title: BundleOptions setup boxes ~120 static-literal strings per startup
findings.problem: `BundleOptions::from_api` (run once per VM init) materialises the default extension-order / main-fields / out-extensions tables as `Box<[Box<[u8]>]>` via `owned_string_list`, even though every element is a `&'static [u8]` literal like `b".tsx"`. Worse, it builds them twice: `main_fields` is allocated for `Target::Browser` at line 1790 then dropped and rebuilt for the actual target at line 1892 (always hit — `boot()` sets `target = Some(Bun)`); `extension_order` is built by `ResolveFileExtensions::default()` (4 lists × 9 strings = 40 allocs) then two lists are overwritten with the Bun 10-string versions at 1935-1936 (+22 allocs, -20 frees). `out_extensions()` at 1964 builds a 9-entry `StringHashMap` the runtime never reads (only `bundler/linker.rs:715` reads it). Then `resolver_bundle_options_subset` (transpiler.rs:1073-1161) deep-clones all of it again for the resolver's nominally-distinct options struct: 4 extension lists (~42 allocs), `main_field_extension_order` (8), `main_fields` (4), CSS (2), plus 3 `ConditionsMap` clones.
findings.evidence: options.rs:1084 `owned_string_list(s) { s.iter().map(|b| Box::<[u8]>::from(*b)).collect() }`; options.rs:1043-1054 `ResolveFileExtensions::default()` calls it 4× on 9-element consts (EXTENSION_ORDER/MODULE_EXTENSION_ORDER at options.rs:2029-2049); options.rs:1790+1892 double-build of `main_fields`; options.rs:1935-1936 override of two extension lists for `Target::Bun`; options.rs:319-343 `out_extensions()` inserts 9 entries, sole reader is linker.rs:715; transpiler.rs:1089-1141 clones every list back into `resolver::options::BundleOptions`.
findings.proposed_fix: (a) Compute `target` first and build `main_fields`/`extension_order` once for the resolved target (eliminates the Browser-then-overwrite waste). (b) Change the field types from `Box<[Box<[u8]>]>` to an enum `ListOrStatic { Static(&'static [&'static [u8]]), Owned(Box<[Box<[u8]>]>) }` (or `Cow`) so the defaults borrow rodata — only user-provided `--extension-order`/`--main-fields` need the owned variant. (c) Make `out_extensions` lazy (`OnceCell`) or skip it when `!write`. (d) Have `resolver_bundle_options_subset` share/borrow the bundler's lists (both live on the process-lifetime `vm.transpiler`) instead of deep-cloning.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~120-140 mimalloc alloc/free pairs of 3-10-byte strings plus ~6 outer Box/HashMap allocs from every `bun run` cold start — the largest single cluster of heap traffic in `VirtualMachine::init` outside JSC itself.

findings.title: Entry script file is opened, closed, then re-opened to read
findings.problem: `maybe_open_with_bun_js` is the fast path for `bun ./script.ts`. It does `open(target, O_RDONLY)` → `fstat(fd)` (reject directories) → `get_fd_path(fd)` (readlink `/proc/self/fd/N` on Linux, `F_GETPATH` on macOS) → `close(fd)`, then passes only the canonical path string to `_boot_and_handle_error` → `boot`. The module loader later reaches `Transpiler::parse` → `read_file_with_allocator` (resolver/lib.rs:2497-2538) with `_file_handle: None` (no watcher on cold start; and `is_main` forces `fd = None` at jsc_hooks.rs:2262 anyway), so it `open()`s the same path again to read the bytes, then `close()`s. Net: 2 open + 2 close + 1 fstat + 1 readlink for one file.
findings.evidence: run_command.rs:2858-2901: `bun_sys::open(open_z, O::RDONLY, 0)` … `bun_sys::fstat(fd)` … `bun_sys::get_fd_path(fd, &mut script_name_buf)` … `let _ = bun_sys::close(fd); Self::_boot_and_handle_error(ctx, &absolute_script_path, None)`. resolver/lib.rs:2512-2538: when `_file_handle.is_none()` → `bun_sys::open_file(path, READ_ONLY)`. jsc_hooks.rs:2231-2264: `fd` comes only from the ImportWatcher (null on cold start) and is forced to `None` for `is_main`.
findings.proposed_fix: Keep the fd from `maybe_open_with_bun_js` alive: stash it (e.g. in a new `ctx.runtime_options.entry_fd: Option<Fd>` or a one-shot `VirtualMachine::main_fd`) instead of `close(fd)`, and thread it to the first transpile via `ParseOptions.file_descriptor` so `read_file_with_allocator` takes the `_file_handle: Some(fd)` branch (`lseek(0)` + read, no open). The existing `should_close_input_file_fd` machinery in `transpile_source_code_inner` already handles ownership/closing when `fd.is_none()` flipped to Some.
findings.workload_impact: Saves 1 `openat()` + 1 `close()` syscall per cold start (the entry file is opened once instead of twice). On cold page-cache this also avoids a second path-walk/dentry lookup for the same inode.

findings.title: Printer re-validates every `.name` with a CodepointIterator walk
findings.problem: `print_expr`'s `EDot` arm calls `lexer::is_identifier(&e.name)` for every property access printed. `is_identifier` (src/bun_core/string/identifier.rs:28) drives a full `CodepointIterator` with per-byte cursor updates (`i`, `width`, `c` stores, two bounds checks, ASCII cmp, ID-part range check ≈ 10 ops/byte). But `E::Dot::name` is filled from `p.lexer.identifier` in `parse_suffix.rs:102` — it was already accepted as `TIdentifier`/keyword, so the walk re-proves a parse-time invariant on the single hottest expression variant after `EIdentifier`.
findings.evidence: src/js_printer/lib.rs:3687 `if lexer::is_identifier(&e.name)`; src/bun_core/string/identifier.rs:28-43 loops `CodepointIterator::next` per byte (no ASCII-bulk fast path); src/js_parser/parse/parse_suffix.rs:96-116 sets `name = E::Str::new(p.lexer.identifier)` only after `is_identifier_or_keyword()`. Same shape repeats at lib.rs:4912 for EString object keys.
findings.proposed_fix: Add an all-ASCII fast path to `is_identifier` that reuses the lexer's existing `latin1_identifier_continue_length_scalar` (src/js_parser/lexer.rs:3999): `text[0] matches [a-zA-Z_$] && latin1_identifier_continue_length_scalar(&text[1..]) == text.len()-1` → return true; fall through to CodepointIterator only when a byte ≥ 0x80 is seen. Alternatively carry a 1-bit `name_is_identifier` on `E::Dot` set at parse time so the printer check is a single load.
findings.workload_impact: Removes ~10 ops/byte × ~8 bytes × ~100K EDot ≈ ~8M ops from the three.js print pass (plus the same per string-keyed property at :4912); ~2-3% of end-to-end transpile wall time on dot-heavy inputs.

findings.title: Per-EDot wyhash into `defines.dots` even when the map is empty
findings.problem: `e_dot` visitor calls `defines.dots.get(e_.name.slice())` on every `.property` expression. `Define::dots` is a `StringHashMap` (hashbrown + `bun_wyhash::BuildHasher`, src/js_parser/lib.rs:505); hashbrown's `get` computes `make_hash(k)` before touching the table, so an empty or 1-entry `dots` still pays a full wyhash of the property name per EDot. In `Bun.Transpiler` / `--no-bundle` with no `--define`, `dots` is empty, making 100% of these hashes dead work.
findings.evidence: visit_expr.rs:1363 `defines.dots.get(e_.name.slice())` with no `is_empty()` guard; lib.rs:504-505 `dots: StringHashMap<Vec<DotDefine>>`; array_hash_map.rs:1576 shows StringHashMap wraps `hashbrown::HashMap<..., bun_wyhash::BuildHasher, _>` (hash computed pre-probe).
findings.proposed_fix: Gate with `if !defines.dots.is_empty() { ... }` (mirrors the `size==0` early-out already present in `zig_hash_map::get_index` at src/collections/zig_hash_map.rs:312). For the non-empty-but-tiny case, precompute the set of terminal-segment lengths once and reject out-of-range names before hashing, same trick `comptime_string_map!` uses.
findings.workload_impact: Saves ~1 wyhash(≈8 B) per property access: ~100K EDot in three.js × ~15 cycles ≈ ~1.5M cycles removed from the visit pass when no dot-defines are configured (the common transpile-only case).
